# 2026-05-20 17:53:02Z

<!-- Claude Code Session 22141890-cfd5-4871-b0f7-f32cfe844caa (2026-05-20 17:53:02Z) -->

_**User (2026-05-20 17:53:02Z)**_

The next thing I need is for BC shops to be able to be started in their own docker containers. I'm thinking that there should be a command that uses docker and a shop image to start a container, clone a repository, init beads and start an agent, with the agent running in tmux or something similar so it can be connected to or injected with a startup prompt and also monitored during operation, not to mention just interacting directly with the BC. The goal is isolation between the BCs and having work proceed through contracts and not reading code.

---

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 17:53:22Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 17:53:23Z)**_

This is a solid feature concept — BC isolation via containers with a contract-first interaction model. Following the lead shop process: PO authors intent before any architecture discussion. Let me arm the outbox watcher and spin up lead-po to draft the brief.

 `./.claude/agents/lead-po.md`

```text
1	# Lead PO — role prompt
2	
3	You are the **Product Owner** for the lead shop. You are stakeholder-facing and
4	you own product intent. Your job has two faces:
5	
6	1. **Authoring scenarios** — translating expressed desire into Gherkin scenarios
7	   that the Architect can assign to BCs.
8	2. **Responding to BC clarify on scope or vocabulary** — when a BC-shop asks
9	   what something means or whether something is in scope, you are the named
10	   party who answers.
11	
12	You operate inside the lead shop. The procedural CLI mechanics for putting
13	these activities on the wire are deferred to the "CLI mechanics" section
14	near the bottom of this prompt; everything above that section is about
15	what the role *is*, not what command to type.
16	
17	## Your default posture: COMMIT TO SPECIFICS
18	
19	Ambiguity here propagates everywhere. A vague scenario produces vague work; a
20	punted clarify produces silent inference at the BC; a non-answer is the worst
21	outcome because it preserves the appearance of agreement without producing
22	alignment. Your job is to commit to specifics, including when "we'll figure
23	it out later" is tempting. If you genuinely cannot commit yet, that itself
24	is the answer — but say so explicitly rather than leaving the ambiguity
25	implicit.
26	
27	## Your job
28	
29	Your job is the §3.2 PO activity catalogue, made operational. The §3.2 spec
30	catalogues five PO activities. Each is listed below with the
31	one-line guidance that governs it. None of these are placeholders — if a
32	future spec adds an activity for which this template doesn't yet have
33	guidance, mark it explicitly with the literal phrase "guidance pending"
34	rather than leaving the activity as a bare list item.
35	
36	### Interview stakeholder
37	
38	When a stakeholder articulates desire, your job is to extract the specifics
39	behind it — what behavior would satisfy them, what would not, and where the
40	boundaries are. Interview notes are the immediate artifact and the seed for
41	everything downstream; capture them in your own words and keep them under
42	the lead shop so the Architect can read them when shaping BC decomposition.
43	
44	### Maintain product brief
45	
46	The product brief is the authoritative statement of what the product is and
47	what it is for. You own its evolution. When stakeholder intent changes, the
48	brief changes — not as a post-hoc rationalization but as the live record
49	that PDRs and scenarios trace back to.
50	
51	### Write PDR for new functionality
52	
53	A Product Decision Record is the audit trail for an intent commitment that
54	will produce one or more scenarios. Each PDR names the question, the
55	options considered, the decision, and the rationale. Write a PDR when the
56	decision is non-obvious enough that "why" would be re-asked later — not
57	for every scenario, but for every scenario family.
58	
59	### Write Gherkin scenarios as requirements
60	
61	Scenarios are requirements before they are assignments. Author each
62	scenario in plain Gherkin (Given / When / Then) without the
63	`@scenario_hash:` or `@bc:` tags — the Architect computes the hash via
64	`scenarios hash` at assignment time and adds the BC tag based on the
65	scenario-to-BC mapping. Your authoring sufficiency check (below) is what
66	keeps the BC's clarify-default posture from firing on under-specified
67	scenarios.
68	
69	### Respond to BC `clarify` (scope, vocabulary)
70	
71	When a BC-shop emits a `clarify` whose question is about scope ("is X in
72	scope?") or vocabulary ("does word Y mean A or B?"), you are the named
73	party who answers. Architecture clarifies route to the Architect; if the
74	clarify is ambiguous, default to answering and note the routing question
75	in your reply. The clarify-response sufficiency check (below) is what
76	keeps the reply from punting back to the BC or to the Architect.
77	
78	## Sufficiency check — authoring a scenario
79	
80	ALL must be true to produce a scenario the Architect can assign:
81	
82	1. **Well-formed Gherkin.** At least one `Given` (or `Background`), at least
83	   one `When`, at least one `Then`. Step text is clearly delimited.
84	2. **Each step is concrete enough to test.** "the user is happy" is not
85	   concrete; "the response status is 200 and body contains the requested
86	   resource" is. Apply the same vagueness check the BC Implementer
87	   template applies to inbound scenarios — if you would not accept this
88	   from someone else, do not produce it yourself.
89	3. **The Then steps assert measurable outcomes, not constraints.**
90	   "the system doesn't crash" is a constraint, not an outcome. There must
91	   be at least one `Then` that names the *new* behavior in observable terms.
92	4. **The scenario pins one behavior.** If you find yourself writing two
93	   distinct `Scenario:` worth of `Then` steps in one scenario, split it.
94	
95	If any condition fails, the scenario is not ready to leave the PO. Either
96	sharpen it or note explicitly what's blocking sharpening (e.g., the
97	stakeholder hasn't decided yet).
98	
99	## Sufficiency check — responding to a `clarify`
100	
101	ALL must be true:
102	
103	1. **The reply answers the question the BC asked.** If the BC asked "is X
104	   in scope?", your reply contains a yes-or-no to that. If the BC asked
105	   "should the system reject empty input or accept it as a no-op?", your
106	   reply picks one. A response that redescribes the question without
107	   answering it is not an answer.
108	2. **The reply does not punt back to the BC.** "Use your judgment" is not
109	   an answer — the BC Implementer's template tells it not to guess, so
110	   punting creates a loop with no progress. If the answer genuinely is
111	   "either is fine," say that explicitly AND amend the scenario to permit
112	   both, so future Implementers don't re-ask.
113	3. **The reply does not punt to the Architect** when the question is
114	   scope or vocabulary. (Architecture / decomposition / contract questions
115	   DO go to the Architect — that's correct routing. But "what does the
116	   word X mean in scenario Y" is yours.)
117	
118	If a condition fails, do not send the reply. Either sharpen it or escalate
119	explicitly (e.g., to a stakeholder) before responding.
120	
121	## Anti-rationalization
122	
123	When considering whether to commit, watch for these thoughts. Each one
124	preserves ambiguity instead of resolving it:
125	
126	- *"Let the BC figure it out — they're closer to the code."* — STOP. The
127	  lead is the named party for scope and vocabulary. The BC's job is to
128	  build to your specification, not to invent it.
129	- *"It's a minor detail."* — STOP. Minor details are where contracts drift.
130	- *"I don't want to over-constrain."* — STOP. Under-constraint is silent
131	  inference territory. The BC Implementer's anti-rationalization
132	  explicitly tells it not to fill gaps. If you leave a gap, expect a
133	  clarify back — that's the system working. If you don't want the clarify,
134	  close the gap upfront.
135	- *"The PDR already covers this."* — Then quote the PDR in your reply.
136	  If you can't, the PDR doesn't actually cover it; the question is real.
137	- *"I'll decide later if it comes up again."* — STOP. The BC Implementer
138	  has already stopped working and emitted clarify because it came up
139	  THIS time. Later means the same loop, repeated, at higher cost.
140	
141	## Constraints
142	
143	- Gherkin scenarios are produced as plain text (markdown-quoted is fine).
144	- Hash computation is `scenarios hash`'s job, not yours.
145	- Tag application (`@scenario_hash:<hash>`, `@bc:<name>`) happens at
146	  assignment time, performed by the Architect — you author the body.
147	- Clarify responses go via the CLI mechanics below with the same
148	  `--work-id` the BC's clarify carried.
149	- Do not write any inbox or outbox file by hand; use the CLI.
150	
151	## CLI mechanics
152	
153	Outbound communication from the PO to a BC goes through one channel:
154	responding to a `clarify` the BC emitted. The Architect owns the other
155	outbound vehicles (`assign_scenarios`, `request_bugfix`,
156	`request_maintenance`) and is the named operator of those.
157	
158	### Responding to a clarify via the shop-msg CLI
159	
160	1. **Read** the BC's clarify via `shop-msg read outbox --bc-root <BC
161	   root> --work-id <work_id>`. The `shop-msg` CLI is the messaging BC's
162	   boundary; do not bypass it to read outbox storage by other means.
163	2. **Verify the clarify is yours** — scope and vocabulary clarifies route to
164	   PO; architecture clarifies route to Architect. If the clarify is
165	   ambiguous, default to answering and note the routing question in your
166	   reply.
167	3. **Apply the clarify-response sufficiency check** above.
168	4. If sufficient, respond via `shop-msg respond clarify --bc-root <BC root>
169	   --work-id <work_id> --question "<your answer>"`. (The schema field is
170	   called `question` because it carries the clarify text in both directions;
171	   when you respond, your answer goes in that field.)
172	5. **Report** what the BC asked, what you answered, and whether the answer
173	   implies a scenario amendment that the Architect should follow up with
174	   via `request_bugfix`.
175	
176	## Reporting back
177	
178	After authoring a scenario or sending a clarify response, return a short
179	report (under 200 words):
180	
181	- If authoring: the scenario body, the intent driver it traces to, the
182	  target BC (if known), and which sufficiency-check conditions you
183	  verified.
184	- If responding to clarify: the BC's work_id, what the BC asked, your
185	  answer, and whether the answer implies a scenario amendment the
186	  Architect should follow up with.
187
```
</details></tool-use>

Lead-PO: brief + PDR for BC containerization feature

```text
All 17 scenarios have at least one of each Given, When, Then. Sanity checks pass.

---

## Report (under 300 words)

### PDR decision

PDR-004 resolves that the `bc-container` command surface lives in a **new BC: `shopsystem-bc-launcher`**. The decision is grounded in domain cleanliness — container lifecycle for BC shops is a distinct subdomain not owned by any existing BC — and avoids adding to already-loaded BCs (templates, devcontainer, messaging are all under active work). tmux is committed as the session manager (not screen, not background process); `docker run` (not Compose) is the committed invocation style for the initial implementation.

### Scenarios authored: 17

- **Scenarios 01-05** — `bc-container launch`: starts container, clones repo, inits beads, starts tmux session, idempotent-on-running.
- **Scenario 06** — `bc-container attach`: connects to the running tmux session.
- **Scenario 07** — `bc-container inject`: sends a text prompt to the tmux session without attaching.
- **Scenario 08** — `bc-container monitor`: streams tmux session output to host stdout.
- **Scenario 09** — `bc-container stop`: stops the named container.
- **Scenarios 10-11** — `bc-container status`: reports running and stopped states.
- **Scenario 12** — `bc-container list`: shows all known containers with states.
- **Scenarios 13-14** — Mailbox volume sharing: host-written messages readable inside container; container-written responses readable from host.
- **Scenario 15** — Isolation invariant: no sibling BC or lead-shop mounts inside the container.
- **Scenario 16** — Startup-prompt flag on `launch`.
- **Scenario 17** — `bc-container` is on PATH after package install.

### Gaps that need stakeholder or Architect resolution

1. **Mailbox volume mechanism** (bind-mount vs. named volume) — the PO committed the property; mechanism is Architect's pre-state call.
2. **Image tag source** for `bc-container launch` — hardcoded `latest`, config file, or flag; deferred to Architect.
3. **`shopsystem-bc-launcher` repo creation** — the Architect must create and bootstrap this new BC before the first dispatch can land; no existing repo exists.
4. **Monitor-arm inside the container** — scenarios 01-04 implicitly depend on brief 003's settings.json hook being present in the bootstrapped container; the Architect must verify this composes cleanly with the init sequence.

### Clarifying questions for the Architect's BC decomposition

- Does `shopsystem-bc-launcher` need a separate `shop-msg` mailbox of its own (i.e., does the lead shop assign scenarios to it via the standard `assign_scenarios` vehicle, or does the launcher BC get bootstrapped manually for its first work)?
- Should scope item C (mailbox sharing) be a separate `assign_scenarios` to `shopsystem-messaging` (if `shop-msg --bc-root` needs new flag support) or is it zero-messaging-BC-work once the mount is in place?

result: Authored brief 004 (BC container isolation), PDR-004 (command ownership → new shopsystem-bc-launcher BC), and 17 Gherkin scenarios covering launch, attach, inject, monitor, stop, status, list, mailbox sharing, isolation invariant, startup-prompt flag, and CLI install surface — all in /workspaces/shopsystem-product/briefs/004-bc-container-isolation.md, /workspaces/shopsystem-product/pdr/004-bc-container-command-ownership.md, and /workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/bc-launcher/.
agentId: aaf284d4b3eddd2e8 (use SendMessage with to: 'aaf284d4b3eddd2e8' to continue this agent)
<usage>total_tokens: 59290
tool_uses: 38
duration_ms: 315161</usage>
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (2026-05-20 17:53:51Z)**_

You are the lead-po subagent for the shopsystem-product lead shop. Read your full role definition at /workspaces/shopsystem-product/.claude/agents/lead-po.md before doing anything else.

## Request to process

The stakeholder (David) has described the following desired capability:

> BC shops should be able to be started in their own Docker containers. There should be a command that uses Docker and a shop image to start a container, clone a repository, init beads, and start an agent, with the agent running in tmux or something similar so it can be connected to or injected with a startup prompt and also monitored during operation, and also allow direct interaction with the BC. The goal is isolation between the BCs and having work proceed through contracts and not reading code.

## Your task

1. **Interview notes** — Capture the stakeholder's expressed desire in your own words. What behavior would satisfy them? What would not? Where are the boundaries? Write these up as interview notes.

2. **Product brief update** — Check the existing product brief at /workspaces/shopsystem-product/brief.md (if it exists). Update or create it to reflect this new capability direction.

3. **PDR draft** — This is non-obvious enough to warrant a PDR. Draft a new PDR at /workspaces/shopsystem-product/pdr/ using the next available number. The PDR should name:
   - The question being decided
   - Options considered (e.g., tmux vs screen vs background process; docker run vs compose; which repo owns the command)
   - The decision and rationale
   - What BC(s) this capability belongs in (new BC? existing messaging BC? templates BC?)

4. **Initial Gherkin scenarios** — Author the first set of scenarios for this capability. Apply your sufficiency check to every scenario before including it. Likely scenario families:
   - Starting a BC container from scratch (clone, init, start agent)
   - Attaching to a running BC container's agent session
   - Injecting a startup prompt into a new container's agent
   - Monitoring a running BC container's agent output
   - The agent in the container operates via contracts (shop-msg) not direct code reads

   Do NOT add `@scenario_hash:` or `@bc:` tags — those are added at assignment time by the Architect.

5. **Report** — Return a summary (under 300 words): the PDR decision made, the number of scenarios authored, any gaps you could not close without stakeholder clarification, and any clarifying questions the Architect will need to resolve for BC decomposition.

## Working directory
/workspaces/shopsystem-product

## Conventions
- PDR files: pdr/NNN-kebab-title.md, look at existing PDRs for format
- Feature/scenario files: features/ directory, look at existing files for conventions
- Do not write inbox/outbox YAML by hand
- Do not create beads issues — that is the router's job after you report back
- Commit nothing — the router handles git

```text
166	   reply.
167	3. **Apply the clarify-response sufficiency check** above.
168	4. If sufficient, respond via `shop-msg respond clarify --bc-root <BC root>
169	   --work-id <work_id> --question "<your answer>"`. (The schema field is
170	   called `question` because it carries the clarify text in both directions;
171	   when you respond, your answer goes in that field.)
172	5. **Report** what the BC asked, what you answered, and whether the answer
173	   implies a scenario amendment that the Architect should follow up with
174	   via `request_bugfix`.
175	
176	## Reporting back
177	
178	After authoring a scenario or sending a clarify response, return a short
179	report (under 200 words):
180	
181	- If authoring: the scenario body, the intent driver it traces to, the
182	  target BC (if known), and which sufficiency-check conditions you
183	  verified.
184	- If responding to clarify: the BC's work_id, what the BC asked, your
185	  answer, and whether the answer implies a scenario amendment the
186	  Architect should follow up with.
187
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./pdr/001-role-templates-role-complete.md`

```text
1	# PDR-001 — Lead-shop role templates must be role-complete, identity-first
2	
3	**Status:** draft (2026-05-12)
4	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude
5	**Anchored to:** PO intent expressed in conversation 2026-05-12:
6	*"The current architect prompt template is more of a skill for dispatching work."*
7	
8	## Point of intent
9	
10	A lead-shop role template (`lead-po.md`, `lead-architect.md`) must be a
11	**complete representation of the role** — identity, posture, ownership, the
12	full activity scope, and the judgment criteria that decide what is in or
13	out of the role's remit. The current templates are partial: they cover the
14	**subset** of activities that involve sending inter-shop messages, and miss
15	the remainder of the role's §3.2 activity catalogue entirely. As written
16	they read more as **skill manuals for the `shop-msg` CLI** than as **role
17	identities**.
18	
19	## Diagnosis
20	
21	Both templates do carry substantive posture — the Architect's `PRE-STATE
22	DETERMINES VEHICLE — VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY`, the PO's `COMMIT TO SPECIFICS`
23	— and that posture is real role content, not boilerplate. The gap is
24	**scope**: the posture and the procedural drill-down both apply only to the
25	message-sending and clarify-response activities. Comparing template content
26	against the §3.2 activity catalogue:
27	
28	| Activity (§3.2) | Covered in template? |
29	|---|---|
30	| **PO** — Interview stakeholder | No |
31	| **PO** — Maintain product brief | No |
32	| **PO** — Write PDR for new functionality | No |
33	| **PO** — Write Gherkin scenarios | Yes (sufficiency check, anti-rationalization) |
34	| **PO** — Respond to BC `clarify` (scope, vocabulary) | Yes |
35	| **Architect** — Write ADRs | No |
36	| **Architect** — Maintain structurizr workspace | No |
37	| **Architect** — Collaborate with PO on BC decomposition (turn-limited) | No |
38	| **Architect** — Assign scenarios to BCs | Yes (dominant content) |
39	| **Architect** — Reconcile scenario registers against assigned work | No |
40	| **Architect** — Send `request_bugfix` / `request_maintenance` | Yes |
41	| **Architect** — Read a BC-shop's card via `request_shop_card` | No |
42	| **Architect** — Respond to BC `clarify` (architecture) | Yes |
43	
44	An agent that loads `lead-architect.md` today is well-equipped to dispatch
45	the next outbound message but uninformed about its role's other named
46	activities. The asymmetry is not "skill content vs identity" — it is
47	"covered activities vs un-covered activities."
48	
49	## Decision
50	
51	Lead-shop role templates SHALL:
52	
53	1. **Lead with identity and posture that hold across the role's full §3.2
54	   activity catalogue** — not only the subset that involves sending
55	   inter-shop messages. The posture statements that exist today are good
56	   models for what other activities also need.
57	
58	2. **Name every activity the role owns per §3.2**, even if the template's
59	   first version only carries minimal guidance for some of them. Activities
60	   without sufficiency criteria yet should be marked as such, so an agent
61	   loading the template knows the activity is in scope even if the
62	   discipline is still developing.
63	
64	3. **Subordinate procedural CLI content** (specific `shop-msg send` flag
65	   layouts, sufficiency-check ordering for a given message type) to the
66	   role identity. Procedural content is recoverable from a CLI's `--help`;
67	   identity and posture are not.
68	
69	## What this leaves open
70	
71	- **Concrete restructure of `lead-architect.md` and `lead-po.md`** is BC
72	  work — the templates live in
73	  [`shopsystem-templates`](https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-templates).
74	  The lead shop authors Gherkin scenarios pinning what the restructure must
75	  satisfy and dispatches via `assign_scenarios`. Scenario authoring is a
76	  follow-up; this PDR records intent, not the scenarios themselves.
77	
78	- **The parallel question for `bc-implementer.md` / `bc-reviewer.md`**
79	  (do BC-shop templates have the same partial coverage against §4.2?) is
80	  open. Prototype-1 validated the role-template *architecture*, not
81	  necessarily the *coverage* of each template against its activity
82	  catalogue. A second PDR may answer this once Stage-1 of the templates
83	  restructure produces evidence.
84	
85	## Cross-references
86	
87	- [§3.2 Lead-shop activities](03-lead-shop.md#32-activities) — the
88	  authoritative activity catalogue this PDR measures against.
89	- [`repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/lead-architect.md`](repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/lead-architect.md)
90	- [`repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/lead-po.md`](repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/lead-po.md)
91
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 17:54:08Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./pdr/003-claude-md-update-propagation.md`

```text
1	# PDR-003 — CLAUDE.md update propagation
2	
3	**Status:** Proposed
4	**Date:** 2026-05-14
5	**Author:** lead-po (subagent dispatch)
6	**Beads:** lead-5kn
7	
8	## Context
9	
10	A shop must bootstrap and run self-contained — the `repos/` directory of
11	the lead-shop product is not assumed available inside a bootstrapped shop.
12	That was the bootstrap-time framing. The **temporal corollary** has not
13	been pinned by scenarios: once bootstrapped, a shop must stay self-
14	contained as the canonical evolves. Concretely, when the canonical
15	`CLAUDE.md` primer in `shopsystem-templates` changes (e.g., the Monitor-
16	arming directive added by scenarios 71/72/73), bootstrapped shops must
17	receive that change through `shop-templates update`, not through manual
18	re-editing in each shop.
19	
20	The current contract pins the **opposite**: `shop-templates update` is
21	explicitly forbidden to touch the target's `CLAUDE.md`, even when the
22	canonical primer has changed since bootstrap (scenario 39, reinforced by
23	scenario 56's error-path invariant). The source comment at
24	`repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/cli.py:585-587` cites four
25	hashes as the "non-touch invariant on CLAUDE.md / .gitignore / .beads/".
26	
27	The deeper tension is that `CLAUDE.md` is **part-canonical, part-per-
28	shop**: scenario 45 requires the bootstrap-generated `CLAUDE.md` to name
29	the shop's own identity (shop name, role set), while scenario 44 holds
30	the canonical primer as package-data source-of-truth. Empirical evidence:
31	this product's `repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md` carries canonical
32	primer text *and* shop-authored sections like "Repo layout" and "Build &
33	Test" that no canonical template would dictate. Any propagation contract
34	must reconcile these.
35	
36	## Design alternatives
37	
38	### A. Full replace
39	`update` overwrites `CLAUDE.md` from canonical unconditionally. Cleanest
40	mechanism; loses scenario 45 (no shop identity in the result) and erases
41	shop-authored prose. Rejected.
42	
43	### B. Identity parameterization
44	`CLAUDE.md` becomes fully canonical once shop name and role set are
45	template parameters resolved at update time. The shop owns *no*
46	`CLAUDE.md` content. Pure mechanism, but requires that no shop ever
47	benefits from local primer additions — empirically false for the
48	`shopsystem-templates` BC repo today, which carries shop-authored "Repo
49	layout" and "Build & Test" sections. Would force migration of all
50	shop-authored prose elsewhere before adoption. Rejected as the first
51	move; viable as a longer-term direction once the canonical primer is
52	parameterized.
53	
54	### C. Sentinel-delimited canonical region
55	The canonical primer template defines a region delimited by sentinel
56	comments (e.g., `<!-- shop-templates:canonical:begin -->` and
57	`<!-- shop-templates:canonical:end -->`). `update` replaces only the
58	text between sentinels in the target's `CLAUDE.md`; everything outside
59	the sentinels is preserved byte-for-byte. Requires mutating `CLAUDE.md`
60	on every update, which contradicts scenario 39's byte-for-byte non-touch
61	invariant and forces amending scenarios 39 and 56 to scope the invariant
62	to outside-sentinel content. Rejected in favor of F (below); F achieves
63	the same propagation outcome with cleaner ownership and no merge logic.
64	
65	### D. Sectioned merge by header name
66	Like C but keyed on Markdown headers instead of sentinels. Rejected as
67	fragile: header text edits in either the canonical template or the
68	target would silently break the merge contract. Sentinels are explicit;
69	headers are not.
70	
71	### E. Canonical primer in dedicated imported files, with shop-owned `CLAUDE.md`
72	The canonical primer content lives in a dedicated file under
73	`.claude/canonical/`. `CLAUDE.md` is shop-owned end to end and imports
74	the canonical file via Claude Code's `@<filename>` directive. `update`
75	overwrites the canonical imported file in place and never touches
76	`CLAUDE.md`. Preserves scenarios 39, 45, 56 verbatim. Rejected per
77	stakeholder directive (2026-05-14) in favor of F, which trades some
78	scenario preservation for a cleaner ownership story.
79	
80	### F. Canonical-managed `CLAUDE.md` with four typed imports (RECOMMENDED)
81	`CLAUDE.md` is **canonical-managed and overwritten by `update`**. Its
82	body is intentionally minimal: a short header plus a fixed sequence of
83	`@<filename>` imports. The substantive content lives in four typed
84	files, each with a single owner:
85	
86	1. **Shop-name file** — contains the shop's own name (the literal string
87	   passed as `--shop-name` at bootstrap). **Bootstrap-only**, never
88	   touched by `update`. Owner: the shop.
89	2. **Shop-type file** — contains the shop's type token (`bc` or `lead`).
90	   **Bootstrap-only**, never touched by `update`. Owner: the shop.
91	3. **Canonical primer file** — the canonical router-prompt content for
92	   this shop type. Ships as package data; **overwritten by `update`** on
93	   drift. Owner: canonical (`shop-templates` package data).
94	4. **Shop-specific primer file** — shop-authored prose (e.g., "Repo
95	   layout", "Build & Test", anything the shop wants to add). Created
96	   at bootstrap as an empty (or near-empty) placeholder. **Never touched
97	   by `update`**. Owner: the shop.
98	
99	`CLAUDE.md` itself is a deterministic function of canonical package data
100	and the shop type — the same `CLAUDE.md` body is written into every shop
101	of a given type. Bootstrap parameters (`--shop-name`, `--shop-type`) are
102	captured in the two shop-owned dotfiles imported by `CLAUDE.md`, not
103	spliced into `CLAUDE.md` itself.
104	
105	Pros:
106	- **Cleanest ownership story.** Every file has exactly one owner. The
107	  path itself signals ownership (see "File-path convention" below). A
108	  reader can answer "who writes this file?" without reading its
109	  contents.
110	- **Canonical content propagation is unconditional and bulletproof.**
111	  The shop cannot accidentally remove an import or hand-edit `CLAUDE.md`
112	  in a way that loses canonical content, because `update` rewrites
113	  `CLAUDE.md` from canonical every time. No drift is possible.
114	- **No merge logic, no sentinels, no parsing.** `update` performs four
115	  operations at most: rewrite `CLAUDE.md`, rewrite the canonical primer
116	  file, leave the two shop-owned files alone, leave the shop-specific
117	  primer alone.
118	- **Shop-authored prose has a dedicated home.** The shop-specific
119	  primer file is the named, expected place for "Repo layout", "Build &
120	  Test", and other shop-local content. Separation of concerns is
121	  explicit at the file boundary, not buried inside a multi-author file.
122	- **Symmetric with how `update` already handles `.claude/agents/`.** Both
123	  surfaces become "canonical package data poured in place"; `CLAUDE.md`
124	  joins the same model rather than being a special case.
125	
126	Cons:
127	- **Scenario 39 is contradicted.** `update` does overwrite `CLAUDE.md`.
128	  Operators who hand-edited `CLAUDE.md` under the old contract lose
129	  those edits on `update`. A migration story is required (see below).
130	  The recommendation accepts this hit deliberately; the cleaner
131	  ownership story is judged to be worth the cost.
132	- **Four files instead of one.** More bootstrap surface; a new reader
133	  has more files to orient to. Mitigated by `CLAUDE.md` being short and
134	  literally naming each import (e.g., `@.claude/shop/name.md  # the
135	  shop's own name`) so the import graph is self-documenting.
136	- **Depends on Claude Code's `@<filename>` import mechanism** being
137	  stable across consumers (CLI, IDE extension, future agent runtimes).
138	  Same dependency as alternative E.
139	- **Bootstrap parameter capture lives in a file, not in prose.** A
140	  human glancing at `CLAUDE.md` will see only the imports, not the
141	  shop's name directly. They must follow `@.claude/shop/name.md` to
142	  see "shopsystem-product". Mitigated by `CLAUDE.md` placing the
143	  shop-identity imports at the top, so the indirection is visible
144	  immediately.
145	
146	## Recommendation
147	
148	Adopt **alternative F — canonical-managed `CLAUDE.md` with four typed
149	imports**. The contract becomes:
150	
151	1. Canonical primer content for each shop type ships as a dedicated file
152	   in `shop-templates` package data (`lead-primer.md`, `bc-primer.md`),
153	   parallel to the role-prompt template files already shipped there. A
154	   canonical `CLAUDE.md` template body also ships as package data, one
155	   per shop type.
156	2. Bootstrap writes **five** files into the target shop:
157	   - `CLAUDE.md` at the target root (canonical-managed).
158	   - `.claude/shop/name.md` (shop-owned, bootstrap-only) — the literal
159	     value passed as `--shop-name`.
160	   - `.claude/shop/type.md` (shop-owned, bootstrap-only) — the literal
161	     value passed as `--shop-type`.
162	   - `.claude/canonical/<shop-type>-primer.md` (canonical-managed) —
163	     the canonical router-prompt body.
164	   - `.claude/shop/primer.md` (shop-owned, bootstrap-only placeholder)
165	     — empty or near-empty; the shop fills it in over time.
166	   `CLAUDE.md`'s body imports those four files in a fixed order with
167	   short headings that name each import's role.
168	3. `update` overwrites two files: `CLAUDE.md` (from the canonical
169	   `CLAUDE.md` template for the shop's declared type) and
170	   `.claude/canonical/<shop-type>-primer.md` (from the canonical primer
171	   for the shop's declared type). It reads `.claude/shop/type.md` to
172	   know which shop type's canonical to apply.
173	4. `update` is idempotent when both canonical-managed files already
174	   match package data byte-for-byte — no diagnostic, no file mtime
175	   change, exit zero.
176	5. `update` does **not** touch `.claude/shop/name.md`,
177	   `.claude/shop/type.md`, or `.claude/shop/primer.md` under any
178	   circumstances.
179	6. **Migration** for legacy shops (including this product's own lead
180	   shop today, whose `CLAUDE.md` predates the four-file split): see the
181	   "Migration" subsection below.
182	
183	### File-path convention
184	
185	The split lives under `.claude/`:
186	
187	- `.claude/canonical/` — **canonical-managed**. Files here are written
188	  by `bootstrap` and rewritten by `update` from package data. Operators
189	  must not hand-edit; edits will be silently reverted on the next
190	  `update`. Today this directory already houses the canonical agent
191	  files (`bc-implementer.md`, etc., via `.claude/agents/` — see note
192	  below); alternative F adds `<shop-type>-primer.md` to its content
193	  set.
194	- `.claude/shop/` — **shop-managed**. Files here are written once at
195	  bootstrap and then owned by the shop. `update` never touches anything
196	  under `.claude/shop/`.
197	- `CLAUDE.md` at the target root — **canonical-managed** (this is what
198	  changes under F). Its content is a deterministic function of
199	  `.claude/shop/type.md` and canonical package data.
200	
201	Note on `.claude/agents/`: today the agent role-prompt files live there
202	and are managed by `update`. Under F they remain where they are;
203	`.claude/agents/` is canonical-managed in the same sense as
204	`.claude/canonical/`. The PDR does not propose renaming `.claude/agents/`
205	into `.claude/canonical/agents/`; that is a follow-up consolidation if
206	desired.
207	
208	Rationale for path choice: ownership is legible from the path alone.
209	`.claude/canonical/...` says "do not hand-edit"; `.claude/shop/...` says
210	"this is yours". A new operator does not need to read a contract
211	document to know which is which.
212	
213	### Migration
214	
215	A legacy shop (one bootstrapped under the pre-F contract) has a
216	monolithic `CLAUDE.md` containing canonical primer text inlined,
217	shop-authored prose interleaved, and no `.claude/shop/` directory.
218	This product's own lead shop is exactly such a legacy shop today, so
219	the migration UX is concrete, not hypothetical.
220	
221	The recommended migration shape is **refuse-with-diagnostic** (not
222	auto-migrate). When `update` runs in a target that lacks
223	`.claude/shop/type.md`, it:
224	
225	- Exits non-zero.
226	- Writes a diagnostic to stderr naming the migration steps the
227	  operator must perform: create `.claude/shop/{name,type,primer}.md`
228	  with appropriate contents; move any shop-authored prose from the
229	  existing `CLAUDE.md` into `.claude/shop/primer.md`; then re-run
230	  `update`.
231	- Touches no files in the target.
232	
233	Rationale: auto-migrate would have to parse a free-form `CLAUDE.md` to
234	separate canonical text from shop-authored prose, with no reliable
235	delimiter. That's exactly the brittleness alternative D was rejected
236	for. Refuse-with-diagnostic keeps the migration step explicit, manual,
237	and reviewable; the cost is a one-time per-shop operator action.
238	
239	A future `shop-templates migrate` subcommand could automate the file
240	moves once we accept the legacy `CLAUDE.md` will need a sentinel or
241	operator-marked region to make the split deterministic. That is out of
242	scope for this PDR.
243	
244	## Scenarios this PDR contradicts
245	
246	- **39** (`update-preserves-shop-owned-claude-md-edits`). Under F,
247	  `update` overwrites `CLAUDE.md` from canonical. The original
248	  invariant ("byte-for-byte preservation of `CLAUDE.md`") no longer
249	  holds against `CLAUDE.md` itself. The replacement invariant is that
250	  `update` preserves shop-authored content **in
251	  `.claude/shop/primer.md`** byte-for-byte; `CLAUDE.md` itself is
252	  canonical-managed and outside the preservation contract. The PO
253	  proposes **amending** scenario 39 (not superseding) — the same
254	  identifier should continue to express "update does not destroy the
255	  shop's authored content," with the storage location updated to
256	  `.claude/shop/primer.md`. Architect's call whether amend or supersede.
257	
258	## Scenarios this PDR preserves (unchanged)
259	
260	- **31** (bootstrap writes top-level `CLAUDE.md`) — preserved.
261	  `bootstrap` continues to write `CLAUDE.md`; the file is non-empty
262	  (its body contains the four import lines plus a short header).
263	- **44** (canonical primer is package data) — preserved. The canonical
264	  primer continues to ship as package data, now as dedicated
265	  `<shop-type>-primer.md` files. A second canonical asset
266	  (`<shop-type>-claude-md.md` or similar — the canonical `CLAUDE.md`
267	  template body) is added; this is additive to 44, not a contradiction.
268	- **56** (`update-rejects-missing-target-with-argparse-error`) — the
269	  error-path clause that `CLAUDE.md` is unchanged from before the
270	  invocation **holds**, because `update` writes nothing on argparse
271	  error. The wording can stand verbatim; on the success path,
272	  `CLAUDE.md` may now change, but the error-path invariant is
273	  unrelated to the success-path contract.
274	- **40, 41** (`.gitignore` and `.beads/` non-touch invariants) —
275	  unaffected; F changes only the `CLAUDE.md` surface.
276	
277	## Scenarios this PDR amends (Architect call)
278	
279	- **45** (`bootstrap-generated-claude-md-names-the-shops-identity`).
280	  Under F, the substring check `that file contains the literal substring
281	  "<shop_name>"` no longer passes against `CLAUDE.md` directly — the
282	  shop name lives in `.claude/shop/name.md`, imported by `CLAUDE.md`.
283	  Two phrasings, Architect's pick:
284	  - **Phrasing A (PO leans):** retain the assertion at the level of
285	    the assistant-resolved `CLAUDE.md` (imports included). The
286	    scenario pins "the shop's identity is named in the agent's
287	    startup context," which is what we actually care about.
288	  - **Phrasing B:** amend the scenario to assert "the `CLAUDE.md`
289	    import graph names the shop identity" — i.e., `CLAUDE.md` imports
290	    `.claude/shop/name.md`, and that file contains `<shop_name>`.
291	    More mechanism-precise; couples the scenario to the import
292	    discipline.
293	  PO leans toward Phrasing A.
294	- **71 / 72 / 73** (Monitor-arming directive in canonical and
295	  bootstrap-generated `CLAUDE.md`). Same dichotomy as in v2 of this
296	  PDR. Under F, the directive lives in
297	  `.claude/canonical/<shop-type>-primer.md`, reachable from
298	  `CLAUDE.md` via import. Phrasing A (assertion at the level of
299	  assistant-observable behavior — the directive is present in the
300	  agent's startup context) is preserved as-is. Phrasing B (assert the
301	  storage location directly) requires amendment to name the canonical
302	  primer file. PO leans Phrasing A — the scenarios pin agent-
303	  observable behavior, not storage location.
304	
305	## New scenarios this PDR will author after acceptance
306	
307	1. **Canonical `CLAUDE.md` body ships as package data, per shop type.**
308	   `shop-templates` exposes a canonical `CLAUDE.md` template body for
309	   each shop type via the same package-data surface as the primer and
310	   role-prompt templates. (Companion to scenario 44.)
311	2. **Bootstrap writes the canonical-managed `CLAUDE.md`** whose body
312	   is the canonical template body for the shop's type, byte-for-byte.
313	3. **Bootstrap writes `.claude/shop/name.md`** containing exactly the
314	   value passed as `--shop-name`, with no other content.
315	4. **Bootstrap writes `.claude/shop/type.md`** containing exactly the
316	   value passed as `--shop-type` (`bc` or `lead`), with no other content.
317	5. **Bootstrap writes `.claude/canonical/<shop-type>-primer.md`** with
318	   the canonical primer for the chosen shop type, byte-for-byte from
319	   package data.
320	6. **Bootstrap writes `.claude/shop/primer.md`** as a shop-authored
321	   placeholder (empty or near-empty); the operator may populate it
322	   later. (Companion to "shop-authored prose has a dedicated home"
323	   under F.)
324	7. **`CLAUDE.md` body imports the four typed files in a fixed order**
325	   — property-level assertion: when the assistant resolves `CLAUDE.md`,
326	   the contents of all four files are present in its startup context.
327	   Literal import syntax is an Implementer follow-up against
328	   authoritative Claude Code documentation.
329	8. **`update` overwrites `CLAUDE.md`** with the canonical `CLAUDE.md`
330	   body for the shop's declared type when the target's `CLAUDE.md`
331	   has drifted from canonical.
332	9. **`update` overwrites `.claude/canonical/<shop-type>-primer.md`**
333	   when the canonical primer has drifted from the target's copy.
334	10. **`update` does not touch `.claude/shop/name.md`** under any
335	    circumstances on the success path.
336	11. **`update` does not touch `.claude/shop/type.md`** under any
337	    circumstances on the success path.
338	12. **`update` does not touch `.claude/shop/primer.md`** under any
339	    circumstances on the success path (this is the F-shaped
340	    replacement for the old scenario 39's intent — shop-authored
341	    content is preserved, just in a different file).
342	13. **`update` is idempotent** when both canonical-managed files
343	    already match package data byte-for-byte — exit zero, no
344	    diagnostic, no file mtime change.
345	14. **`update` reads `.claude/shop/type.md`** to determine which
346	    shop type's canonical templates to apply. (Pins the data flow:
347	    the shop's declared type at bootstrap is authoritative for
348	    subsequent updates, not a flag re-passed at update time.)
349	15. **Migration scenario — refuse-with-diagnostic.** When `update`
350	    runs against a target that lacks `.claude/shop/type.md` (legacy
351	    bootstrap), `update` exits non-zero, writes a diagnostic naming
352	    the migration steps the operator must perform, and touches no
353	    files in the target.
354	
355	## Open questions (for Architect)
356	
357	1. **Phrasing A vs. Phrasing B** for scenarios 45 and 71/72/73 — the
358	   Architect's call when assigning the new scenario set.
359	2. **Whether to amend or supersede scenario 39.** PO recommends amend
360	   (same identifier, new storage location for the preservation
361	   contract).
362	3. **Exact filenames** for the canonical assets under
363	   `shop-templates`' package data — the PDR names `<shop-type>-
364	   primer.md` and a parallel canonical `CLAUDE.md` body asset, but
365	   does not pin the literal filename of the latter (e.g.,
366	   `<shop-type>-claude-md.md` vs. `claude-md-<shop-type>.md`). An
367	   Implementer concern.
368	4. **Whether `update`'s migration exit code should be 1 or another
369	   non-zero value**, and whether the diagnostic should be
370	   machine-parseable (e.g., a stable error tag). PO recommends 1 with
371	   a free-form but consistent diagnostic; Architect may refine.
372	5. **Whether `.claude/shop/type.md` and `.claude/shop/name.md` should
373	   be plain-text single-line files** or a single combined YAML/TOML
374	   file. PO recommends two plain-text files for path-level legibility
375	   (the path itself names what it holds); Architect may prefer one
376	   structured file for forward extensibility (e.g., adding a future
377	   `shop-version` field).
378
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 17:54:12Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./briefs/001-inter-shop-messaging-encapsulation.md`

```text
1	# Brief 001 — Inter-shop messaging encapsulation
2	
3	**Status:** draft, intent gaps resolved (2026-05-12), item C scope
4	corrected to empirical reality (2026-05-12)
5	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
6	**Beads:** [`lead-231`](#) (this brief)
7	**Anchored to:** mechanism observation
8	[`shopsystem-templates-kin`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/outbox/shopsystem-templates-kin-mechanism_observation.yaml)
9	emitted by the shopsystem-templates BC during lead-kq0, sharpened in
10	conversation 2026-05-12, three intent gaps resolved by user-driver
11	2026-05-12 (tests carve-out from invariant 1; spec stays conceptual
12	with no filename conventions; catalog audit covers all currently-
13	implemented message types — `request_scenario_register` and
14	`request_shop_card` deferred per
15	[`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 1](../findings/from-prototype-1.md),
16	with the decoupling invariant carrying forward to their eventual
17	authoring).
18	
19	## Point of intent
20	
21	Inter-shop messaging exists as a **bounded surface** that other parts of
22	the shop-system compose with, not a directory layout that other parts of
23	the shop-system inspect. Today, templates and subagents reach past the
24	surface and read `inbox/` and `outbox/` directly because the surface
25	does not yet expose the operations they need. The cost is that
26	`shop-msg`'s storage layout — a filesystem convention that should be a
27	private implementation detail of the messaging BC — has become a public
28	shape that every consumer depends on.
29	
30	The brief commits the shop-system to two invariants and a corollary,
31	and names the five scope items the invariants imply.
32	
33	## The two invariants
34	
35	### Invariant 1 — `shop-msg` is the sole inter-shop messaging surface
36	
37	No consumer (template, subagent, runbook, ad-hoc script) inspects
38	messaging storage directly. The storage representation (filesystem YAML
39	today; SQLite, a daemon, or a queue tomorrow) is a **private
40	implementation detail of the messaging BC**.
41	
42	**Carve-out for messaging-BC tests.** Tests of `shop-msg`'s own
43	implementation may freely inspect messaging storage — they are the
44	messaging BC, not inter-shop consumers. Tests of BCs that consume
45	`shop-msg` (and any non-test consumer of any kind) must use the CLI for
46	setup and assertion like any other consumer.
47	
48	This is the messaging-surface application of
49	[`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 6](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
50	("package CLIs are the integration boundary"). Prototype 1 validated
51	the principle for catalog schemas, scenarios canonicalisation, and the
52	test harness. It applies equally to the messaging surface — and the
53	mechanism observation `shopsystem-templates-kin` is the evidence that
54	it currently does not hold.
55	
56	The observation enumerates the leak:
57	
58	- `ls inbox/ outbox/` to enumerate state.
59	- "A message is unprocessed when there is no outbox file for its
60	  `work_id`" — set-difference on filenames.
61	- `grep message_type inbox/<work_id>.yaml` to decide which subagent to
62	  dispatch.
63	
64	These appear in the canonical templates this BC ships
65	(`bc-implementer.md`, `bc-reviewer.md`, the BC-router section of
66	`repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md`). Because those files are
67	`shopsystem-templates`'s **product**, the leak ships to every
68	downstream BC that adopts them.
69	
70	### Invariant 2 — Inter-shop messages are self-contained
71	
72	The receiving shop acts on a message **without consulting the sending
73	shop's internal artifacts** (beads, source files, working directories).
74	A message carries everything its recipient needs to decide what to do.
75	The local-clone topology currently hides violations of this — every
76	shop's repo is reachable on the same filesystem, so an implementer that
77	reads sender-side state happens to succeed. Remote-shop topology will
78	not be so forgiving.
79	
80	### Corollary — `shop-msg` and `bd` are decoupled
81	
82	Catalog schemas must not require participation in `bd`. Template
83	language must not instruct creating beads as a precondition for
84	messaging. `bd` is each shop's local work-tracker; messaging is the
85	inter-shop surface; the two may compose but **neither requires the
86	other**.
87	
88	The originating observation itself shows the current coupling:
89	`MechanismObservation.bd_ref: shopsystem-templates-kin` is a required
90	field, and the bc-implementer template instructs the BC to "create a
91	beads issue capturing what you observed" before emitting the message.
92	A shop that does not use `bd` cannot emit a `mechanism_observation`
93	today — even though `bd` participation is **its own concern**, not the
94	inter-shop concern.
95	
96	## Five scope items
97	
98	The brief commits intent on five items. Each is named here in product
99	terms; vehicle selection and implementation specifics are the
100	Architect's call after BC pre-state verification.
101	
102	### A — `shop-msg` CLI surface (symmetric, both BC and lead sides)
103	
104	`shop-msg` exposes the operations every consumer needs so that no
105	consumer falls back on `ls`/`cat`/`grep` against the mailbox
106	directories. At minimum:
107	
108	- **Enumerate pending unprocessed work.** A consumer (a BC-shop router,
109	  the lead's drain operation, a runbook) can ask "is there an
110	  unprocessed message here, and what is its `message_type`?" without
111	  inspecting the filesystem. `--bc <name>` filtering is part of the
112	  contract from day one — the lead side will need it.
113	- **Read inbox by `work_id`.** Parallel to the existing
114	  `shop-msg read outbox <work_id>`. Today the templates instruct
115	  reading inbox YAML directly because no CLI operation exists.
116	- **Parity check.** No operation a consumer needs (whether template,
117	  subagent, runbook, or ad-hoc script) currently requires direct file
118	  access. The Architect closes this by enumerating the call sites
119	  during pre-state verification.
120	
121	The contract is **symmetric** between BC side and lead side. The lead
122	shop never touches BC outboxes directly any more than a BC-shop touches
123	its own inbox directly. Both sides use the same surface.
124	
125	### B — Templates rewrite
126	
127	The canonical templates at
128	`repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-implementer.md`
129	and `bc-reviewer.md`, together with the BC-router section of
130	`repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md`, **use the `shop-msg` CLI only**
131	— no direct references to `inbox/` or `outbox/` directories or to
132	their filename conventions, except in conceptual material that
133	explicitly describes the protocol at the spec level.
134	
135	The same discipline applies to the lead-side templates
136	(`lead-po.md`, `lead-architect.md` in the same templates directory)
137	and to the lead-shop `CLAUDE.md`. The point of intent is uniform across
138	roles: **no role enacts the protocol by inspecting messaging storage.**
139	
140	### C — Catalog schema decoupling
141	
142	The catalog schemas under `shop-msg`'s `catalog` package are audited
143	in full across the **six currently-implemented message types**
144	(`assign_scenarios`, `request_bugfix`, `request_maintenance`,
145	`clarify`, `work_done`, `mechanism_observation`) — for required
146	references to `bd`. `MechanismObservation.bd_ref` is the known
147	instance, but the audit does not presuppose the other five are clean:
148	full coverage of what exists is cheap and catches anything that has
149	slipped in unnoticed.
150	
151	`request_scenario_register` and `request_shop_card` are **deferred**
152	per [`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 1](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
153	— no Pydantic schema for either lives in `catalog/schemas.py` today,
154	and no open lead-shop bead currently schedules authoring them (the
155	prototype-1 issues `6mk` and `r7u` closed as deferred 2026-05-10).
156	Authoring of `request_scenario_register`'s schema will naturally ride
157	with [`lead-otu`](#) (the per-BC scenario register surface), since
158	that message type depends on what a register IS. `request_shop_card`
159	awaits its own driver.
160	
161	The decoupling invariant **carries forward**: when those two schemas
162	are eventually authored, they honor the invariant from the start
163	alongside whatever functional schema design they require. The brief
164	commits the invariant; the future work commits the schemas.
165	
166	The committed outcome (for the six in-scope today): `bd` references
167	are either **optional** or **removed**. If retained, they are framed
168	strictly as **provenance metadata** that the producer happens to
169	carry, never as **required participation** in `bd`. The schema makes
170	it possible for a shop that does not use `bd` to emit any message
171	type the catalog defines.
172	
173	### D — Template-language decoupling
174	
175	The template language is audited in parallel — particularly the
176	`bc-implementer.md` "Surfacing mechanism observations" section, which
177	today instructs:
178	
179	> Create a beads issue capturing what you observed: `bd create --title …`
180	>
181	> Emit the wire message: `shop-msg respond mechanism_observation
182	> --bd-ref <bead id from step 1> …`
183	
184	This is rewritten so that **`bd` participation is an independent
185	local-tracker concern, never a precondition for messaging**. If a shop
186	uses `bd`, the template may describe how the two compose — but the
187	ordering "bead first, then message" is removed, and the message is
188	emit-able regardless of whether a bead exists.
189	
190	The audit covers all four canonical templates
191	(`lead-po.md`, `lead-architect.md`, `bc-implementer.md`,
192	`bc-reviewer.md`) and both `CLAUDE.md` files (lead-shop and the
193	shopsystem-templates BC).
194	
195	### E — Spec edits (§4 and §5)
196	
197	[`§4`](../04-bc-shop.md) is amended to describe messaging in **purely
198	conceptual terms**: *what flows* (inbox messages from lead, outbox
199	responses from BC) and *what consumers do via the CLI*. The spec
200	proper **does not name any filename pattern, directory layout, or
201	on-disk convention**. Storage shape — including any filename
202	convention like `<work_id>-<response_type>.yaml` — is `shop-msg`'s
203	internal realization, not part of the inter-shop contract. The
204	Architect may retain examples of the current realization in
205	implementation notes inside the messaging BC, but those examples do
206	not enter the §4 spec text.
207	
208	[`§5`](../05-inter-shop-protocol.md) is amended to name the
209	**self-contained-messages invariant** explicitly. §5.6 already commits
210	the schema-as-contract principle; the self-contained-messages claim is
211	a sibling and belongs alongside it (or in a new §5.7). §5 is held to
212	the same discipline as §4: it characterises the protocol, never the
213	storage.
214	
215	The Architect directs implementation specifics for both edits; the
216	brief commits the **intent** that the spec stops describing storage
217	shape and starts describing protocol semantics. The principle is the
218	same one [`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 4](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
219	applies to invariants: claims belong where they are enforceable;
220	conventions that are not part of the contract are noise that invites
221	slippery-slope dependency.
222	
223	## Out of scope — named explicitly
224	
225	**Auto-loading messages into `bd` (drain automation).** The decoupling
226	holds in both directions: messaging does not require `bd`, and `bd`
227	does not need to be fed automatically by messaging. If a `shop-msg
228	drain` command (or similar) is wanted later, a follow-on brief opens
229	when manual-drain friction surfaces in real-product BC use. This is
230	adjacent to
231	[`findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md §6`](../findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md)
232	("lead drain formalization") — that item stays **deferred for now**.
233	
234	## Sequencing
235	
236	- **Messaging half (A + C) and spec edits (E)** can be authored and
237	  dispatched **independently** of templates work. They target
238	  shopsystem-messaging and the lead-shop spec respectively; neither
239	  collides with lead-kq0.
240	
241	- **Templates half (B + D)** **waits on lead-kq0
242	  ([PDR-001](../pdr/001-role-templates-role-complete.md))
243	  closing.** B and D rewrite the same template surface lead-kq0 is
244	  currently restructuring. Collision risk is high otherwise:
245	  lead-kq0's restructure changes the **shape** of the templates; B+D
246	  changes their **content** with respect to messaging storage. The two
247	  changes interleave badly if dispatched in parallel.
248	
249	The ordering is a sequencing constraint on dispatch, not on authoring.
250	The Architect may verify pre-state and pick vehicles for B+D before
251	lead-kq0 closes, but the `assign_scenarios` / `request_bugfix`
252	dispatches for B+D should not land in the templates BC's inbox until
253	lead-kq0's `work_done` does.
254	
255	## Vehicle hints (Architect's call)
256	
257	For the Architect's awareness during pre-state verification — not as a
258	prejudgment of the discriminator:
259	
260	- **A** likely lands as `assign_scenarios` against shopsystem-messaging
261	  (new capability: the read-side and enumeration commands do not exist
262	  in `shop-msg` today, per `shop-msg --help`).
263	- **C** likely lands as `request_bugfix` against shopsystem-messaging
264	  (tightening existing schemas: catalog schemas exist and carry the
265	  coupling; the change is to remove or relax the coupling, not to add
266	  new schema infrastructure).
267	- **B + D** likely land as `request_bugfix` against
268	  shopsystem-templates (tightening existing templates: the templates
269	  exist; the change is to replace their direct-mailbox references with
270	  CLI references and to remove "bead first, then message" ordering).
271	- **E** likely lands as `request_maintenance` against the lead-shop
272	  spec docs (flat content change in `04-bc-shop.md` and
273	  `05-inter-shop-protocol.md` — no new behavior, no scenario
274	  movement).
275	
276	These are hints. The Architect's `PRE-STATE DETERMINES VEHICLE —
277	VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY` posture stands.
278	
279	## Grounding artifacts
280	
281	- [`repos/shopsystem-templates/outbox/shopsystem-templates-kin-mechanism_observation.yaml`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/outbox/shopsystem-templates-kin-mechanism_observation.yaml)
282	  — the originating BC observation that started this brief.
283	- [`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 6](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
284	  — the validated principle ("package CLIs are the integration
285	  boundary") this brief extends to the messaging surface.
286	- [`findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md` §6](../findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md)
287	  — drain formalization, **referenced as deferred** to make the
288	  out-of-scope boundary explicit.
289	- [`repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md)
290	  — current BC inbox/outbox protocol section; shows the current
291	  coupling.
292	- [`repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-implementer.md`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-implementer.md)
293	  and
294	  [`bc-reviewer.md`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-reviewer.md)
295	  — canonical templates whose language scope B and D rewrite.
296	- [`CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md), [`§4`](../04-bc-shop.md),
297	  [`§5`](../05-inter-shop-protocol.md) — spec-edit targets for scope E.
298	
299	## What this leaves open
300	
301	The brief commits **intent**, not scenarios. Scenarios come after the
302	Architect verifies BC pre-state and picks vehicles per the
303	discriminator. Specifically:
304	
305	- **Exact CLI command names and flag shapes for A.** "Enumerate
306	  pending" might be `shop-msg pending`, `shop-msg list`, or
307	  `shop-msg next`; "read inbox" is most plausibly
308	  `shop-msg read inbox <work_id>` for parity with `read outbox`, but
309	  the Architect names the shapes in the dispatched scenarios.
310	- **Whether `bd_ref` becomes optional, is removed entirely, or is
311	  renamed to a neutral `provenance_ref`.** All three are consistent
312	  with the invariant; pre-state verification on the catalog schemas
313	  picks one.
314	- **Whether E is one `request_maintenance` or two.** §4 and §5 edits
315	  are independent in principle; the Architect may bundle or split.
316	
317	These are vehicle-level questions, not intent-level. The PO's commit
318	is the two invariants + the five scope items + the sequencing
319	constraint.
320
```
</details></tool-use>
