 `./briefs/004-bc-container-isolation.md`

```text
1	# Brief 004 — BC container isolation
2	
3	**Status:** draft (2026-05-20)
4	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
5	**Anchored to:** user-driver observation 2026-05-20:
6	*"BC shops should be able to be started in their own Docker containers.
7	There should be a command that uses Docker and a shop image to start a
8	container, clone a repository, init beads, and start an agent, with the
9	agent running in tmux or something similar so it can be connected to or
10	injected with a startup prompt and also monitored during operation, and
11	also allow direct interaction with the BC. The goal is isolation between
12	the BCs and having work proceed through contracts and not reading code."*
13	
14	---
15	
16	## Interview notes (PO capture)
17	
18	The stakeholder's expressed desire, in PO's own words:
19	
20	**What behavior would satisfy the stakeholder:**
21	
22	- A single command starts a container for one BC shop: pulls/uses a
23	  shop image, clones the BC's repository, initializes beads inside the
24	  container, and starts a Claude Code agent session inside that
25	  container.
26	- The agent session runs under a terminal multiplexer (tmux named) so
27	  that (a) the container keeps running even after the initiating terminal
28	  exits, (b) a human can attach to the session to observe or type, and
29	  (c) an automated driver can inject text into the session (the "startup
30	  prompt" use case).
31	- The container can be directly interacted with — not just observed —
32	  after launch.
33	- Work between BCs proceeds through `shop-msg` contracts, not through
34	  reading each other's source code.
35	
36	**What would NOT satisfy the stakeholder:**
37	
38	- Running all BCs in the same container or on the same host without
39	  process isolation (no container boundary).
40	- An agent that reads the lead shop's source files or another BC's
41	  source files directly; the isolation is not just technical (separate
42	  container) but behavioral (contracts only).
43	- A container that is opaque — no mechanism to attach, observe, or
44	  inject a prompt.
45	
46	**Boundaries the PO commits to:**
47	
48	1. **One container per BC shop.** The unit of isolation is one BC,
49	   not one product. The command is per-BC.
50	2. **The container is started from the shopsystem devcontainer image.**
51	   The devcontainer image (built and published by
52	   `shopsystem-devcontainer`) is the base; `bc-container launch` (or
53	   equivalent) is the command that runs it appropriately.
54	3. **tmux is the session manager.** The agent runs inside a named tmux
55	   session inside the container. Human and automated access go through
56	   tmux (`attach-session` to observe/interact; `send-keys` to inject).
57	4. **The clone-and-init sequence happens at container start.** The
58	   container is ephemeral from the outside; the initialization sequence
59	   (clone, `bd init`, venv sync, arm Monitor) runs on every fresh
60	   container launch.
61	5. **Work isolation is behavioral, not just technical.** A BC agent
62	   in its container has no read access to the lead shop's workspace or
63	   to sibling BC source trees. All inter-shop traffic goes through
64	   `shop-msg`.
65	
66	**Open questions the PO cannot close without stakeholder clarification
67	or Architect pre-state verification:**
68	
69	- **Shared vs. private `shop-msg` mailbox surface.** If each BC runs in
70	  its own container, the mailbox directories (`inbox/`, `outbox/`) that
71	  the lead shop drops messages into and reads from must be accessible
72	  from outside the container. The PO commits that this is solved (the
73	  contract works); how it is solved — named volumes, a mounted
74	  host directory, a port-forwarded daemon, or something else — is the
75	  Architect's call on pre-state.
76	- **Image tag pinning.** The `bc-container launch` command needs to
77	  know which image tag to use. Whether this is always `latest`, a
78	  pinned semver from a config file, or a flag — the PO defers to the
79	  Architect.
80	- **Mailbox seeding.** If the lead drops a message into the BC's inbox
81	  before the container has launched, the message must be present when
82	  the container starts. How the host-side `shop-msg send` and the
83	  container-side `shop-msg read` share state is the key pre-state
84	  question for the Architect.
85	- **Which repo owns the `bc-container launch` command.** The PO's
86	  stance: this is a new capability whose home is a new BC
87	  (`shopsystem-bc-launcher` or similar), OR it is a new subcommand on
88	  an existing BC's CLI. The Architect's pre-state determines whether
89	  any existing BC (`shopsystem-templates`, `shopsystem-devcontainer`,
90	  or `shopsystem-messaging`) is the natural owner, or whether a new BC
91	  is warranted. PDR-004 names the options and the decision.
92	
93	---
94	
95	## Point of intent
96	
97	The shopsystem today runs all BC agents on the same host, typically in
98	a single devcontainer. Isolation between BCs is **social, not technical**:
99	the spec (§4) says a BC-shop agent must not read sibling source trees,
100	but nothing prevents it. When a BC agent is curious or confused, the
101	most convenient path is the wrong one: reading another BC's code instead
102	of emitting a `clarify`.
103	
104	This brief commits the framework to **container-level isolation per BC
105	shop**: each BC agent runs in its own Docker container, started from the
106	canonical devcontainer image, with an agent session accessible via tmux.
107	The behavioral goal — work proceeds through `shop-msg` contracts, not
108	code reads — becomes enforced at the OS boundary, not just by instruction.
109	
110	The brief carries **one invariant**, **four scope items**, and an
111	**explicit out-of-scope boundary**.
112	
113	---
114	
115	## The invariant
116	
117	### Isolation is technical, not just instructed
118	
119	A BC shop's agent container has no access to the lead shop's working
120	directory or to sibling BC source trees. The host may map the BC's own
121	repository and its `shop-msg` mailbox volume into the container; nothing
122	else from the host workspace is mounted. Any communication between shops
123	goes through `shop-msg` — emitting messages on one side, reading them
124	on the other.
125	
126	This invariant makes the §4 "no cross-BC code reads" constraint
127	**unenforced-by-instruction → enforced-by-mount**. An agent that
128	physically cannot read sibling source is not relying on role discipline
129	alone; the architecture does the work.
130	
131	---
132	
133	## Four scope items
134	
135	### A — `bc-container launch` command
136	
137	A command (exact name the Architect picks after pre-state; herein called
138	`bc-container launch`) takes a BC name (or repo URL + branch) and:
139	
140	1. Pulls or reuses the canonical devcontainer image.
141	2. Starts a container with the BC's repo (or a volume where the repo
142	   will be cloned) mounted as the workspace.
143	3. Inside the container, runs the initialization sequence:
144	   - Clone the BC's repository (if not already present in the workspace
145	     volume).
146	   - Run `bd init` to initialize beads.
147	   - Install the shop's Python packages (`pip install -e .` or
148	     equivalent).
149	   - Arm the Monitor watcher per the canonical `.claude/settings.json`
150	     hook (brief 003).
151	4. Starts a **named tmux session** inside the container with the Claude
152	   Code agent as the foreground process.
153	5. Returns to the host with the container running in the background, the
154	   tmux session active.
155	
156	The command is **scriptable**: machine-friendly arguments, no
157	interactive prompts, deterministic exit codes. It is called once per
158	BC per launch, not once per session (containers are stopped and
159	restarted; the init sequence re-runs on each fresh container start).
160	
161	The command is **idempotent on a running container**: if a container
162	for the named BC is already running, the command reports its state
163	rather than starting a second container.
164	
165	### B — Attachment and injection surface
166	
167	The surface for human and automated interaction with a running BC
168	container:
169	
170	- **Attach:** a command that connects a terminal to the named tmux
171	  session inside the container, giving full interactive access to the
172	  agent session.
173	- **Inject:** a command that sends a string of text (a startup prompt
174	  or a follow-up prompt) to the tmux session's active pane without
175	  requiring a human to attach. The injected text lands as if the human
176	  typed it.
177	- **Monitor:** a command that streams the tmux session's output to
178	  stdout on the host, without attaching interactively, so an automated
179	  driver or a human can observe without interfering.
180	
181	All three are separate operations (separate flags or subcommands) that
182	target an already-running container by BC name.
183	
184	### C — Mailbox volume sharing
185	
186	The `shop-msg` mailbox surface (inbox and outbox) used by the BC inside
187	the container must be reachable by the host-side `shop-msg` CLI (used
188	by the lead shop to drop messages in and read responses out). The
189	committed property:
190	
191	- The lead shop can call `shop-msg send ... --bc-root <path>` and have
192	  the resulting inbox YAML appear inside the container at the path
193	  the BC agent expects.
194	- The BC agent can call `shop-msg respond ...` and have the resulting
195	  outbox YAML appear at the path the lead shop reads when calling
196	  `shop-msg read outbox`.
197	
198	**Realization is the Architect's call.** A named Docker volume shared
199	between host and container is the obvious shape; a bind-mounted host
200	directory (`repos/<bc>/`) is another. The brief commits the property
201	(reachable from both sides); the storage mechanism is pre-state-
202	determined.
203	
204	### D — Container lifecycle commands
205	
206	Alongside `launch`, the surface includes:
207	
208	- **Stop:** stop a named BC's container cleanly (flush any in-flight
209	  tmux activity, then `docker stop`).
210	- **Status:** report the running/stopped state of a named BC's
211	  container, including the tmux session state (active, exited, no
212	  session).
213	- **List:** report all BC containers this command surface knows about,
214	  with their states.
215	
216	These are operational hygiene: the lead shop (or a human operator)
217	needs to know what is running without calling `docker ps` and
218	interpreting raw output.
219	
220	---
221	
222	## Out of scope — named explicitly
223	
224	**Agent session persistence across container restarts.** When a
225	container is stopped and restarted, the Claude Code session starts
226	fresh. No conversation history is preserved across container restarts.
227	This is acceptable: the BC's repository state (committed work) is
228	persisted in the mounted volume; the agent recovers context from `bd`
229	and the repo state, not from the conversation history.
230	
231	**Orchestration of all BCs at once.** The command launches one BC at a
232	time. A product-level bootstrap that starts all N BCs for a product
233	(parallel `bc-container launch` calls) is a follow-on. This brief is
234	the per-BC building block; composition is adjacent future work.
235	
236	**Building the devcontainer image.** The image is built and published by
237	`shopsystem-devcontainer` (briefs/features already in flight). This
238	brief consumes that image; it does not extend its build pipeline.
239	
240	**Network isolation between containers.** Containers on the same Docker
241	bridge network can reach each other. This brief does not commit to
242	network-level isolation between BC containers — the behavioral isolation
243	(no shared mounts) is the committed property. Network-level isolation
244	(custom bridge-per-BC or `--network none`) is a follow-on if the
245	invariant needs strengthening.
246	
247	**Lead shop containerization.** The lead shop runs on the host (or in
248	its own existing devcontainer). This brief is BC-container-only.
249	
250	---
251	
252	## Sequencing
253	
254	- **Scope item A** requires the devcontainer image to be built and
255	  accessible (`shopsystem-devcontainer` work in flight). Scenarios for
256	  A should not be dispatched until the devcontainer BC's image-build
257	  and publish work is closed and an image tag is available to pull.
258	- **Scope item B** (attach/inject/monitor) is a pure addition to the
259	  `bc-container` surface; it depends on A landing first but not on any
260	  other brief.
261	- **Scope item C** (mailbox volume sharing) is the key cross-brief
262	  dependency: brief 001's invariant 1 (shop-msg as sole messaging
263	  surface) constrains how the Architect implements the sharing. The
264	  mechanism must not require direct filesystem inspection on either
265	  side; it must be expressed through `shop-msg` CLI calls.
266	- **Scope item D** (lifecycle commands) is independent of B and C; it
267	  depends only on A (a container must exist to stop/inspect).
268	
269	## Vehicle hints (Architect's call)
270	
271	- The `bc-container launch` command is **net-new capability**. No
272	  existing BC owns it today (PDR-004 resolves ownership). Net-new
273	  capability points to `assign_scenarios`.
274	- If the Architect determines the command lives in an existing BC
275	  (e.g., `shopsystem-templates` or `shopsystem-devcontainer`), the
276	  vehicle may remain `assign_scenarios` for the new subcommand surface
277	  even if the BC already exists.
278	- Scope item C (mailbox volume sharing) depends on what `shop-msg`
279	  already supports. If `shop-msg` can already direct its mailbox root
280	  to an arbitrary path via a flag, C may be zero-BC-work (just a
281	  mount and a flag). If not, C lands as `assign_scenarios` against
282	  `shopsystem-messaging`.
283	
284	These are hints. The Architect's `PRE-STATE DETERMINES VEHICLE —
285	VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY` posture stands.
286	
287	## Grounding artifacts
288	
289	- [brief 001](001-inter-shop-messaging-encapsulation.md) — invariant 1
290	  (shop-msg as sole surface); scope item C must not violate it.
291	- [brief 002](002-shop-bootstrap-cli-surface.md) — the per-shop bootstrap
292	  sequence (clone, `bd init`, managed files) that the container init
293	  sequence replicates inside Docker.
294	- [brief 003](003-event-driven-shop-activation.md) — the Monitor-arm
295	  hook that the container init sequence must also activate.
296	- [features/devcontainer/](../features/devcontainer/) — scenarios
297	  pinning the devcontainer image that `bc-container launch` consumes.
298	- [PDR-004](../pdr/004-bc-container-command-ownership.md) — decision on
299	  which BC owns the `bc-container` command surface.
300	
301	## What this leaves open
302	
303	The brief commits **intent**, not scenarios. Scenarios come after the
304	Architect verifies BC pre-state and picks vehicles per the discriminator.
305	
306	- **Exact command name and flag shape for A.** Whether the surface is
307	  `bc-container launch <bc-name>`, `shop-bc start <bc-name>`,
308	  `shop-templates bc-launch`, or something else is the Architect's call
309	  after PDR-004 resolves ownership and pre-state is verified.
310	- **Mailbox volume mechanism for C.** Bind-mount vs. named volume vs.
311	  something else; the brief commits the property, not the mechanism.
312	- **tmux session naming convention.** The brief commits tmux-as-manager;
313	  the Architect picks the session name (e.g., `bc-<name>-agent` or
314	  `agent`) in the dispatched scenarios.
315	- **Which BC owns the command (PDR-004).** Until that PDR resolves, the
316	  scenarios cannot be assigned. The PO flags PDR-004 as the blocker
317	  for Architect BC decomposition.
318
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 18:17:35Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 18:17:36Z)**_

The brief has multiple references to filesystem-based mailboxes (`inbox/`, `outbox/` YAML, `--bc-root <path>` as a directory mount, "mailbox volume sharing"). Let me look at what the PO was reading from.

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 18:17:37Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./briefs/001-inter-shop-messaging-encapsulation.md`

```text
1	# Brief 001 — Inter-shop messaging encapsulation
2	
3	**Status:** draft, intent gaps resolved (2026-05-12), item C scope
4	corrected to empirical reality (2026-05-12)
5	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
6	**Beads:** [`lead-231`](#) (this brief)
7	**Anchored to:** mechanism observation
8	[`shopsystem-templates-kin`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/outbox/shopsystem-templates-kin-mechanism_observation.yaml)
9	emitted by the shopsystem-templates BC during lead-kq0, sharpened in
10	conversation 2026-05-12, three intent gaps resolved by user-driver
11	2026-05-12 (tests carve-out from invariant 1; spec stays conceptual
12	with no filename conventions; catalog audit covers all currently-
13	implemented message types — `request_scenario_register` and
14	`request_shop_card` deferred per
15	[`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 1](../findings/from-prototype-1.md),
16	with the decoupling invariant carrying forward to their eventual
17	authoring).
18	
19	## Point of intent
20	
21	Inter-shop messaging exists as a **bounded surface** that other parts of
22	the shop-system compose with, not a directory layout that other parts of
23	the shop-system inspect. Today, templates and subagents reach past the
24	surface and read `inbox/` and `outbox/` directly because the surface
25	does not yet expose the operations they need. The cost is that
26	`shop-msg`'s storage layout — a filesystem convention that should be a
27	private implementation detail of the messaging BC — has become a public
28	shape that every consumer depends on.
29	
30	The brief commits the shop-system to two invariants and a corollary,
31	and names the five scope items the invariants imply.
32	
33	## The two invariants
34	
35	### Invariant 1 — `shop-msg` is the sole inter-shop messaging surface
36	
37	No consumer (template, subagent, runbook, ad-hoc script) inspects
38	messaging storage directly. The storage representation (filesystem YAML
39	today; SQLite, a daemon, or a queue tomorrow) is a **private
40	implementation detail of the messaging BC**.
41	
42	**Carve-out for messaging-BC tests.** Tests of `shop-msg`'s own
43	implementation may freely inspect messaging storage — they are the
44	messaging BC, not inter-shop consumers. Tests of BCs that consume
45	`shop-msg` (and any non-test consumer of any kind) must use the CLI for
46	setup and assertion like any other consumer.
47	
48	This is the messaging-surface application of
49	[`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 6](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
50	("package CLIs are the integration boundary"). Prototype 1 validated
51	the principle for catalog schemas, scenarios canonicalisation, and the
52	test harness. It applies equally to the messaging surface — and the
53	mechanism observation `shopsystem-templates-kin` is the evidence that
54	it currently does not hold.
55	
56	The observation enumerates the leak:
57	
58	- `ls inbox/ outbox/` to enumerate state.
59	- "A message is unprocessed when there is no outbox file for its
60	  `work_id`" — set-difference on filenames.
61	- `grep message_type inbox/<work_id>.yaml` to decide which subagent to
62	  dispatch.
63	
64	These appear in the canonical templates this BC ships
65	(`bc-implementer.md`, `bc-reviewer.md`, the BC-router section of
66	`repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md`). Because those files are
67	`shopsystem-templates`'s **product**, the leak ships to every
68	downstream BC that adopts them.
69	
70	### Invariant 2 — Inter-shop messages are self-contained
71	
72	The receiving shop acts on a message **without consulting the sending
73	shop's internal artifacts** (beads, source files, working directories).
74	A message carries everything its recipient needs to decide what to do.
75	The local-clone topology currently hides violations of this — every
76	shop's repo is reachable on the same filesystem, so an implementer that
77	reads sender-side state happens to succeed. Remote-shop topology will
78	not be so forgiving.
79	
80	### Corollary — `shop-msg` and `bd` are decoupled
81	
82	Catalog schemas must not require participation in `bd`. Template
83	language must not instruct creating beads as a precondition for
84	messaging. `bd` is each shop's local work-tracker; messaging is the
85	inter-shop surface; the two may compose but **neither requires the
86	other**.
87	
88	The originating observation itself shows the current coupling:
89	`MechanismObservation.bd_ref: shopsystem-templates-kin` is a required
90	field, and the bc-implementer template instructs the BC to "create a
91	beads issue capturing what you observed" before emitting the message.
92	A shop that does not use `bd` cannot emit a `mechanism_observation`
93	today — even though `bd` participation is **its own concern**, not the
94	inter-shop concern.
95	
96	## Five scope items
97	
98	The brief commits intent on five items. Each is named here in product
99	terms; vehicle selection and implementation specifics are the
100	Architect's call after BC pre-state verification.
101	
102	### A — `shop-msg` CLI surface (symmetric, both BC and lead sides)
103	
104	`shop-msg` exposes the operations every consumer needs so that no
105	consumer falls back on `ls`/`cat`/`grep` against the mailbox
106	directories. At minimum:
107	
108	- **Enumerate pending unprocessed work.** A consumer (a BC-shop router,
109	  the lead's drain operation, a runbook) can ask "is there an
110	  unprocessed message here, and what is its `message_type`?" without
111	  inspecting the filesystem. `--bc <name>` filtering is part of the
112	  contract from day one — the lead side will need it.
113	- **Read inbox by `work_id`.** Parallel to the existing
114	  `shop-msg read outbox <work_id>`. Today the templates instruct
115	  reading inbox YAML directly because no CLI operation exists.
116	- **Parity check.** No operation a consumer needs (whether template,
117	  subagent, runbook, or ad-hoc script) currently requires direct file
118	  access. The Architect closes this by enumerating the call sites
119	  during pre-state verification.
120	
121	The contract is **symmetric** between BC side and lead side. The lead
122	shop never touches BC outboxes directly any more than a BC-shop touches
123	its own inbox directly. Both sides use the same surface.
124	
125	### B — Templates rewrite
126	
127	The canonical templates at
128	`repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-implementer.md`
129	and `bc-reviewer.md`, together with the BC-router section of
130	`repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md`, **use the `shop-msg` CLI only**
131	— no direct references to `inbox/` or `outbox/` directories or to
132	their filename conventions, except in conceptual material that
133	explicitly describes the protocol at the spec level.
134	
135	The same discipline applies to the lead-side templates
136	(`lead-po.md`, `lead-architect.md` in the same templates directory)
137	and to the lead-shop `CLAUDE.md`. The point of intent is uniform across
138	roles: **no role enacts the protocol by inspecting messaging storage.**
139	
140	### C — Catalog schema decoupling
141	
142	The catalog schemas under `shop-msg`'s `catalog` package are audited
143	in full across the **six currently-implemented message types**
144	(`assign_scenarios`, `request_bugfix`, `request_maintenance`,
145	`clarify`, `work_done`, `mechanism_observation`) — for required
146	references to `bd`. `MechanismObservation.bd_ref` is the known
147	instance, but the audit does not presuppose the other five are clean:
148	full coverage of what exists is cheap and catches anything that has
149	slipped in unnoticed.
150	
151	`request_scenario_register` and `request_shop_card` are **deferred**
152	per [`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 1](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
153	— no Pydantic schema for either lives in `catalog/schemas.py` today,
154	and no open lead-shop bead currently schedules authoring them (the
155	prototype-1 issues `6mk` and `r7u` closed as deferred 2026-05-10).
156	Authoring of `request_scenario_register`'s schema will naturally ride
157	with [`lead-otu`](#) (the per-BC scenario register surface), since
158	that message type depends on what a register IS. `request_shop_card`
159	awaits its own driver.
160	
161	The decoupling invariant **carries forward**: when those two schemas
162	are eventually authored, they honor the invariant from the start
163	alongside whatever functional schema design they require. The brief
164	commits the invariant; the future work commits the schemas.
165	
166	The committed outcome (for the six in-scope today): `bd` references
167	are either **optional** or **removed**. If retained, they are framed
168	strictly as **provenance metadata** that the producer happens to
169	carry, never as **required participation** in `bd`. The schema makes
170	it possible for a shop that does not use `bd` to emit any message
171	type the catalog defines.
172	
173	### D — Template-language decoupling
174	
175	The template language is audited in parallel — particularly the
176	`bc-implementer.md` "Surfacing mechanism observations" section, which
177	today instructs:
178	
179	> Create a beads issue capturing what you observed: `bd create --title …`
180	>
181	> Emit the wire message: `shop-msg respond mechanism_observation
182	> --bd-ref <bead id from step 1> …`
183	
184	This is rewritten so that **`bd` participation is an independent
185	local-tracker concern, never a precondition for messaging**. If a shop
186	uses `bd`, the template may describe how the two compose — but the
187	ordering "bead first, then message" is removed, and the message is
188	emit-able regardless of whether a bead exists.
189	
190	The audit covers all four canonical templates
191	(`lead-po.md`, `lead-architect.md`, `bc-implementer.md`,
192	`bc-reviewer.md`) and both `CLAUDE.md` files (lead-shop and the
193	shopsystem-templates BC).
194	
195	### E — Spec edits (§4 and §5)
196	
197	[`§4`](../04-bc-shop.md) is amended to describe messaging in **purely
198	conceptual terms**: *what flows* (inbox messages from lead, outbox
199	responses from BC) and *what consumers do via the CLI*. The spec
200	proper **does not name any filename pattern, directory layout, or
201	on-disk convention**. Storage shape — including any filename
202	convention like `<work_id>-<response_type>.yaml` — is `shop-msg`'s
203	internal realization, not part of the inter-shop contract. The
204	Architect may retain examples of the current realization in
205	implementation notes inside the messaging BC, but those examples do
206	not enter the §4 spec text.
207	
208	[`§5`](../05-inter-shop-protocol.md) is amended to name the
209	**self-contained-messages invariant** explicitly. §5.6 already commits
210	the schema-as-contract principle; the self-contained-messages claim is
211	a sibling and belongs alongside it (or in a new §5.7). §5 is held to
212	the same discipline as §4: it characterises the protocol, never the
213	storage.
214	
215	The Architect directs implementation specifics for both edits; the
216	brief commits the **intent** that the spec stops describing storage
217	shape and starts describing protocol semantics. The principle is the
218	same one [`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 4](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
219	applies to invariants: claims belong where they are enforceable;
220	conventions that are not part of the contract are noise that invites
221	slippery-slope dependency.
222	
223	## Out of scope — named explicitly
224	
225	**Auto-loading messages into `bd` (drain automation).** The decoupling
226	holds in both directions: messaging does not require `bd`, and `bd`
227	does not need to be fed automatically by messaging. If a `shop-msg
228	drain` command (or similar) is wanted later, a follow-on brief opens
229	when manual-drain friction surfaces in real-product BC use. This is
230	adjacent to
231	[`findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md §6`](../findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md)
232	("lead drain formalization") — that item stays **deferred for now**.
233	
234	## Sequencing
235	
236	- **Messaging half (A + C) and spec edits (E)** can be authored and
237	  dispatched **independently** of templates work. They target
238	  shopsystem-messaging and the lead-shop spec respectively; neither
239	  collides with lead-kq0.
240	
241	- **Templates half (B + D)** **waits on lead-kq0
242	  ([PDR-001](../pdr/001-role-templates-role-complete.md))
243	  closing.** B and D rewrite the same template surface lead-kq0 is
244	  currently restructuring. Collision risk is high otherwise:
245	  lead-kq0's restructure changes the **shape** of the templates; B+D
246	  changes their **content** with respect to messaging storage. The two
247	  changes interleave badly if dispatched in parallel.
248	
249	The ordering is a sequencing constraint on dispatch, not on authoring.
250	The Architect may verify pre-state and pick vehicles for B+D before
251	lead-kq0 closes, but the `assign_scenarios` / `request_bugfix`
252	dispatches for B+D should not land in the templates BC's inbox until
253	lead-kq0's `work_done` does.
254	
255	## Vehicle hints (Architect's call)
256	
257	For the Architect's awareness during pre-state verification — not as a
258	prejudgment of the discriminator:
259	
260	- **A** likely lands as `assign_scenarios` against shopsystem-messaging
261	  (new capability: the read-side and enumeration commands do not exist
262	  in `shop-msg` today, per `shop-msg --help`).
263	- **C** likely lands as `request_bugfix` against shopsystem-messaging
264	  (tightening existing schemas: catalog schemas exist and carry the
265	  coupling; the change is to remove or relax the coupling, not to add
266	  new schema infrastructure).
267	- **B + D** likely land as `request_bugfix` against
268	  shopsystem-templates (tightening existing templates: the templates
269	  exist; the change is to replace their direct-mailbox references with
270	  CLI references and to remove "bead first, then message" ordering).
271	- **E** likely lands as `request_maintenance` against the lead-shop
272	  spec docs (flat content change in `04-bc-shop.md` and
273	  `05-inter-shop-protocol.md` — no new behavior, no scenario
274	  movement).
275	
276	These are hints. The Architect's `PRE-STATE DETERMINES VEHICLE —
277	VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY` posture stands.
278	
279	## Grounding artifacts
280	
281	- [`repos/shopsystem-templates/outbox/shopsystem-templates-kin-mechanism_observation.yaml`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/outbox/shopsystem-templates-kin-mechanism_observation.yaml)
282	  — the originating BC observation that started this brief.
283	- [`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 6](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
284	  — the validated principle ("package CLIs are the integration
285	  boundary") this brief extends to the messaging surface.
286	- [`findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md` §6](../findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md)
287	  — drain formalization, **referenced as deferred** to make the
288	  out-of-scope boundary explicit.
289	- [`repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md)
290	  — current BC inbox/outbox protocol section; shows the current
291	  coupling.
292	- [`repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-implementer.md`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-implementer.md)
293	  and
294	  [`bc-reviewer.md`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-reviewer.md)
295	  — canonical templates whose language scope B and D rewrite.
296	- [`CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md), [`§4`](../04-bc-shop.md),
297	  [`§5`](../05-inter-shop-protocol.md) — spec-edit targets for scope E.
298	
299	## What this leaves open
300	
301	The brief commits **intent**, not scenarios. Scenarios come after the
302	Architect verifies BC pre-state and picks vehicles per the
303	discriminator. Specifically:
304	
305	- **Exact CLI command names and flag shapes for A.** "Enumerate
306	  pending" might be `shop-msg pending`, `shop-msg list`, or
307	  `shop-msg next`; "read inbox" is most plausibly
308	  `shop-msg read inbox <work_id>` for parity with `read outbox`, but
309	  the Architect names the shapes in the dispatched scenarios.
310	- **Whether `bd_ref` becomes optional, is removed entirely, or is
311	  renamed to a neutral `provenance_ref`.** All three are consistent
312	  with the invariant; pre-state verification on the catalog schemas
313	  picks one.
314	- **Whether E is one `request_maintenance` or two.** §4 and §5 edits
315	  are independent in principle; the Architect may bundle or split.
316	
317	These are vehicle-level questions, not intent-level. The PO's commit
318	is the two invariants + the five scope items + the sequencing
319	constraint.
320
```

```text
12:Messages are YAML, validated by Pydantic schemas. YAML is chosen because it is the easiest to parse and maintain, is human-editable, validates cleanly, and fits the existing Markdown / Gherkin / Structurizr DSL stack.
37:Each shop's card lives at a known path in its repo (e.g. `shop-card.yaml`) — that's where it is maintained. The lead shop acquires BC-shop cards via `request_shop_card`; the BC responds with the card content. Keeping acquisition on the typed channel preserves the uniform inter-shop traffic discipline (no shop reads another shop's repo directly).
49:A message must carry everything the receiving shop needs to act on it. The receiving shop acts on a message without consulting the sending shop's internal artifacts — its beads database, its source files, its working directory, or any other state private to the sender. The schema captures the meaning; the wire payload captures the data. Anything load-bearing for the receiver belongs in the message itself.
51:This is a sibling invariant to the schema-as-contract principle in §5.6. Schema-level invariants ensure each message is well-formed; the self-contained-messages invariant ensures each message is *complete*. Together they keep the inter-shop boundary clean: a shop reasons about what arrives in its inbox, not about how the sender constructed it. Cross-shop read coupling — receiver inspecting sender's repo to interpret a message — defeats the typed-channel discipline §5.1 and §5.4 establish and tightens the topology to single-host single-process by accident.
```

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 18:19:30Z)**_

Clear picture now. Here's the diagnosis:

**The PO was grounding in brief 001** — specifically this line (brief 001:39):

> *"The storage representation (filesystem YAML today; SQLite, a daemon, or a queue tomorrow) is a private implementation detail of the messaging BC."*

The PO read `"filesystem YAML today"` as current truth, not as a historical label. Brief 001 also describes the problem it was solving in terms of `ls inbox/ outbox/`, "inbox YAML directly," and "mailbox directories" — language that bleeds into the PO's scope item C framing in brief 004 ("mailbox directories (`inbox/`, `outbox/`)", "inbox YAML appear inside the container", "Mailbox volume sharing" as the section title).

The `--bc-root <path>` flag visible in the lead-shop CLAUDE.md reinforced the filesystem model — the PO read it as a directory path that would need mounting.

**This is a documentation inconsistency, not an insufficient PO prompt.** The PO did the right things: deferred realization to the Architect ("Realization is the Architect's call"), and even listed "a port-forwarded daemon, or something else" as options — meaning the PO was not fully locked into the filesystem model. But brief 001's "filesystem YAML today" language is now stale, and the PO had no signal that "today" had ended.

**What needs fixing:**

1. **Brief 001** — the "filesystem YAML today" parenthetical in invariant 1 needs a status note reflecting that the migration to PostgreSQL has occurred (the parenthetical was a forward-look, not a permanent label).

2. **Brief 004 scope item C** — "Mailbox volume sharing" title and the open question framing around "mailbox directories (`inbox/`, `outbox/`)" and "inbox YAML" need to be reframed. The committed *property* (accessible from both sides via `shop-msg` CLI) is still correct in spirit; the mechanism framing needs to drop the filesystem assumptions and instead note that the BC agent in the container needs access to the same PostgreSQL database the host's `shop-msg` uses.

Want me to dispatch lead-po to amend both briefs, or do you want to discuss the PostgreSQL connectivity model first so the amendments are grounded correctly?

---

```text
Everything looks coherent. Here is my report.

---

## Report

### Brief 001 — what changed

**Invariant 1, parenthetical** (exact old → new):

Old:
> "The storage representation (filesystem YAML today; SQLite, a daemon, or a queue tomorrow) is a **private implementation detail of the messaging BC**."

New:
> "The storage representation (PostgreSQL — migrated from the earlier filesystem-YAML prototype; a different backend tomorrow) is a **private implementation detail of the messaging BC**. The parenthetical is a completed-migration note, not a forward-look: shop-msg already uses PostgreSQL."

The invariant's meaning (storage is private; consumers use the CLI) is unchanged.

---

### Brief 004 — what changed, section by section

**Interview notes / open questions block:** Replaced the three filesystem-model questions ("Shared vs. private shop-msg mailbox surface", "Mailbox seeding") with three network-model questions: Docker network name, DB connection env var, message ordering across container restarts. Retained Image tag pinning and Command ownership.

**The invariant:** Removed "its shop-msg mailbox volume" from the mount list. Added that communication goes through the shared Docker network connecting both sides to the same PostgreSQL backend.

**Scope item C:** Renamed "Mailbox volume sharing" → "Shared network connectivity". Rewrote the committed property: both sides use the same PostgreSQL instance via a named Docker network; BC container configures via env var (e.g. `SHOP_MSG_DB_URL`); filesystem mounts are explicitly excluded.

**Sequencing note for C:** Replaced volume-sharing / filesystem framing with the network model. Noted that brief 001 invariant 1 still applies (both sides use the CLI, not direct DB access).

**Vehicle hint for C:** Replaced "just a mount and a flag" with "if `shop-msg` already supports a DB URL env var, C may be zero-BC-work; if not, `assign_scenarios` against shopsystem-messaging."

**"What this leaves open":** Replaced single "Mailbox volume mechanism for C" item with two items: Docker network name for C, and DB connection env var for C.

---

### Scenario files amended

**File 13 (`13-mailbox-readable-from-host.gherkin`)**

Before key steps:
- `And a Docker container … is running with its mailbox volume mounted`
- `When I run shop-msg send … with bc-root pointing to the container's mailbox mount path`

After:
- `And a Docker container … is running on the shared Docker network`
- `And the container has SHOP_MSG_DB_URL set to the shared PostgreSQL instance`
- `When I run shop-msg send … targeting the "shopsystem-messaging" BC` (no `--bc-root` path)
- Then: `shop-msg pending inside the container reports work-id "lead-500" as pending`

**File 14 (`14-mailbox-response-readable-from-host.gherkin`)**

Before key steps:
- `And a Docker container … is running with its mailbox volume mounted`
- `And an inbox message … exists inside the container`
- `Then running shop-msg read outbox on the host with bc-root pointing to the container's mailbox mount path`

After:
- `And a Docker container … is running on the shared Docker network`
- `And the container has SHOP_MSG_DB_URL set to the shared PostgreSQL instance`
- `And an inbox message … exists in the shared PostgreSQL backend`
- `Then running shop-msg read outbox on the host with work-id "lead-500" exits zero` (no `--bc-root` filesystem path)

Both scenarios pass the sufficiency check: concrete Given/When/Then, measurable Then assertions, no filesystem-mount mechanism pinned, one behavior per scenario.

---

### Conditions not yet satisfiable — Architect call needed

The scenarios use `SHOP_MSG_DB_URL` as a placeholder env var name and `"shopsystem"` as an example network name. **The Architect must verify `shop-msg`'s pre-state** to confirm (a) the exact env var the CLI honors for its PostgreSQL connection, and (b) the well-known network name to adopt. Once those are confirmed, the placeholder names in scenarios 13 and 14 should be updated to the exact values before dispatch.

result: Brief 001 invariant 1 updated from "filesystem YAML today" to PostgreSQL-completed-migration language; brief 004 scope item C renamed from "Mailbox volume sharing" to "Shared network connectivity" with Docker-network/PostgreSQL model throughout; scenarios 13 and 14 rewritten from filesystem-mount framing to shared-network/env-var framing, all passing PO sufficiency check.
```

_**Agent - sidechain (2026-05-20 18:23:00Z)**_

You are the lead-po subagent for the shopsystem-product lead shop. Read your full role definition at /workspaces/shopsystem-product/.claude/agents/lead-po.md before doing anything.

## Stakeholder clarification to apply

The stakeholder has provided the following clarification on how BC containers communicate with shop-msg's PostgreSQL backend:

> "The connectivity should require that all of the related containers are on the same docker network and should use a well-known name."

This closes the open question in brief 004 scope item C about how the BC agent inside a container reaches the shop-msg database. The answer is: a shared Docker network with a well-known name (e.g. `shopsystem` or similar — the exact name is the Architect's call; the PO commits that there IS a well-known name, not that it is specific value).

## Context: what was wrong

Brief 001 (/workspaces/shopsystem-product/briefs/001-inter-shop-messaging-encapsulation.md) contains stale language. In invariant 1, the parenthetical reads:

> "The storage representation (filesystem YAML today; SQLite, a daemon, or a queue tomorrow) is a private implementation detail of the messaging BC."

"filesystem YAML today" is no longer true — shop-msg now uses PostgreSQL. This language should be updated to reflect the current backend while preserving the invariant's meaning (storage is private; consumers use the CLI).

Brief 004 (/workspaces/shopsystem-product/briefs/004-bc-container-isolation.md) contains confusing scope item C ("Mailbox volume sharing") which uses filesystem terminology (inbox/outbox directories, inbox YAML, mailbox volume sharing, --bc-root as a mount path). The stakeholder's clarification replaces the volume-sharing model entirely: containers communicate with shop-msg via a shared Docker network, not via mounted directories.

## Your task

### 1. Amend brief 001

In invariant 1, update the parenthetical that says "filesystem YAML today; SQLite, a daemon, or a queue tomorrow" to reflect that shop-msg has migrated to PostgreSQL. The invariant itself (storage is private; consumers use the CLI) is unchanged and correct. Only the "today" description needs updating. Add a note that the migration has occurred so future readers understand the parenthetical is not a forward-look but a completed migration.

### 2. Amend brief 004

Make the following changes:

**a. Rename and reframe scope item C.** "Mailbox volume sharing" is the wrong concept now. Rename to something like "Shared network connectivity" or "shop-msg network access". The committed property is: a BC agent running inside a container can reach the same shop-msg PostgreSQL instance as the host-side shop-msg CLI, because both are on the same Docker network with a well-known name.

**b. Rewrite the open question about mailbox surface.** The question "Shared vs. private shop-msg mailbox surface" and its sub-items about "mailbox directories (inbox/, outbox/)", "inbox YAML", "named volumes", "bind-mounted host directory", "port-forwarded daemon" — all of these reflect the old filesystem model. Replace with the stakeholder's decision: shared Docker network with a well-known name. The Architect still determines the exact network name; the property (same network, well-known name) is now committed by the PO.

**c. Remove or correct the sequencing note for scope C** if it references brief 001's invariant 1 in a way that implies filesystem constraints. The invariant 1 constraint (use shop-msg CLI, not direct storage access) still applies — that's unchanged. Only the "how shop-msg stores data" model changes.

**d. Update the vehicle hint for scope C.** Currently it says "C may be zero-BC-work (just a mount and a flag)." With the Docker network model, the vehicle hint should note that C may be zero-BC-work if shop-msg already supports configuring its DB connection via environment variable (which containers on a named network would set). Or it may require a new shop-msg flag/env var. This remains the Architect's pre-state call; update the hint to reflect the network model rather than the mount model.

**e. Update the scenarios** at /workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/bc-launcher/ that pin scope item C. These are scenario files 13 and 14 (mailbox-readable-from-host and mailbox-response-readable-from-host). Read them, then replace the filesystem/volume framing with the Docker network model. The observable behavior is still "lead shop can send a message and the BC agent receives it" — but the mechanism is now network connectivity, not mounted files. Apply your sufficiency check to the amended scenarios.

### Apply your sufficiency check

After amending brief 004 and the scenario files, verify:
- Each amended scenario still has concrete Given/When/Then
- The Then steps assert measurable outcomes
- No scenario pins a mechanism that is now wrong (filesystem mount)
- Scenarios that previously said "inbox YAML appears at path X" should now say something about the BC agent successfully receiving the message via shop-msg CLI

### Report back (under 250 words)
- What you changed in brief 001 (exact old text → new text)
- What you changed in brief 004 (section by section)
- Which scenario files were amended and what the before/after was for the key steps
- Any conditions you could not satisfy (e.g., if the Architect needs to confirm the well-known network name before a step can be made concrete)

Working directory: /workspaces/shopsystem-product
Do not commit — the router handles git.

 `./.claude/agents/lead-po.md`

```text
1	# Lead PO — role prompt
2	
3	You are the **Product Owner** for the lead shop. You are stakeholder-facing and
4	you own product intent. Your job has two faces:
5	
6	1. **Authoring scenarios** — translating expressed desire into Gherkin scenarios
7	   that the Architect can assign to BCs.
8	2. **Responding to BC clarify on scope or vocabulary** — when a BC-shop asks
9	   what something means or whether something is in scope, you are the named
10	   party who answers.
11	
12	You operate inside the lead shop. The procedural CLI mechanics for putting
13	these activities on the wire are deferred to the "CLI mechanics" section
14	near the bottom of this prompt; everything above that section is about
15	what the role *is*, not what command to type.
16	
17	## Your default posture: COMMIT TO SPECIFICS
18	
19	Ambiguity here propagates everywhere. A vague scenario produces vague work; a
20	punted clarify produces silent inference at the BC; a non-answer is the worst
21	outcome because it preserves the appearance of agreement without producing
22	alignment. Your job is to commit to specifics, including when "we'll figure
23	it out later" is tempting. If you genuinely cannot commit yet, that itself
24	is the answer — but say so explicitly rather than leaving the ambiguity
25	implicit.
26	
27	## Your job
28	
29	Your job is the §3.2 PO activity catalogue, made operational. The §3.2 spec
30	catalogues five PO activities. Each is listed below with the
31	one-line guidance that governs it. None of these are placeholders — if a
32	future spec adds an activity for which this template doesn't yet have
33	guidance, mark it explicitly with the literal phrase "guidance pending"
34	rather than leaving the activity as a bare list item.
35	
36	### Interview stakeholder
37	
38	When a stakeholder articulates desire, your job is to extract the specifics
39	behind it — what behavior would satisfy them, what would not, and where the
40	boundaries are. Interview notes are the immediate artifact and the seed for
41	everything downstream; capture them in your own words and keep them under
42	the lead shop so the Architect can read them when shaping BC decomposition.
43	
44	### Maintain product brief
45	
46	The product brief is the authoritative statement of what the product is and
47	what it is for. You own its evolution. When stakeholder intent changes, the
48	brief changes — not as a post-hoc rationalization but as the live record
49	that PDRs and scenarios trace back to.
50	
51	### Write PDR for new functionality
52	
53	A Product Decision Record is the audit trail for an intent commitment that
54	will produce one or more scenarios. Each PDR names the question, the
55	options considered, the decision, and the rationale. Write a PDR when the
56	decision is non-obvious enough that "why" would be re-asked later — not
57	for every scenario, but for every scenario family.
58	
59	### Write Gherkin scenarios as requirements
60	
61	Scenarios are requirements before they are assignments. Author each
62	scenario in plain Gherkin (Given / When / Then) without the
63	`@scenario_hash:` or `@bc:` tags — the Architect computes the hash via
64	`scenarios hash` at assignment time and adds the BC tag based on the
65	scenario-to-BC mapping. Your authoring sufficiency check (below) is what
66	keeps the BC's clarify-default posture from firing on under-specified
67	scenarios.
68	
69	### Respond to BC `clarify` (scope, vocabulary)
70	
71	When a BC-shop emits a `clarify` whose question is about scope ("is X in
72	scope?") or vocabulary ("does word Y mean A or B?"), you are the named
73	party who answers. Architecture clarifies route to the Architect; if the
74	clarify is ambiguous, default to answering and note the routing question
75	in your reply. The clarify-response sufficiency check (below) is what
76	keeps the reply from punting back to the BC or to the Architect.
77	
78	## Sufficiency check — authoring a scenario
79	
80	ALL must be true to produce a scenario the Architect can assign:
81	
82	1. **Well-formed Gherkin.** At least one `Given` (or `Background`), at least
83	   one `When`, at least one `Then`. Step text is clearly delimited.
84	2. **Each step is concrete enough to test.** "the user is happy" is not
85	   concrete; "the response status is 200 and body contains the requested
86	   resource" is. Apply the same vagueness check the BC Implementer
87	   template applies to inbound scenarios — if you would not accept this
88	   from someone else, do not produce it yourself.
89	3. **The Then steps assert measurable outcomes, not constraints.**
90	   "the system doesn't crash" is a constraint, not an outcome. There must
91	   be at least one `Then` that names the *new* behavior in observable terms.
92	4. **The scenario pins one behavior.** If you find yourself writing two
93	   distinct `Scenario:` worth of `Then` steps in one scenario, split it.
94	
95	If any condition fails, the scenario is not ready to leave the PO. Either
96	sharpen it or note explicitly what's blocking sharpening (e.g., the
97	stakeholder hasn't decided yet).
98	
99	## Sufficiency check — responding to a `clarify`
100	
101	ALL must be true:
102	
103	1. **The reply answers the question the BC asked.** If the BC asked "is X
104	   in scope?", your reply contains a yes-or-no to that. If the BC asked
105	   "should the system reject empty input or accept it as a no-op?", your
106	   reply picks one. A response that redescribes the question without
107	   answering it is not an answer.
108	2. **The reply does not punt back to the BC.** "Use your judgment" is not
109	   an answer — the BC Implementer's template tells it not to guess, so
110	   punting creates a loop with no progress. If the answer genuinely is
111	   "either is fine," say that explicitly AND amend the scenario to permit
112	   both, so future Implementers don't re-ask.
113	3. **The reply does not punt to the Architect** when the question is
114	   scope or vocabulary. (Architecture / decomposition / contract questions
115	   DO go to the Architect — that's correct routing. But "what does the
116	   word X mean in scenario Y" is yours.)
117	
118	If a condition fails, do not send the reply. Either sharpen it or escalate
119	explicitly (e.g., to a stakeholder) before responding.
120	
121	## Anti-rationalization
122	
123	When considering whether to commit, watch for these thoughts. Each one
124	preserves ambiguity instead of resolving it:
125	
126	- *"Let the BC figure it out — they're closer to the code."* — STOP. The
127	  lead is the named party for scope and vocabulary. The BC's job is to
128	  build to your specification, not to invent it.
129	- *"It's a minor detail."* — STOP. Minor details are where contracts drift.
130	- *"I don't want to over-constrain."* — STOP. Under-constraint is silent
131	  inference territory. The BC Implementer's anti-rationalization
132	  explicitly tells it not to fill gaps. If you leave a gap, expect a
133	  clarify back — that's the system working. If you don't want the clarify,
134	  close the gap upfront.
135	- *"The PDR already covers this."* — Then quote the PDR in your reply.
136	  If you can't, the PDR doesn't actually cover it; the question is real.
137	- *"I'll decide later if it comes up again."* — STOP. The BC Implementer
138	  has already stopped working and emitted clarify because it came up
139	  THIS time. Later means the same loop, repeated, at higher cost.
140	
141	## Constraints
142	
143	- Gherkin scenarios are produced as plain text (markdown-quoted is fine).
144	- Hash computation is `scenarios hash`'s job, not yours.
145	- Tag application (`@scenario_hash:<hash>`, `@bc:<name>`) happens at
146	  assignment time, performed by the Architect — you author the body.
147	- Clarify responses go via the CLI mechanics below with the same
148	  `--work-id` the BC's clarify carried.
149	- Do not write any inbox or outbox file by hand; use the CLI.
150	
151	## CLI mechanics
152	
153	Outbound communication from the PO to a BC goes through one channel:
154	responding to a `clarify` the BC emitted. The Architect owns the other
155	outbound vehicles (`assign_scenarios`, `request_bugfix`,
156	`request_maintenance`) and is the named operator of those.
157	
158	### Responding to a clarify via the shop-msg CLI
159	
160	1. **Read** the BC's clarify via `shop-msg read outbox --bc-root <BC
161	   root> --work-id <work_id>`. The `shop-msg` CLI is the messaging BC's
162	   boundary; do not bypass it to read outbox storage by other means.
163	2. **Verify the clarify is yours** — scope and vocabulary clarifies route to
164	   PO; architecture clarifies route to Architect. If the clarify is
165	   ambiguous, default to answering and note the routing question in your
166	   reply.
167	3. **Apply the clarify-response sufficiency check** above.
168	4. If sufficient, respond via `shop-msg respond clarify --bc-root <BC root>
169	   --work-id <work_id> --question "<your answer>"`. (The schema field is
170	   called `question` because it carries the clarify text in both directions;
171	   when you respond, your answer goes in that field.)
172	5. **Report** what the BC asked, what you answered, and whether the answer
173	   implies a scenario amendment that the Architect should follow up with
174	   via `request_bugfix`.
175	
176	## Reporting back
177	
178	After authoring a scenario or sending a clarify response, return a short
179	report (under 200 words):
180	
181	- If authoring: the scenario body, the intent driver it traces to, the
182	  target BC (if known), and which sufficiency-check conditions you
183	  verified.
184	- If responding to clarify: the BC's work_id, what the BC asked, your
185	  answer, and whether the answer implies a scenario amendment the
186	  Architect should follow up with.
187
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./briefs/001-inter-shop-messaging-encapsulation.md`

```text
1	# Brief 001 — Inter-shop messaging encapsulation
2	
3	**Status:** draft, intent gaps resolved (2026-05-12), item C scope
4	corrected to empirical reality (2026-05-12)
5	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
6	**Beads:** [`lead-231`](#) (this brief)
7	**Anchored to:** mechanism observation
8	[`shopsystem-templates-kin`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/outbox/shopsystem-templates-kin-mechanism_observation.yaml)
9	emitted by the shopsystem-templates BC during lead-kq0, sharpened in
10	conversation 2026-05-12, three intent gaps resolved by user-driver
11	2026-05-12 (tests carve-out from invariant 1; spec stays conceptual
12	with no filename conventions; catalog audit covers all currently-
13	implemented message types — `request_scenario_register` and
14	`request_shop_card` deferred per
15	[`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 1](../findings/from-prototype-1.md),
16	with the decoupling invariant carrying forward to their eventual
17	authoring).
18	
19	## Point of intent
20	
21	Inter-shop messaging exists as a **bounded surface** that other parts of
22	the shop-system compose with, not a directory layout that other parts of
23	the shop-system inspect. Today, templates and subagents reach past the
24	surface and read `inbox/` and `outbox/` directly because the surface
25	does not yet expose the operations they need. The cost is that
26	`shop-msg`'s storage layout — a filesystem convention that should be a
27	private implementation detail of the messaging BC — has become a public
28	shape that every consumer depends on.
29	
30	The brief commits the shop-system to two invariants and a corollary,
31	and names the five scope items the invariants imply.
32	
33	## The two invariants
34	
35	### Invariant 1 — `shop-msg` is the sole inter-shop messaging surface
36	
37	No consumer (template, subagent, runbook, ad-hoc script) inspects
38	messaging storage directly. The storage representation (filesystem YAML
39	today; SQLite, a daemon, or a queue tomorrow) is a **private
40	implementation detail of the messaging BC**.
41	
42	**Carve-out for messaging-BC tests.** Tests of `shop-msg`'s own
43	implementation may freely inspect messaging storage — they are the
44	messaging BC, not inter-shop consumers. Tests of BCs that consume
45	`shop-msg` (and any non-test consumer of any kind) must use the CLI for
46	setup and assertion like any other consumer.
47	
48	This is the messaging-surface application of
49	[`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 6](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
50	("package CLIs are the integration boundary"). Prototype 1 validated
51	the principle for catalog schemas, scenarios canonicalisation, and the
52	test harness. It applies equally to the messaging surface — and the
53	mechanism observation `shopsystem-templates-kin` is the evidence that
54	it currently does not hold.
55	
56	The observation enumerates the leak:
57	
58	- `ls inbox/ outbox/` to enumerate state.
59	- "A message is unprocessed when there is no outbox file for its
60	  `work_id`" — set-difference on filenames.
61	- `grep message_type inbox/<work_id>.yaml` to decide which subagent to
62	  dispatch.
63	
64	These appear in the canonical templates this BC ships
65	(`bc-implementer.md`, `bc-reviewer.md`, the BC-router section of
66	`repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md`). Because those files are
67	`shopsystem-templates`'s **product**, the leak ships to every
68	downstream BC that adopts them.
69	
70	### Invariant 2 — Inter-shop messages are self-contained
71	
72	The receiving shop acts on a message **without consulting the sending
73	shop's internal artifacts** (beads, source files, working directories).
74	A message carries everything its recipient needs to decide what to do.
75	The local-clone topology currently hides violations of this — every
76	shop's repo is reachable on the same filesystem, so an implementer that
77	reads sender-side state happens to succeed. Remote-shop topology will
78	not be so forgiving.
79	
80	### Corollary — `shop-msg` and `bd` are decoupled
81	
82	Catalog schemas must not require participation in `bd`. Template
83	language must not instruct creating beads as a precondition for
84	messaging. `bd` is each shop's local work-tracker; messaging is the
85	inter-shop surface; the two may compose but **neither requires the
86	other**.
87	
88	The originating observation itself shows the current coupling:
89	`MechanismObservation.bd_ref: shopsystem-templates-kin` is a required
90	field, and the bc-implementer template instructs the BC to "create a
91	beads issue capturing what you observed" before emitting the message.
92	A shop that does not use `bd` cannot emit a `mechanism_observation`
93	today — even though `bd` participation is **its own concern**, not the
94	inter-shop concern.
95	
96	## Five scope items
97	
98	The brief commits intent on five items. Each is named here in product
99	terms; vehicle selection and implementation specifics are the
100	Architect's call after BC pre-state verification.
101	
102	### A — `shop-msg` CLI surface (symmetric, both BC and lead sides)
103	
104	`shop-msg` exposes the operations every consumer needs so that no
105	consumer falls back on `ls`/`cat`/`grep` against the mailbox
106	directories. At minimum:
107	
108	- **Enumerate pending unprocessed work.** A consumer (a BC-shop router,
109	  the lead's drain operation, a runbook) can ask "is there an
110	  unprocessed message here, and what is its `message_type`?" without
111	  inspecting the filesystem. `--bc <name>` filtering is part of the
112	  contract from day one — the lead side will need it.
113	- **Read inbox by `work_id`.** Parallel to the existing
114	  `shop-msg read outbox <work_id>`. Today the templates instruct
115	  reading inbox YAML directly because no CLI operation exists.
116	- **Parity check.** No operation a consumer needs (whether template,
117	  subagent, runbook, or ad-hoc script) currently requires direct file
118	  access. The Architect closes this by enumerating the call sites
119	  during pre-state verification.
120	
121	The contract is **symmetric** between BC side and lead side. The lead
122	shop never touches BC outboxes directly any more than a BC-shop touches
123	its own inbox directly. Both sides use the same surface.
124	
125	### B — Templates rewrite
126	
127	The canonical templates at
128	`repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-implementer.md`
129	and `bc-reviewer.md`, together with the BC-router section of
130	`repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md`, **use the `shop-msg` CLI only**
131	— no direct references to `inbox/` or `outbox/` directories or to
132	their filename conventions, except in conceptual material that
133	explicitly describes the protocol at the spec level.
134	
135	The same discipline applies to the lead-side templates
136	(`lead-po.md`, `lead-architect.md` in the same templates directory)
137	and to the lead-shop `CLAUDE.md`. The point of intent is uniform across
138	roles: **no role enacts the protocol by inspecting messaging storage.**
139	
140	### C — Catalog schema decoupling
141	
142	The catalog schemas under `shop-msg`'s `catalog` package are audited
143	in full across the **six currently-implemented message types**
144	(`assign_scenarios`, `request_bugfix`, `request_maintenance`,
145	`clarify`, `work_done`, `mechanism_observation`) — for required
146	references to `bd`. `MechanismObservation.bd_ref` is the known
147	instance, but the audit does not presuppose the other five are clean:
148	full coverage of what exists is cheap and catches anything that has
149	slipped in unnoticed.
150	
151	`request_scenario_register` and `request_shop_card` are **deferred**
152	per [`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 1](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
153	— no Pydantic schema for either lives in `catalog/schemas.py` today,
154	and no open lead-shop bead currently schedules authoring them (the
155	prototype-1 issues `6mk` and `r7u` closed as deferred 2026-05-10).
156	Authoring of `request_scenario_register`'s schema will naturally ride
157	with [`lead-otu`](#) (the per-BC scenario register surface), since
158	that message type depends on what a register IS. `request_shop_card`
159	awaits its own driver.
160	
161	The decoupling invariant **carries forward**: when those two schemas
162	are eventually authored, they honor the invariant from the start
163	alongside whatever functional schema design they require. The brief
164	commits the invariant; the future work commits the schemas.
165	
166	The committed outcome (for the six in-scope today): `bd` references
167	are either **optional** or **removed**. If retained, they are framed
168	strictly as **provenance metadata** that the producer happens to
169	carry, never as **required participation** in `bd`. The schema makes
170	it possible for a shop that does not use `bd` to emit any message
171	type the catalog defines.
172	
173	### D — Template-language decoupling
174	
175	The template language is audited in parallel — particularly the
176	`bc-implementer.md` "Surfacing mechanism observations" section, which
177	today instructs:
178	
179	> Create a beads issue capturing what you observed: `bd create --title …`
180	>
181	> Emit the wire message: `shop-msg respond mechanism_observation
182	> --bd-ref <bead id from step 1> …`
183	
184	This is rewritten so that **`bd` participation is an independent
185	local-tracker concern, never a precondition for messaging**. If a shop
186	uses `bd`, the template may describe how the two compose — but the
187	ordering "bead first, then message" is removed, and the message is
188	emit-able regardless of whether a bead exists.
189	
190	The audit covers all four canonical templates
191	(`lead-po.md`, `lead-architect.md`, `bc-implementer.md`,
192	`bc-reviewer.md`) and both `CLAUDE.md` files (lead-shop and the
193	shopsystem-templates BC).
194	
195	### E — Spec edits (§4 and §5)
196	
197	[`§4`](../04-bc-shop.md) is amended to describe messaging in **purely
198	conceptual terms**: *what flows* (inbox messages from lead, outbox
199	responses from BC) and *what consumers do via the CLI*. The spec
200	proper **does not name any filename pattern, directory layout, or
201	on-disk convention**. Storage shape — including any filename
202	convention like `<work_id>-<response_type>.yaml` — is `shop-msg`'s
203	internal realization, not part of the inter-shop contract. The
204	Architect may retain examples of the current realization in
205	implementation notes inside the messaging BC, but those examples do
206	not enter the §4 spec text.
207	
208	[`§5`](../05-inter-shop-protocol.md) is amended to name the
209	**self-contained-messages invariant** explicitly. §5.6 already commits
210	the schema-as-contract principle; the self-contained-messages claim is
211	a sibling and belongs alongside it (or in a new §5.7). §5 is held to
212	the same discipline as §4: it characterises the protocol, never the
213	storage.
214	
215	The Architect directs implementation specifics for both edits; the
216	brief commits the **intent** that the spec stops describing storage
217	shape and starts describing protocol semantics. The principle is the
218	same one [`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 4](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
219	applies to invariants: claims belong where they are enforceable;
220	conventions that are not part of the contract are noise that invites
221	slippery-slope dependency.
222	
223	## Out of scope — named explicitly
224	
225	**Auto-loading messages into `bd` (drain automation).** The decoupling
226	holds in both directions: messaging does not require `bd`, and `bd`
227	does not need to be fed automatically by messaging. If a `shop-msg
228	drain` command (or similar) is wanted later, a follow-on brief opens
229	when manual-drain friction surfaces in real-product BC use. This is
230	adjacent to
231	[`findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md §6`](../findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md)
232	("lead drain formalization") — that item stays **deferred for now**.
233	
234	## Sequencing
235	
236	- **Messaging half (A + C) and spec edits (E)** can be authored and
237	  dispatched **independently** of templates work. They target
238	  shopsystem-messaging and the lead-shop spec respectively; neither
239	  collides with lead-kq0.
240	
241	- **Templates half (B + D)** **waits on lead-kq0
242	  ([PDR-001](../pdr/001-role-templates-role-complete.md))
243	  closing.** B and D rewrite the same template surface lead-kq0 is
244	  currently restructuring. Collision risk is high otherwise:
245	  lead-kq0's restructure changes the **shape** of the templates; B+D
246	  changes their **content** with respect to messaging storage. The two
247	  changes interleave badly if dispatched in parallel.
248	
249	The ordering is a sequencing constraint on dispatch, not on authoring.
250	The Architect may verify pre-state and pick vehicles for B+D before
251	lead-kq0 closes, but the `assign_scenarios` / `request_bugfix`
252	dispatches for B+D should not land in the templates BC's inbox until
253	lead-kq0's `work_done` does.
254	
255	## Vehicle hints (Architect's call)
256	
257	For the Architect's awareness during pre-state verification — not as a
258	prejudgment of the discriminator:
259	
260	- **A** likely lands as `assign_scenarios` against shopsystem-messaging
261	  (new capability: the read-side and enumeration commands do not exist
262	  in `shop-msg` today, per `shop-msg --help`).
263	- **C** likely lands as `request_bugfix` against shopsystem-messaging
264	  (tightening existing schemas: catalog schemas exist and carry the
265	  coupling; the change is to remove or relax the coupling, not to add
266	  new schema infrastructure).
267	- **B + D** likely land as `request_bugfix` against
268	  shopsystem-templates (tightening existing templates: the templates
269	  exist; the change is to replace their direct-mailbox references with
270	  CLI references and to remove "bead first, then message" ordering).
271	- **E** likely lands as `request_maintenance` against the lead-shop
272	  spec docs (flat content change in `04-bc-shop.md` and
273	  `05-inter-shop-protocol.md` — no new behavior, no scenario
274	  movement).
275	
276	These are hints. The Architect's `PRE-STATE DETERMINES VEHICLE —
277	VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY` posture stands.
278	
279	## Grounding artifacts
280	
281	- [`repos/shopsystem-templates/outbox/shopsystem-templates-kin-mechanism_observation.yaml`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/outbox/shopsystem-templates-kin-mechanism_observation.yaml)
282	  — the originating BC observation that started this brief.
283	- [`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 6](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
284	  — the validated principle ("package CLIs are the integration
285	  boundary") this brief extends to the messaging surface.
286	- [`findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md` §6](../findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md)
287	  — drain formalization, **referenced as deferred** to make the
288	  out-of-scope boundary explicit.
289	- [`repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md)
290	  — current BC inbox/outbox protocol section; shows the current
291	  coupling.
292	- [`repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-implementer.md`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-implementer.md)
293	  and
294	  [`bc-reviewer.md`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/bc-reviewer.md)
295	  — canonical templates whose language scope B and D rewrite.
296	- [`CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md), [`§4`](../04-bc-shop.md),
297	  [`§5`](../05-inter-shop-protocol.md) — spec-edit targets for scope E.
298	
299	## What this leaves open
300	
301	The brief commits **intent**, not scenarios. Scenarios come after the
302	Architect verifies BC pre-state and picks vehicles per the
303	discriminator. Specifically:
304	
305	- **Exact CLI command names and flag shapes for A.** "Enumerate
306	  pending" might be `shop-msg pending`, `shop-msg list`, or
307	  `shop-msg next`; "read inbox" is most plausibly
308	  `shop-msg read inbox <work_id>` for parity with `read outbox`, but
309	  the Architect names the shapes in the dispatched scenarios.
310	- **Whether `bd_ref` becomes optional, is removed entirely, or is
311	  renamed to a neutral `provenance_ref`.** All three are consistent
312	  with the invariant; pre-state verification on the catalog schemas
313	  picks one.
314	- **Whether E is one `request_maintenance` or two.** §4 and §5 edits
315	  are independent in principle; the Architect may bundle or split.
316	
317	These are vehicle-level questions, not intent-level. The PO's commit
318	is the two invariants + the five scope items + the sequencing
319	constraint.
320
```
</details></tool-use>
