 `./briefs/004-bc-container-isolation.md`

```text
1	# Brief 004 — BC container isolation
2	
3	**Status:** draft (2026-05-20)
4	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
5	**Anchored to:** user-driver observation 2026-05-20:
6	*"BC shops should be able to be started in their own Docker containers.
7	There should be a command that uses Docker and a shop image to start a
8	container, clone a repository, init beads, and start an agent, with the
9	agent running in tmux or something similar so it can be connected to or
10	injected with a startup prompt and also monitored during operation, and
11	also allow direct interaction with the BC. The goal is isolation between
12	the BCs and having work proceed through contracts and not reading code."*
13	
14	---
15	
16	## Interview notes (PO capture)
17	
18	The stakeholder's expressed desire, in PO's own words:
19	
20	**What behavior would satisfy the stakeholder:**
21	
22	- A single command starts a container for one BC shop: pulls/uses a
23	  shop image, clones the BC's repository, initializes beads inside the
24	  container, and starts a Claude Code agent session inside that
25	  container.
26	- The agent session runs under a terminal multiplexer (tmux named) so
27	  that (a) the container keeps running even after the initiating terminal
28	  exits, (b) a human can attach to the session to observe or type, and
29	  (c) an automated driver can inject text into the session (the "startup
30	  prompt" use case).
31	- The container can be directly interacted with — not just observed —
32	  after launch.
33	- Work between BCs proceeds through `shop-msg` contracts, not through
34	  reading each other's source code.
35	
36	**What would NOT satisfy the stakeholder:**
37	
38	- Running all BCs in the same container or on the same host without
39	  process isolation (no container boundary).
40	- An agent that reads the lead shop's source files or another BC's
41	  source files directly; the isolation is not just technical (separate
42	  container) but behavioral (contracts only).
43	- A container that is opaque — no mechanism to attach, observe, or
44	  inject a prompt.
45	
46	**Boundaries the PO commits to:**
47	
48	1. **One container per BC shop.** The unit of isolation is one BC,
49	   not one product. The command is per-BC.
50	2. **The container is started from the shopsystem devcontainer image.**
51	   The devcontainer image (built and published by
52	   `shopsystem-devcontainer`) is the base; `bc-container launch` (or
53	   equivalent) is the command that runs it appropriately.
54	3. **tmux is the session manager.** The agent runs inside a named tmux
55	   session inside the container. Human and automated access go through
56	   tmux (`attach-session` to observe/interact; `send-keys` to inject).
57	4. **The clone-and-init sequence happens at container start.** The
58	   container is ephemeral from the outside; the initialization sequence
59	   (clone, `bd init`, venv sync, arm Monitor) runs on every fresh
60	   container launch.
61	5. **Work isolation is behavioral, not just technical.** A BC agent
62	   in its container has no read access to the lead shop's workspace or
63	   to sibling BC source trees. All inter-shop traffic goes through
64	   `shop-msg`.
65	
66	**Open questions the PO cannot close without stakeholder clarification
67	or Architect pre-state verification:**
68	
69	- **Docker network name.** The stakeholder has committed that the
70	  shared network uses a well-known name. The exact name (e.g.
71	  `shopsystem`) is the Architect's call on pre-state verification.
72	- **DB connection environment variable.** The Architect determines
73	  whether `shop-msg` already honours a `SHOP_MSG_DB_URL` or equivalent
74	  env var for its PostgreSQL connection, or whether that support must
75	  be added as part of scope item C. Pre-state determines vehicle.
76	- **Message ordering across container restarts.** Because shop-msg
77	  persists to PostgreSQL (not a filesystem volume), messages sent by
78	  the lead before a BC container launches are already present in the
79	  DB when the container starts. The PO commits this is the expected
80	  behavior; the Architect verifies it holds.
81	- **Image tag pinning.** The `bc-container launch` command needs to
82	  know which image tag to use. Whether this is always `latest`, a
83	  pinned semver from a config file, or a flag — the PO defers to the
84	  Architect.
85	- **Which repo owns the `bc-container launch` command.** The PO's
86	  stance: this is a new capability whose home is a new BC
87	  (`shopsystem-bc-launcher` or similar), OR it is a new subcommand on
88	  an existing BC's CLI. The Architect's pre-state determines whether
89	  any existing BC (`shopsystem-templates`, `shopsystem-devcontainer`,
90	  or `shopsystem-messaging`) is the natural owner, or whether a new BC
91	  is warranted. PDR-004 names the options and the decision.
92	
93	---
94	
95	## Point of intent
96	
97	The shopsystem today runs all BC agents on the same host, typically in
98	a single devcontainer. Isolation between BCs is **social, not technical**:
99	the spec (§4) says a BC-shop agent must not read sibling source trees,
100	but nothing prevents it. When a BC agent is curious or confused, the
101	most convenient path is the wrong one: reading another BC's code instead
102	of emitting a `clarify`.
103	
104	This brief commits the framework to **container-level isolation per BC
105	shop**: each BC agent runs in its own Docker container, started from the
106	canonical devcontainer image, with an agent session accessible via tmux.
107	The behavioral goal — work proceeds through `shop-msg` contracts, not
108	code reads — becomes enforced at the OS boundary, not just by instruction.
109	
110	The brief carries **one invariant**, **four scope items**, and an
111	**explicit out-of-scope boundary**.
112	
113	---
114	
115	## The invariant
116	
117	### Isolation is technical, not just instructed
118	
119	A BC shop's agent container has no access to the lead shop's working
120	directory or to sibling BC source trees. The host may map the BC's own
121	repository into the container; nothing else from the host workspace is
122	mounted. Any communication between shops goes through `shop-msg` —
123	emitting messages on one side, reading them on the other — via the
124	shared Docker network that connects both sides to the same PostgreSQL
125	backend.
126	
127	This invariant makes the §4 "no cross-BC code reads" constraint
128	**unenforced-by-instruction → enforced-by-mount**. An agent that
129	physically cannot read sibling source is not relying on role discipline
130	alone; the architecture does the work.
131	
132	---
133	
134	## Four scope items
135	
136	### A — `bc-container launch` command
137	
138	A command (exact name the Architect picks after pre-state; herein called
139	`bc-container launch`) takes a BC name (or repo URL + branch) and:
140	
141	1. Pulls or reuses the canonical devcontainer image.
142	2. Starts a container with the BC's repo (or a volume where the repo
143	   will be cloned) mounted as the workspace.
144	3. Inside the container, runs the initialization sequence:
145	   - Clone the BC's repository (if not already present in the workspace
146	     volume).
147	   - Run `bd init` to initialize beads.
148	   - Install the shop's Python packages (`pip install -e .` or
149	     equivalent).
150	   - Arm the Monitor watcher per the canonical `.claude/settings.json`
151	     hook (brief 003).
152	4. Starts a **named tmux session** inside the container with the Claude
153	   Code agent as the foreground process.
154	5. Returns to the host with the container running in the background, the
155	   tmux session active.
156	
157	The command is **scriptable**: machine-friendly arguments, no
158	interactive prompts, deterministic exit codes. It is called once per
159	BC per launch, not once per session (containers are stopped and
160	restarted; the init sequence re-runs on each fresh container start).
161	
162	The command is **idempotent on a running container**: if a container
163	for the named BC is already running, the command reports its state
164	rather than starting a second container.
165	
166	### B — Attachment and injection surface
167	
168	The surface for human and automated interaction with a running BC
169	container:
170	
171	- **Attach:** a command that connects a terminal to the named tmux
172	  session inside the container, giving full interactive access to the
173	  agent session.
174	- **Inject:** a command that sends a string of text (a startup prompt
175	  or a follow-up prompt) to the tmux session's active pane without
176	  requiring a human to attach. The injected text lands as if the human
177	  typed it.
178	- **Monitor:** a command that streams the tmux session's output to
179	  stdout on the host, without attaching interactively, so an automated
180	  driver or a human can observe without interfering.
181	
182	All three are separate operations (separate flags or subcommands) that
183	target an already-running container by BC name.
184	
185	### C — Shared network connectivity
186	
187	The BC agent running inside its container must be able to reach the
188	same `shop-msg` PostgreSQL backend as the host-side `shop-msg` CLI.
189	The committed property:
190	
191	- Both the host-side `shop-msg` CLI (used by the lead shop to drop
192	  messages and read responses) and the BC agent's container-side
193	  `shop-msg` CLI talk to the **same PostgreSQL instance**.
194	- This is achieved by placing the BC container and the host (or its
195	  devcontainer) on a **shared Docker network with a well-known name**.
196	  The exact network name (e.g. `shopsystem`) is the Architect's call;
197	  the property (same network, well-known name) is committed by the PO.
198	- The BC agent configures its database connection via an environment
199	  variable (e.g. `SHOP_MSG_DB_URL` or equivalent) set at container
200	  start; the value points to the PostgreSQL service reachable over the
201	  shared network.
202	
203	**Realization is still the Architect's call** on the exact network
204	name and environment-variable convention. The brief commits the
205	property (both sides reach the same DB via a named Docker network);
206	filesystem mounts and mailbox volume sharing are not part of this
207	scope item.
208	
209	### D — Container lifecycle commands
210	
211	Alongside `launch`, the surface includes:
212	
213	- **Stop:** stop a named BC's container cleanly (flush any in-flight
214	  tmux activity, then `docker stop`).
215	- **Status:** report the running/stopped state of a named BC's
216	  container, including the tmux session state (active, exited, no
217	  session).
218	- **List:** report all BC containers this command surface knows about,
219	  with their states.
220	
221	These are operational hygiene: the lead shop (or a human operator)
222	needs to know what is running without calling `docker ps` and
223	interpreting raw output.
224	
225	---
226	
227	## Out of scope — named explicitly
228	
229	**Agent session persistence across container restarts.** When a
230	container is stopped and restarted, the Claude Code session starts
231	fresh. No conversation history is preserved across container restarts.
232	This is acceptable: the BC's repository state (committed work) is
233	persisted in the mounted volume; the agent recovers context from `bd`
234	and the repo state, not from the conversation history.
235	
236	**Orchestration of all BCs at once.** The command launches one BC at a
237	time. A product-level bootstrap that starts all N BCs for a product
238	(parallel `bc-container launch` calls) is a follow-on. This brief is
239	the per-BC building block; composition is adjacent future work.
240	
241	**Building the devcontainer image.** The image is built and published by
242	`shopsystem-devcontainer` (briefs/features already in flight). This
243	brief consumes that image; it does not extend its build pipeline.
244	
245	**Network isolation between containers.** Containers on the same Docker
246	bridge network can reach each other. This brief does not commit to
247	network-level isolation between BC containers — the behavioral isolation
248	(no shared mounts) is the committed property. Network-level isolation
249	(custom bridge-per-BC or `--network none`) is a follow-on if the
250	invariant needs strengthening.
251	
252	**Lead shop containerization.** The lead shop runs on the host (or in
253	its own existing devcontainer). This brief is BC-container-only.
254	
255	---
256	
257	## Sequencing
258	
259	- **Scope item A** requires the devcontainer image to be built and
260	  accessible (`shopsystem-devcontainer` work in flight). Scenarios for
261	  A should not be dispatched until the devcontainer BC's image-build
262	  and publish work is closed and an image tag is available to pull.
263	- **Scope item B** (attach/inject/monitor) is a pure addition to the
264	  `bc-container` surface; it depends on A landing first but not on any
265	  other brief.
266	- **Scope item C** (shared network connectivity) is the key cross-brief
267	  dependency: brief 001's invariant 1 (shop-msg as sole messaging
268	  surface) still applies — both sides must communicate through the
269	  `shop-msg` CLI, not by direct PostgreSQL access or filesystem
270	  inspection. The Docker-network model satisfies this: both sides call
271	  the same CLI, which connects to the same PostgreSQL backend via the
272	  shared network. No new filesystem-inspection mechanism is introduced.
273	- **Scope item D** (lifecycle commands) is independent of B and C; it
274	  depends only on A (a container must exist to stop/inspect).
275	
276	## Vehicle hints (Architect's call)
277	
278	- The `bc-container launch` command is **net-new capability**. No
279	  existing BC owns it today (PDR-004 resolves ownership). Net-new
280	  capability points to `assign_scenarios`.
281	- If the Architect determines the command lives in an existing BC
282	  (e.g., `shopsystem-templates` or `shopsystem-devcontainer`), the
283	  vehicle may remain `assign_scenarios` for the new subcommand surface
284	  even if the BC already exists.
285	- Scope item C (shared network connectivity) depends on what `shop-msg`
286	  already supports for configuring its PostgreSQL connection. If
287	  `shop-msg` already accepts the DB URL via an environment variable,
288	  C may be zero-BC-work (just network placement and a documented env
289	  var). If `shop-msg` hard-codes its DB connection and needs a new
290	  env-var flag, C lands as `assign_scenarios` against
291	  `shopsystem-messaging`. Pre-state determines vehicle.
292	
293	These are hints. The Architect's `PRE-STATE DETERMINES VEHICLE —
294	VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY` posture stands.
295	
296	## Grounding artifacts
297	
298	- [brief 001](001-inter-shop-messaging-encapsulation.md) — invariant 1
299	  (shop-msg as sole surface); scope item C must not violate it.
300	- [brief 002](002-shop-bootstrap-cli-surface.md) — the per-shop bootstrap
301	  sequence (clone, `bd init`, managed files) that the container init
302	  sequence replicates inside Docker.
303	- [brief 003](003-event-driven-shop-activation.md) — the Monitor-arm
304	  hook that the container init sequence must also activate.
305	- [features/devcontainer/](../features/devcontainer/) — scenarios
306	  pinning the devcontainer image that `bc-container launch` consumes.
307	- [PDR-004](../pdr/004-bc-container-command-ownership.md) — decision on
308	  which BC owns the `bc-container` command surface.
309	
310	## What this leaves open
311	
312	The brief commits **intent**, not scenarios. Scenarios come after the
313	Architect verifies BC pre-state and picks vehicles per the discriminator.
314	
315	- **Exact command name and flag shape for A.** Whether the surface is
316	  `bc-container launch <bc-name>`, `shop-bc start <bc-name>`,
317	  `shop-templates bc-launch`, or something else is the Architect's call
318	  after PDR-004 resolves ownership and pre-state is verified.
319	- **Docker network name for C.** The brief commits that a well-known
320	  Docker network name is used; the Architect picks the name after
321	  pre-state verification.
322	- **DB connection env var for C.** Whether `shop-msg` already supports
323	  a `SHOP_MSG_DB_URL` or equivalent; the Architect verifies and names
324	  the convention in the dispatched scenarios.
325	- **tmux session naming convention.** The brief commits tmux-as-manager;
326	  the Architect picks the session name (e.g., `bc-<name>-agent` or
327	  `agent`) in the dispatched scenarios.
328	- **Which BC owns the command (PDR-004).** Until that PDR resolves, the
329	  scenarios cannot be assigned. The PO flags PDR-004 as the blocker
330	  for Architect BC decomposition.
331
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 21:22:46Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./pdr/005-architect-technical-review-gate.md`

```text
1	# PDR-005 — Architect technical review gate before dispatch
2	
3	**Status:** draft (2026-05-20)
4	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
5	**Anchored to:** two dispatch errors on 2026-05-20:
6	(1) `SHOP_MSG_DB_URL` used in scenarios 13-14 when pre-state verification
7	had already established `SHOPMSG_DSN` as the correct name (source:
8	`storage.py:62`); (2) `SHOPMSG_DSN` described as a "socket or volume"
9	in scenario 15 when it is an environment variable consumed over the
10	Docker network — dispatched without Architect reading the Then step.
11	
12	## The question
13	
14	Should the Architect review PO scenario content for technical accuracy
15	before dispatching, and if so, how? And should the PO be constrained
16	from naming specific technical artifacts in the first place?
17	
18	Both errors shared the same structure: the PO named a technical artifact
19	without consulting the pre-state findings; the Architect dispatched
20	without checking the PO's text against those findings. The question is
21	whether to fix the PO side, the Architect side, or both.
22	
23	## Context
24	
25	The session's error sequence:
26	
27	1. The Architect ran `storage.py:62` empirically and confirmed the env
28	   var name is `SHOPMSG_DSN`.
29	2. The PO authored scenarios 13-14 using `SHOP_MSG_DB_URL` — a name
30	   that was never in the pre-state findings.
31	3. The Architect dispatched scenarios 13-14 without cross-checking the
32	   env var name against the empirical finding.
33	4. The PO authored scenario 15 with a Then step describing `SHOPMSG_DSN`
34	   as a "socket or volume." `SHOPMSG_DSN` is a connection string env
35	   var; the BC reaches PostgreSQL over the Docker network. There is no
36	   socket mount.
37	5. The Architect dispatched scenario 15 without reading the Then step
38	   for technical plausibility.
39	
40	Both errors were caught only after the BC emitted a clarify (scenario
41	15) or after the user noticed (scenarios 13-14). Each cost at least one
42	extra round trip.
43	
44	The errors are symmetric: the PO introduced the wrong technical artifact;
45	the Architect failed to catch it. Fixing only one side of the contract
46	leaves the other side exposed.
47	
48	## Options considered
49	
50	### Option A — Architect pre-dispatch review step
51	
52	Add a step to the Architect's `assign_scenarios` sufficiency check
53	requiring the Architect to read each scenario for steps that make
54	technical claims (env var names, mount types, command flags, protocol
55	mechanics, network topology) and verify each claim against pre-state
56	findings before dispatching.
57	
58	**Pros:**
59	- Catches errors regardless of their origin; a single gate covers PO
60	  errors and the Architect's own analytical errors.
61	- No change to how the PO works. If the Architect's review is fast and
62	  accurate, the PO can write freely.
63	- The Architect is the natural checker: they did the pre-state
64	  verification and hold the findings.
65	
66	**Cons:**
67	- The PO is still free to introduce unverified technical claims.
68	  Reviewing them adds Architect work that would not exist if the PO had
69	  avoided them. A bad PO habit is subsidized by Architect labor.
70	- If the Architect's review misses something (as it did this session),
71	  the error still ships. One gate, one point of failure.
72	- Does not shorten the feedback loop to the point where the error is
73	  first introduced.
74	
75	### Option B — PO constraint: no unverified technical specifics
76	
77	Add a constraint to the PO role template instructing the PO not to name
78	specific technical artifacts (env var names, port numbers, command
79	flags, protocol specifics, mount types) unless the Architect has
80	confirmed them. The PO uses placeholder language; the Architect fills in
81	verified values before dispatch.
82	
83	Placeholder example: "the container receives the database connection
84	string via an environment variable whose name and value are confirmed in
85	pre-state" rather than "the container receives `SHOPMSG_DSN` set to
86	the database URL."
87	
88	**Pros:**
89	- Eliminates the source of the error. A PO who does not name
90	  `SHOP_MSG_DB_URL` cannot introduce a wrong env var name.
91	- Forces the Architect to be the sole source of verified technical
92	  artifact names in final scenario text — consistent with the
93	  Architect's empirical pre-state role.
94	- Shorter feedback loop: the error is caught at the PO authoring step,
95	  not the Architect dispatch step.
96	
97	**Cons:**
98	- Placeholder language in Then steps reduces scenario readability.
99	  Implementers work better with concrete artifact names.
100	- Increases PO→Architect coordination overhead. For every technical
101	  artifact a scenario references, the PO must wait for the Architect to
102	  supply the verified name before authoring is complete.
103	- The PO's sufficiency check already requires "each step is concrete
104	  enough to test." Placeholders conflict with that check unless the
105	  check is carved out for artifact names.
106	
107	### Option C — Both A and B
108	
109	Two-sided fix: the PO avoids unverified technical claims, and the
110	Architect reviews the scenario text against pre-state findings before
111	dispatching. Neither role is solely responsible.
112	
113	**Pros:**
114	- Defense in depth. Two checkpoints catch errors that one misses.
115	- The PO constraint reduces the volume of technical claims that reach
116	  the Architect's review — less to check, less to miss.
117	- Preserves scenario concreteness: the Architect fills in verified
118	  artifact names at the review step, so final scenario text is concrete.
119	- Establishes the correct ownership: the PO owns scope and vocabulary
120	  for the *behavior*; the Architect owns vocabulary for the *technical
121	  artifact* that implements it.
122	
123	**Cons:**
124	- More process. Two roles have new checklist items.
125	- PO→Architect handoff for artifact names adds a coordination step that
126	  did not exist before.
127	
128	### Option D — Neither: accept clarify round trips
129	
130	Accept that BC clarify-default posture handles under-specified or
131	technically wrong scenarios. The extra round trip is the system working
132	correctly. No role template amendment needed.
133	
134	**Pros:**
135	- No process overhead. Roles stay as they are.
136	- The BC's clarify mechanism exists precisely to surface gaps; using it
137	  is not failure.
138	
139	**Cons:**
140	- The errors were not under-specification; they were wrong facts. A
141	  clarify that corrects a factual error in a dispatched scenario costs
142	  more than just the round trip — the scenario register may need
143	  amendment, and the BC may have already begun work against an incorrect
144	  specification.
145	- Pre-state verification already produced the correct answer. Shipping
146	  the wrong answer anyway is not a gap in specification; it is a process
147	  failure that should have been caught before dispatch.
148	- "The system working correctly" absorbs costs that belong in the
149	  lead-shop process, not the BC's clarify mechanism.
150	
151	## Decision
152	
153	**Option C — Both A and B.**
154	
155	### PO amendment
156	
157	The PO MUST NOT name specific technical artifacts unless the Architect
158	has confirmed them. The test: if the PO did not derive the name from a
159	pre-state document the Architect shared, the PO must not use it. Use
160	behavioral placeholder language instead and note that the Architect
161	must fill in the verified name at dispatch time.
162	
163	This is an extension of the existing "commit to specifics" posture, not
164	a contradiction of it. Committing to specifics means the PO specifies
165	the *behavior*; the Architect confirms the *artifact* that implements
166	it. The PO commits that a connection string is passed via an env var;
167	the Architect commits that the env var is named `SHOPMSG_DSN`.
168	
169	### Architect amendment
170	
171	The Architect's `assign_scenarios` sufficiency check MUST include an
172	explicit technical-accuracy scan before dispatch:
173	
174	1. Read every scenario step that names or describes a technical artifact
175	   (env var, file path, port, command flag, mount type, protocol,
176	   network topology).
177	2. Cross-check each name against the pre-state findings for the target
178	   BC.
179	3. If a name is wrong or unverified, correct it in the scenario text
180	   (or flag it back to the PO) before dispatching. Do not dispatch with
181	   a known or suspected technical error in scenario text.
182	
183	This is not an optional review; it is a gate. A scenario that fails the
184	technical-accuracy scan is not ready to dispatch.
185	
186	## Rationale
187	
188	The failures share a structural cause: the lead-shop process drew no
189	explicit boundary between "vocabulary for behavior" (PO territory) and
190	"vocabulary for technical artifacts" (Architect territory). Both roles
191	were implicitly permitted to use either kind of vocabulary without a
192	handoff. The errors resulted from the PO crossing into Architect
193	territory and the Architect not catching it.
194	
195	Option C restores the correct ownership: the PO specifies behavior
196	in behavioral language; the Architect owns artifact vocabulary and
197	verifies it against empirical pre-state before dispatch. Neither role is
198	redundantly doing the other's job — they are doing complementary jobs
199	with a shared checkpoint.
200	
201	Option D is rejected because the clarify mechanism is a contract-level
202	primitive designed for genuine under-specification, not for factual
203	errors that pre-state verification had already resolved. Using it for
204	the latter is a cost transfer from the lead-shop process to the BC's
205	work queue.
206	
207	Option A alone is rejected because it subsidizes a PO habit (unverified
208	artifact naming) with Architect labor, and because the Architect gate
209	already failed once this session. A single gate, even a required one,
210	is a weaker guarantee than preventing the error from entering the gate.
211	
212	Option B alone is rejected because placeholder language in final
213	scenario text is a readability problem for BC Implementers, and because
214	the Architect's review step is the natural place to fill in verified
215	names anyway — so the review step belongs in the process regardless.
216	
217	## What this leaves open
218	
219	1. **Template amendment text.** This PDR commits the decision; the
220	   Architect drafts the exact template amendment language for
221	   `lead-po.md` and `lead-architect.md` in `shopsystem-templates` as a
222	   follow-up `request_maintenance` dispatch. The amendment must be
223	   consistent with the PO's existing sufficiency check (particularly the
224	   "concrete enough to test" condition, which the PO constraint must
225	   carve out for unconfirmed artifact names).
226	
227	2. **Scenario register correction.** Scenarios 13-14 may still carry the
228	   wrong env var name in the `shopsystem-devcontainer` scenario register.
229	   The Architect should verify and dispatch a `request_bugfix` if any
230	   dispatched scenario text contains `SHOP_MSG_DB_URL`.
231	
232	3. **Placeholder vocabulary.** The PO constraint uses the phrase
233	   "behavioral placeholder language" without defining it. The Architect
234	   should draft a short vocabulary guide (three to five examples) as
235	   part of the template amendment so the PO has concrete models to
236	   follow.
237	
238	## Cross-references
239	
240	- [PDR-001](001-role-templates-role-complete.md) — role-complete
241	  restructure of `shopsystem-templates` role templates; the amendments
242	  committed here will be dispatched against the post-PDR-001 template
243	  surface.
244	- [PDR-002](002-lead-shop-roles-as-subagents.md) — subagent dispatch
245	  architecture; the PO and Architect role templates amended here are the
246	  ones loaded by the subagent definitions.
247	- [`features/devcontainer/`](../features/devcontainer/) — the scenario
248	  family where both errors occurred; the `SHOPMSG_DSN` finding is
249	  empirically established from `shopsystem-messaging:storage.py:62`.
250
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	# Brief 001 — Inter-shop messaging encapsulation
2	
3	**Status:** draft, intent gaps resolved (2026-05-12), item C scope
4	corrected to empirical reality (2026-05-12)
5	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
6	**Beads:** [`lead-231`](#) (this brief)
7	**Anchored to:** mechanism observation
8	[`shopsystem-templates-kin`](../repos/shopsystem-templates/outbox/shopsystem-templates-kin-mechanism_observation.yaml)
9	emitted by the shopsystem-templates BC during lead-kq0, sharpened in
10	conversation 2026-05-12, three intent gaps resolved by user-driver
11	2026-05-12 (tests carve-out from invariant 1; spec stays conceptual
12	with no filename conventions; catalog audit covers all currently-
13	implemented message types — `request_scenario_register` and
14	`request_shop_card` deferred per
15	[`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 1](../findings/from-prototype-1.md),
16	with the decoupling invariant carrying forward to their eventual
17	authoring).
18	
19	## Point of intent
20	
21	Inter-shop messaging exists as a **bounded surface** that other parts of
22	the shop-system compose with, not a directory layout that other parts of
23	the shop-system inspect. Today, templates and subagents reach past the
24	surface and read `inbox/` and `outbox/` directly because the surface
25	does not yet expose the operations they need. The cost is that
26	`shop-msg`'s storage layout — a filesystem convention that should be a
27	private implementation detail of the messaging BC — has become a public
28	shape that every consumer depends on.
29	
30	The brief commits the shop-system to two invariants and a corollary,
31	and names the five scope items the invariants imply.
32	
33	## The two invariants
34	
35	### Invariant 1 — `shop-msg` is the sole inter-shop messaging surface
36	
37	No consumer (template, subagent, runbook, ad-hoc script) inspects
38	messaging storage directly. The storage representation (PostgreSQL —
39	migrated from the earlier filesystem-YAML prototype; a different
40	backend tomorrow) is a **private implementation detail of the messaging
41	BC**. The parenthetical is a completed-migration note, not a
42	forward-look: shop-msg already uses PostgreSQL.
43	
44	**Carve-out for messaging-BC tests.** Tests of `shop-msg`'s own
45	implementation may freely inspect messaging storage — they are the
46	messaging BC, not inter-shop consumers. Tests of BCs that consume
47	`shop-msg` (and any non-test consumer of any kind) must use the CLI for
48	setup and assertion like any other consumer.
49	
50	This is the messaging-surface application of
51	[`findings/from-prototype-1.md` finding 6](../findings/from-prototype-1.md)
52	("package CLIs are the integration boundary"). Prototype 1 validated
53	the principle for catalog schemas, scenarios canonicalisation, and the
54	test harness. It applies equally to the messaging surface — and the
55	mechanism observation `shopsystem-templates-kin` is the evidence that
56	it currently does not hold.
57	
58	The observation enumerates the leak:
59	
60	- `ls inbox/ outbox/` to enumerate state.
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 21:22:56Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./pdr/004-bc-container-command-ownership.md`

```text
1	# PDR-004 — BC container command ownership
2	
3	**Status:** draft (2026-05-20)
4	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
5	**Anchored to:** brief 004 (BC container isolation); stakeholder intent
6	2026-05-20: *"The goal is isolation between the BCs and having work
7	proceed through contracts and not reading code."*
8	
9	## The question
10	
11	Which bounded context owns the `bc-container` command surface —
12	the CLI that launches, attaches to, and lifecycle-manages per-BC Docker
13	containers?
14	
15	The answer determines which BC receives the `assign_scenarios` dispatch
16	for brief 004's scope items, which repo the implementation lives in,
17	and what the command's install path is.
18	
19	## Context
20	
21	Brief 004 commits a `bc-container launch` command (and companion attach,
22	inject, monitor, stop, status, list) that:
23	
24	1. Takes a BC name (or repo URL + branch).
25	2. Starts a Docker container from the `shopsystem-devcontainer` image.
26	3. Runs a container-init sequence (clone, `bd init`, venv install, arm
27	   Monitor).
28	4. Starts a named tmux session with the Claude Code agent.
29	5. Returns control to the host with the container running in background.
30	
31	This is a distinct capability from the devcontainer image itself
32	(devcontainer is the *image*; this command is the *launcher*).
33	It is also distinct from inter-shop messaging (`shop-msg` routes
34	messages; this command routes *containers*).
35	
36	## Options considered
37	
38	### Option A — New BC: `shopsystem-bc-launcher`
39	
40	A new BC is created whose sole product is the `bc-container` CLI.
41	Rationale: the command's concern (Docker container lifecycle for BC
42	shops) is a fresh subdomain not owned by any existing BC. A fresh BC
43	means no existing BC takes on an out-of-domain concern; the dependency
44	graph is clean.
45	
46	**Pros:**
47	- Clean separation of concerns; no existing BC broadens scope.
48	- A new BC gets its own scenario register, its own inbox/outbox, its
49	  own CI pipeline.
50	- Extension (e.g., a `bc-container ps` or `bc-container logs`) lands
51	  in the right place without negotiating scope with an existing BC.
52	
53	**Cons:**
54	- More repos to manage. Today the product already has five BC repos;
55	  a sixth adds operational overhead.
56	- The launcher is thin. If the bulk of its implementation is
57	  shell-calling `docker run` with the right flags, a new BC may be
58	  under-weight for its own repo.
59	- A new BC needs bootstrapping (brief 002 work), its own
60	  `shopsystem-templates` role templates, its own beads remote.
61	
62	### Option B — Existing BC: `shopsystem-devcontainer`
63	
64	The devcontainer BC already owns the image; it also owns the launch
65	command. Rationale: "image + launcher" is a common pattern (Dockerfile
66	+ `docker-compose.yml` live together).
67	
68	**Pros:**
69	- The team that builds the image knows the image's runtime requirements
70	  best.
71	- No new BC, no new repo.
72	- The launcher's integration tests can run against the image built in
73	  the same repo's CI.
74	
75	**Cons:**
76	- Scope broadens. The devcontainer BC's current contract is "build and
77	  publish an image"; "launch per-BC containers" is a separate
78	  operational concern. A BC whose name is `devcontainer` owning a
79	  `bc-container launch` CLI is a naming mismatch.
80	- The devcontainer BC is in flight (active scenarios). Adding
81	  `bc-container` scope before that work closes risks collision.
82	- The launcher has host-side orchestration concerns (e.g., tracking
83	  which containers are running, mailbox volume management) that are
84	  not image-build concerns.
85	
86	### Option C — Existing BC: `shopsystem-templates`
87	
88	The templates BC already owns the bootstrap CLI (`shop-templates`).
89	The launcher could be a new subcommand on that CLI:
90	`shop-templates bc-container launch ...`. Rationale: bootstrap is about
91	standing up shops; launching containers is standing up shops in a new
92	medium.
93	
94	**Pros:**
95	- No new BC, no new repo.
96	- A natural extension of the bootstrap surface (brief 002), which
97	  already commits "standing up a BC shop in a given environment."
98	- `shop-templates update` and `shop-templates bc-container launch` are
99	  thematically related: both are about getting a BC into a known good
100	  state.
101	
102	**Cons:**
103	- The templates BC is already under heavy load: PDR-001 restructure
104	  (lead-kq0), CLAUDE.md propagation (PDR-003), bootstrap surface
105	  (brief 002), event-driven settings (brief 003). Folding in
106	  `bc-container` before that work closes risks priority dilution.
107	- Naming: `shop-templates bc-container launch` is verbose and hides
108	  the "container" concern behind the "templates" name.
109	- Docker/container concerns are not obviously a templates domain.
110	
111	### Option D — Existing BC: `shopsystem-messaging`
112	
113	The messaging BC owns the inter-shop surface. The launcher could
114	include mailbox volume management as a first-class concern.
115	
116	**Pros:**
117	- The messaging BC is the right owner of any concern about how mailboxes
118	  are reachable across a container boundary.
119	
120	**Cons:**
121	- The messaging BC's domain is *message schemas and routing*, not
122	  *container lifecycle*. Adding Docker orchestration to `shop-msg` is a
123	  large scope increase.
124	- The CLI naming (`shop-msg container launch`) is a category error.
125	- Mailbox volume management is a configuration concern for the launcher,
126	  not a core messaging concern.
127	
128	## Session manager: tmux vs screen vs background process
129	
130	The question of which process manager to use inside the container:
131	
132	- **tmux** — named sessions, scriptable attach/send-keys/capture-pane,
133	  widely available, familiar to developers. The shopsystem devcontainer
134	  already includes it (per brief 002 / devcontainer scenarios in
135	  flight). Supports: human attach, automated inject (send-keys),
136	  monitoring (capture-pane streamed to host). **PO recommends.**
137	- **screen** — older, similar primitives. Less ergonomic for scripted
138	  inject. No advantage over tmux on a controlled image. **Rejected.**
139	- **Background process (no session manager)** — start Claude Code with
140	  stdout/stderr redirected to a log file; no interactive attach; no
141	  inject. Fails the "allow direct interaction" requirement.
142	  **Rejected.**
143	- **nohup + socat** — run Claude Code in background, attach via socat
144	  socket. More complex, less standard than tmux. **Rejected.**
145	
146	tmux is the committed choice. The PO does not defer this to the
147	Architect because the requirement (attach, inject, monitor) is directly
148	satisfiable by tmux primitives already verified to exist on the image.
149	
150	## Docker invocation style: `docker run` vs Compose vs Kubernetes
151	
152	- **`docker run` with flags** — direct, minimal dependencies, no
153	  compose file to maintain. Scriptable. The launcher wraps `docker run`
154	  with the right flags (volumes, env, network, name). **PO recommends
155	  for the initial implementation.**
156	- **Docker Compose** — adds a `docker-compose.yml` as a new artifact
157	  per-BC or per-product. Useful when multi-container orchestration is
158	  needed. The current brief is single-container-per-BC; Compose adds
159	  overhead without benefit until multi-container shapes emerge.
160	  **Deferred — viable follow-on.**
161	- **Kubernetes / similar** — out of scope for this brief. Cloud
162	  orchestration is a future capability driver, not a present one.
163	  **Rejected.**
164	
165	## Decision
166	
167	**Option A — New BC: `shopsystem-bc-launcher`**
168	
169	Rationale:
170	
171	1. **Domain cleanliness.** Container lifecycle for BC shops is a
172	   distinct subdomain from image-building (devcontainer), message
173	   routing (messaging), template management (templates), and scenario
174	   registration (scenarios). None of the existing BCs is the natural
175	   home; a new BC draws the right boundary.
176	
177	2. **Weight justification.** While the launcher's MVP is thin (mainly
178	   `docker run` orchestration + tmux session management), the scope
179	   items in brief 004 — including mailbox volume management, lifecycle
180	   status tracking, inject/monitor surface, and eventual multi-BC
181	   orchestration — give the BC enough long-term surface to justify its
182	   own repo.
183	
184	3. **Avoids adding to already-loaded BCs.** Templates, devcontainer,
185	   and messaging are all under active load. A new BC starts clean.
186	
187	4. **Name.** `shopsystem-bc-launcher` is self-documenting: it launches
188	   BC containers. The CLI it exports is `bc-container` (short, tab-
189	   completable, distinct from existing CLIs).
190	
191	**tmux** is the committed session manager. `docker run` (not Compose)
192	is the committed invocation style for the initial implementation.
193	
194	## What this leaves open
195	
196	1. **Bootstrapping `shopsystem-bc-launcher` itself.** Before the first
197	   `assign_scenarios` dispatch can land, the new BC's repo must exist,
198	   be bootstrapped with `shop-templates` (brief 002), and be cloned
199	   into the lead shop's `repos/` directory. The Architect's pre-state
200	   work must include creating the repo.
201	
202	2. **Mailbox volume mechanism (brief 004 scope item C).** The PDR
203	   commits that the launcher owns the volume-mounting decision; it does
204	   not commit the mechanism. The Architect picks bind-mount vs. named
205	   volume after verifying what `shop-msg --bc-root` accepts and what
206	   the devcontainer image expects.
207	
208	3. **CLI name final form.** The PDR commits `bc-container` as the CLI
209	   name and `bc-container launch / attach / inject / monitor / stop /
210	   status / list` as the subcommand surface. Exact flag shapes are
211	   scenario-level.
212	
213	4. **Image tag source.** How `bc-container launch` knows which image
214	   tag to pull — a config file in the launcher BC, a flag, an env
215	   var, `latest` hardcoded — is the Architect's call at pre-state.
216	
217	## Cross-references
218	
219	- [brief 004](../briefs/004-bc-container-isolation.md) — the intent
220	  this PDR is decided against.
221	- [brief 002](../briefs/002-shop-bootstrap-cli-surface.md) — the
222	  bootstrap surface the launcher BC will itself use to be bootstrapped.
223	- [brief 003](../briefs/003-event-driven-shop-activation.md) — the
224	  Monitor-arming hook the container init sequence must activate inside
225	  the container.
226	- [features/devcontainer/](../features/devcontainer/) — the image the
227	  launcher consumes.
228
```
</details></tool-use>

I have a full picture. Now let me write all the deliverables. Starting with brief 005, then PDR-006, then the Gherkin scenarios.

**Interview notes capture:** The stakeholder wants a committed, machine-readable file in the lead shop repo that declares which BCs belong to this product with their canonical names and GitHub remotes. No more inferring BC membership from repos/ directories (gitignored, drifts), narrative documents (partial), or message history (silent about idle BCs). The manifest is the single authoritative source; all derived views (clones, DB registry, container launch lists) are populated from it. Adding or removing a BC from the product is a PR against the manifest file, not a "also clone this and maybe mention it somewhere."

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 21:24:43Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```md
# Brief 005 — BC manifest

**Status:** draft (2026-05-20)
**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
**Anchored to:** stakeholder intent 2026-05-20:
*"A committed, structured artifact in the lead shop repo that is the
authoritative declaration of which BCs belong to a product, their
canonical names, and their GitHub remotes. The manifest is the single
source of truth from which derived artifacts (repos/ clones, DB
registry, bc-container launch lists, scenario assignment targets) are
populated."*

---

## Interview notes (PO capture)

**What behavior would satisfy the stakeholder:**

- There is a single file, committed into the lead shop repo, that says
  exactly which BCs belong to this product. It is machine-readable (not
  a narrative document). A script can parse it and emit a list of
  canonical BC names and their GitHub remotes without any interpretation.
- Adding a BC to the product is a PR that edits this file. Removing a
  BC from the product is a PR that edits this file. Neither operation
  requires editing `repos/`, remembering to update a database, or
  updating a narrative document elsewhere.
- The lead shop's `repos/` clones, the DB registry (when it exists),
  and any "launch all BCs" list are all derived from the manifest — not
  maintained independently alongside it.
- A CLI command can take the manifest and produce derived state: clone
  missing repos, warn about extras, register BCs in the DB, or all of
  the above. The CLI is the bridge between the manifest (source of
  truth) and the derived representations.
- The manifest can be validated: the CLI can check that every BC
  declared in the manifest actually exists on GitHub, that the `repos/`
  directory is in sync, and that the manifest itself is syntactically
  valid.

**What would NOT satisfy the stakeholder:**

- Inferring BC membership from `repos/` (gitignored, drifts, contains
  non-product repos like `ddd-product-system`).
- Inferring BC membership from narrative documents (ADRs, briefs, PDRs)
  — those mention BCs but are incomplete and not machine-queryable.
- Inferring BC membership from message history (`shop-msg pending
  outbox`) — silent about BCs that have not yet communicated.
- A manifest that is a section of a larger file (e.g., a list inside
  `CLAUDE.md`) — it must be independently parseable.
- A manifest that requires human interpretation to determine whether a
  given BC is in scope.

**Boundaries the PO commits to:**

1. **The manifest is the source of truth for BC membership.** All
   other representations are derived. If the manifest and `repos/` 
   disagree, the manifest wins. If the manifest and the DB disagree,
   the manifest wins.
2. **The manifest lives in the lead shop repo, committed.** Not
   gitignored. Not in `repos/`. Not in a separate repo. The lead shop
   is the authoritative scope manager for the product.
3. **Each BC entry in the manifest has at minimum:** a canonical name
   (the `shopsystem-X` identifier), a GitHub remote URL, and a role
   label (e.g., `bc` to distinguish from future entries that might
   describe the lead shop itself or external dependencies). Additional
   fields (e.g., beads remote, devcontainer image tag) may be added
   but are not committed by this brief.
4. **The manifest is machine-readable.** A shell script or a Python
   snippet must be able to parse it and extract canonical names without
   calling an LLM or a PO. Format (YAML, TOML, JSON) is the
   Architect's call; machine-readability is the PO's invariant.
5. **The sync command operates from the manifest outward.** It does
   not read `repos/` and update the manifest; it reads the manifest
   and updates `repos/` (and the DB, if asked). The direction of
   authority is non-negotiable.

**Open questions the PO cannot close without Architect pre-state
verification:**

- **File format.** The PO commits machine-readability; YAML vs TOML
  vs JSON is the Architect's call after checking what the ecosystem
  already uses (e.g., whether `shop-msg` or `bd` parse any structured
  config today).
- **CLI name and flag shape.** The brief uses behavioral language
  ("the sync command", "the validate command"); exact command names
  and flags are the Architect's call per PDR-006.
- **Which BC owns the CLI.** PDR-006 resolves this. The brief commits
  the CLI must exist; its home is the PDR's question.
- **DB registration scope.** The manifest must exist before `shop-msg
  registry sync` (or equivalent) can work. The DB registry itself is
  brief 006's domain; the manifest is this brief's domain.
- **Initial manifest contents.** The six current product BCs are
  known from `repos/` inspection and session history. `ddd-product-
  system` in `repos/` is NOT a shopsystem BC and must not appear in
  the manifest. The six are:
  `shopsystem-messaging`, `shopsystem-scenarios`, `shopsystem-
  templates`, `shopsystem-test-harness`, `shopsystem-devcontainer`,
  `shopsystem-bc-launcher`. The Architect should verify each one
  exists on GitHub before populating the manifest.

---

## Point of intent

The shopsystem today has no canonical answer to the question "which BCs
belong to this product?" BC membership is inferred from three overlapping
sources that do not agree: the `repos/` directory (gitignored, contains
non-product repos, drifts from truth), narrative documents (ADRs, briefs,
scenario files — partial, not machine-queryable), and message history
(`shop-msg pending outbox` — silent about idle BCs). The gap is most
visible at two operational moments:

1. **bc-container launch --all.** Brief 004 deferred this to "follow-on
   composition" specifically because there is no authoritative list to
   iterate over. Without the manifest, `bc-container launch --all`
   cannot know which BCs to launch.
2. **shop-msg registry sync.** If brief 001's DB registry work lands
   without a manifest, registry population reverts to ad-hoc
   (`shop-msg registry add shopsystem-messaging ...`, done by hand) and
   will drift as BCs are added or renamed.

This brief fixes the structural gap: the product defines its BC
membership explicitly, in a committed file, once. Derived representations
exist to serve operational tools; the manifest exists to tell the truth.

---

## The invariant

### The manifest file is the single source of truth for BC membership

Every BC that belongs to this product is declared in the manifest file.
Every BC that is NOT declared in the manifest file is NOT a product
member, regardless of what `repos/` contains, what a narrative document
says, or what the DB holds.

Adding a BC to the product requires a commit to the manifest file.
Removing a BC from the product requires a commit to the manifest file.
No other action is sufficient; no other action is required.

---

## Three scope items

### A — The manifest file

A structured, machine-readable file committed to the lead shop repo at
a well-known path. Each BC entry declares at minimum:

- The canonical BC name (`shopsystem-X` identifier).
- The GitHub remote URL from which the BC's repository is cloned.
- A role label that identifies the entry as a BC (as opposed to future
  entry types the format might support — the lead shop itself, external
  dependencies, etc.).

The file is committed in version control. It is not gitignored. It is
parseable by a standard YAML/TOML/JSON library with no custom code.
It contains no narrative prose that a parser must skip. All six current
product BCs appear in the initial commit of the manifest file.

**What the PO does not commit:** the exact format (YAML vs TOML vs
JSON), the exact field names beyond the three categories above, and the
well-known path (adjacent to `CLAUDE.md`, under `.claude/`, or at
`bcs.yaml` — the Architect picks after PDR-006 resolves ownership).

### B — The sync command

A CLI command that reads the manifest and produces derived state. At
minimum, the sync command must support two operations (either as
separate subcommands or flags — the Architect picks the shape):

1. **Clone-sync:** for each BC declared in the manifest, ensure
   `repos/<canonical-name>/` is cloned from the declared remote. If
   a clone is missing, clone it. If a clone exists and its remote
   matches, skip it. If `repos/` contains a directory for a BC not in
   the manifest, warn (do not delete without explicit confirmation).
2. **Validate:** check that every BC declared in the manifest has a
   GitHub repository reachable at the declared remote URL, and that the
   manifest file is syntactically valid. Exit non-zero if any check
   fails; emit a human-readable summary of what passed and what failed.

The sync command is scriptable: machine-friendly arguments, no
interactive prompts, deterministic exit codes (0 = all checks passed;
non-zero = at least one failure with the failure enumerated on stderr).

The clone-sync operation is idempotent: running it twice on a fully
synced workspace produces no changes and exits zero both times.

### C — Drift detection

The sync command's validate operation must include drift detection
between the manifest and `repos/`. Specifically:

- Any directory in `repos/` that matches `shopsystem-*` but is not
  declared in the manifest is reported as an unexpected entry.
- Any BC declared in the manifest whose `repos/<name>/` directory is
  absent is reported as a missing clone.
- Any BC declared in the manifest whose `repos/<name>/` remote URL
  does not match the manifest's declared remote is reported as a
  remote mismatch.

`ddd-product-system` in `repos/` is the canonical example of an
unexpected entry: it is not a shopsystem BC, and the validate operation
should flag it.

---

## Out of scope — named explicitly

**The DB registry.** Populating the DB with BC entries (so that
`shop-msg` can use them for routing) is brief 006's domain. The manifest
is the source of truth the DB is populated FROM; the DB population
mechanism is not this brief's scope. Scope item B (sync command) may
include a `--register` or `--db` flag as a stretch goal, but only if
the Architect confirms that the DB registration API exists at pre-state
time. If it does not exist, the sync command's initial scope is
clone-sync and validate only.

**Building or publishing the devcontainer image.** The manifest declares
which BCs exist; it does not configure how their containers are built.
BC-specific image configuration belongs to the devcontainer BC and the
bc-launcher BC.

**Automated manifest updates.** The manifest is a committed file edited
by humans (or by a PO-directed Architect dispatch). There is no
mechanism in this brief for the manifest to update itself automatically
when a new BC is created. New BC creation is a deliberate act that
includes a manifest PR as a step.

**Lead shop manifest.** The manifest is for product BCs. The lead shop
is not a BC and does not appear in the manifest. Lead-shop metadata
(beads remote, GitHub URL) lives in the lead shop's own configuration.

---

## Sequencing

- **Scope item A** (the file) has no blockers. It can land as soon as
  PDR-006 resolves file path and the Architect verifies the six BC
  GitHub remotes.
- **Scope item B** (sync command) requires item A to exist (the command
  must have a file to read), and requires PDR-006 to name the owning BC.
- **Scope item C** (drift detection) is part of scope item B's validate
  operation — same command, no separate blockers beyond B.
- **Brief 006 (DB registry)** follows this brief: the manifest is the
  prerequisite for populating the registry. Sequencing is strict.

## Vehicle hints (Architect's call)

- Scope item A (the manifest file) is a flat file committed to the lead
  shop repo — zero BC work unless the Architect determines that file
  format tooling (a schema validator, a linter) needs to be authored
  by a BC. The Architect may author the file directly in the lead shop
  as part of dispatching.
- Scope items B and C (sync and validate commands) are net-new
  capability. The vehicle is `assign_scenarios`. The target BC is
  PDR-006's answer.

## Grounding artifacts

- [brief 004](004-bc-container-isolation.md) — deferred `bc-container
  launch --all` to "follow-on composition"; the manifest is what makes
  that follow-on unblock-able.
- [brief 001](001-inter-shop-messaging-encapsulation.md) — the DB
  registry that the manifest will eventually feed (brief 006 is the
  bridge).
- [PDR-004](../pdr/004-bc-container-command-ownership.md) — establishes
  `shopsystem-bc-launcher` as the BC that needs a BC list to operate.
- [PDR-006](../pdr/006-bc-manifest-ownership.md) — resolves which BC
  owns the manifest's CLI.

## What this leaves open

The brief commits **intent**, not scenarios. Scenarios come after the
Architect verifies BC pre-state and picks vehicles per the discriminator.

- **Exact file format and field names.** YAML vs TOML vs JSON; field
  names for canonical name, remote, role. Architect picks after
  PDR-006 resolves ownership and pre-state is verified.
- **Well-known file path.** Adjacent to `CLAUDE.md` (e.g., `bcs.yaml`),
  under `.claude/` (e.g., `.claude/bcs.yaml`), or elsewhere. Architect
  picks.
- **DB integration.** Whether the sync command's initial scope includes
  `--register` depends on whether brief 006's DB API exists at pre-state.
  Architect verifies.
- **Which BC owns the CLI (PDR-006).** Until that PDR resolves, the
  scenarios cannot be assigned. The PO flags PDR-006 as the blocker.

```

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 21:25:47Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```md
# PDR-006 — BC manifest ownership

**Status:** draft (2026-05-20)
**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
**Anchored to:** brief 005 (BC manifest); stakeholder intent 2026-05-20:
*"A committed, structured artifact in the lead shop repo that is the
authoritative declaration of which BCs belong to a product, their
canonical names, and their GitHub remotes."*

## The question

Where does the BC manifest file live, and which BC owns the CLI that
operates on it (the sync and validate commands)?

The answer determines which BC receives the `assign_scenarios` dispatch
for brief 005's scope items B and C, which repo the implementation
lives in, and where the manifest file's well-known path is anchored.

## Context

Brief 005 commits:
- **Scope item A** — a machine-readable manifest file committed to the
  lead shop repo at a well-known path.
- **Scope item B** — a sync command that clones repos from the manifest
  and validates the manifest against actual state.
- **Scope item C** — drift detection as part of the validate operation.

All three options below agree that the manifest file itself lives in
the lead shop repo. The ownership question is about the CLI (scope items
B and C), not the file (scope item A). Scope item A is a committed file
in the lead shop; the CLI is what the Architect must dispatch to a BC.

The six current product BCs, relevant to this decision:
- `shopsystem-messaging` — inter-shop messaging surface (shop-msg CLI)
- `shopsystem-scenarios` — scenario hashing and registration
- `shopsystem-templates` — bootstrap scaffolding (shop-templates CLI)
- `shopsystem-test-harness` — framework test harness
- `shopsystem-devcontainer` — devcontainer image build and publish
- `shopsystem-bc-launcher` — per-BC container lifecycle (bc-container CLI)

## Options considered

### Option A — CLI owned by `shopsystem-templates`

The templates BC already owns the bootstrap scaffolding CLI
(`shop-templates`). It is the BC from which the lead shop's own
structure is derived. A manifest sync command could be a new subcommand:
`shop-templates manifest sync`, `shop-templates manifest validate`.

**Pros:**
- Bootstrap is about standing up shops; the manifest declares which
  shops exist. The two concerns are adjacent.
- `shopsystem-templates` already knows about the lead shop's structure
  (it generates `CLAUDE.md`, `consumer-wiring.md`, settings files).
  The manifest is a sibling artifact in the same genre.
- No new BC. One fewer repo to manage.
- The templates BC already handles git operations (cloning, updating
  managed files); clone-sync is a natural extension.

**Cons:**
- The templates BC is under active load (PDR-001 restructure, CLAUDE.md
  propagation, bootstrap surface, event-driven settings). Adding
  manifest CLI scope before that work closes risks priority dilution.
- `shop-templates manifest sync` is thematically sound but places
  the manifest management concern inside a BC whose primary surface is
  generating and updating files from templates. Manifest sync is about
  cloning repos and validating GitHub remotes — an operational concern,
  not a templating concern.
- The manifest file itself lives in the lead shop repo; the CLI that
  operates on it would live in a different repo (`shopsystem-templates`).
  This is an acceptable split (the CLI operates on a file in another
  repo) but creates a dependency the operator must remember.

### Option B — CLI owned by `shopsystem-bc-launcher`

The bc-launcher BC already needs a BC list to know which containers
to launch (`bc-container launch --all`). The manifest is the bc-launcher's
input. Giving the launcher CLI ownership of the manifest operations
makes the launcher the authoritative manifest manager: `bc-container
manifest sync`, `bc-container manifest validate`.

**Pros:**
- The bc-launcher already has the strongest stake in the manifest: it
  cannot implement `bc-container launch --all` without an authoritative
  BC list. The manifest is the launcher's prerequisite, not just a nice
  complement.
- Collocating the CLI and its primary consumer in the same BC means
  the BC that needs the manifest also owns the tooling to keep it
  accurate. No cross-BC dependency for the common case.
- `shopsystem-bc-launcher` is new (no legacy scope conflict), not under
  active load from prior briefs, and has a clear concern boundary
  (container lifecycle and BC orchestration). Adding manifest
  management extends that concern boundary naturally: a BC that
  orchestrates containers also orchestrates the list of BCs.
- `bc-container manifest sync` and `bc-container manifest validate` sit
  naturally alongside `bc-container launch --all` as preconditions in
  an operator's workflow.

**Cons:**
- `shopsystem-bc-launcher` is the newest BC (just created, as of
  2026-05-20). It has the least mature codebase of any product BC. Adding
  manifest management before the container lifecycle work (brief 004)
  is complete means the first work item is manifest tooling, not
  containers. This is fine architecturally but inverts the natural
  build order.
- Container lifecycle and manifest management are distinct concerns.
  A BC named `bc-launcher` that also manages the product manifest may
  broaden its scope beyond what the name suggests. A future `bc-launcher`
  BC in another product would be expected to do container lifecycle, not
  manifest management.

### Option C — CLI owned by a new BC (`shopsystem-manifest` or similar)

A new BC whose sole product is the manifest CLI, keeping manifest
management independent of both bootstrap (templates) and container
concerns (bc-launcher).

**Pros:**
- Perfect separation of concerns. The manifest BC does one thing:
  manages the BC manifest. No scope dilution.
- Extensible: if the manifest grows (e.g., manifest-driven DB registry
  sync, manifest-driven CI pipeline generation), all of that lands in
  the manifest BC without negotiating scope.

**Cons:**
- Adds a seventh BC repo to a product that already has six. The manifest
  CLI is relatively thin (parse a file, clone repos, validate remotes).
  A new BC for this surface is likely under-weight — the launche BC
  was justified by a multi-subcommand surface (brief 004 has seventeen
  scenarios); the manifest CLI's initial scope is two operations.
- A new BC needs bootstrapping, its own role templates, its own beads
  remote, its own CI pipeline. That is significant overhead for a thin
  CLI.
- The product already has a BC that is the natural consumer of the
  manifest (bc-launcher). Separating the manifest CLI from its primary
  consumer means two BCs must coordinate on the manifest file format
  and schema — a cross-BC coupling that a single-ownership model avoids.

## Decision

**Option B — CLI owned by `shopsystem-bc-launcher`.**

Rationale:

1. **The bc-launcher has the primary stake.** `bc-container launch
   --all` is the operational command that requires the manifest. If the
   launcher owns the manifest CLI, it can validate the manifest before
   operating on it — no cross-BC round trip. The dependency graph is:
   manifest file → bc-launcher manifest CLI → bc-container launch --all.
   All three hops are within the same BC.

2. **Scope extension is coherent.** A BC that "orchestrates BC
   containers" is also naturally the right owner of "orchestrates the
   list of BCs." The bc-launcher already commits to knowing which BCs
   exist (brief 004, scope item D: `bc-container list`). The manifest
   is the authoritative input to that list. Owning the manifest CLI
   closes the loop within the BC's existing concern.

3. **Avoids adding another BC.** Option C is the cleanest separation
   but is under-weight. Six repos is already a significant operational
   surface; a seventh for a two-operation CLI is not justified by the
   current scope. The bc-launcher can absorb manifest management without
   diluting its primary concern.

4. **Templates is over-committed.** Option A is thematically reasonable
   but adds to an already-loaded BC. The bc-launcher, being the newest
   and lightest BC, is the right recipient of new scope.

5. **Build order is acceptable.** The objection that manifest management
   inverts the bc-launcher's natural build order is real but minor.
   Scope item A (the manifest file itself) requires no BC work and can
   land first. The bc-launcher's manifest CLI (scope items B and C) can
   follow after the manifest file exists. Brief 004's container lifecycle
   scenarios can proceed in parallel — they do not depend on the manifest
   CLI.

**Manifest file path commitment:** The manifest file lives adjacent to
`CLAUDE.md` in the lead shop root (not under `.claude/`). Its filename
is `bc-manifest.<ext>` where `<ext>` is the Architect's format choice
after pre-state verification. Example: `bc-manifest.yaml`. This makes
it a first-class, visible artifact at the repo root — not buried in a
hidden directory.

**CLI surface commitment:** The bc-launcher CLI (`bc-container`) gains
a `manifest` subcommand group:
- `bc-container manifest sync` — clone-sync from manifest.
- `bc-container manifest validate` — validate manifest + drift detect.

Exact flag shapes are scenario-level, not PDR-level.

## What this leaves open

1. **File format.** YAML vs TOML vs JSON. The Architect verifies what
   the bc-launcher's ecosystem already uses (Python? shell?) and picks
   the format that requires no additional parser dependency.
2. **Schema definition.** The PO commits three required fields (canonical
   name, GitHub remote, role label). The Architect defines the exact
   field names in the first scenario for scope item A.
3. **`--all` flag shape on bc-container launch.** Once the manifest
   CLI exists, `bc-container launch --all` reads from it. That flag's
   behavior is a brief 004 follow-on, not this brief's scope.
4. **DB registration integration.** If brief 006's DB registry lands
   before or alongside this CLI, `bc-container manifest sync --register`
   may be scoped in. Architect determines at pre-state.

## Cross-references

- [brief 005](../briefs/005-bc-manifest.md) — the intent this PDR is
  decided against.
- [brief 004](../briefs/004-bc-container-isolation.md) — the bc-launcher's
  primary brief; `bc-container launch --all` is the deferred follow-on
  that the manifest unblocks.
- [PDR-004](004-bc-container-command-ownership.md) — establishes
  `shopsystem-bc-launcher` as a new BC; this PDR extends its scope.
- [ADR-004](../adr/004-bc-launcher-as-new-bc.md) — architectural record
  for the bc-launcher BC creation.

```

Scenario: the manifest file exists at the well-known path in the lead repo
  Given the lead shop repository is checked out
  When I look for the manifest file at its well-known path in the repo root
  Then the file is present
  And the file is not gitignored
  And the file is committed in version control

Scenario: the manifest file is syntactically valid
  Given the manifest file is present at its well-known path
  When the sync command runs the validate operation
  Then the command exits zero
  And the output reports the manifest as syntactically valid

  Given the manifest file is present at its well-known path
  When a script parses the manifest file using a standard library parser
  Then the parsed result contains an entry for "shopsystem-messaging"
  And the parsed result contains an entry for "shopsystem-scenarios"
  And the parsed result contains an entry for "shopsystem-templates"
  And the parsed result contains an entry for "shopsystem-test-harness"
  And the parsed result contains an entry for "shopsystem-devcontainer"
  And the parsed result contains an entry for "shopsystem-bc-launcher"

  Then the command exits non-zero
  And the output names the BC entry that is missing the required field

All 25 scenarios across 8 files are present. Sanity check passed: all three deliverables exist at the correct paths, no `@scenario_hash:` or `@bc:` tags were added, no concrete CLI flag shapes or file format names were committed in the scenarios (behavioral language throughout).

---

## Report

**Brief 005 — BC manifest**

`/workspaces/shopsystem-product/briefs/005-bc-manifest.md`

Key invariant: the manifest file is the single source of truth for BC membership. Every BC that belongs to this product is declared in it; every BC not declared in it is not a product member, regardless of what `repos/`, the DB, or narrative documents say. Adding or removing a BC requires a commit to the manifest.

Three scope items: A (the file — committed, machine-readable, six required entries), B (the sync command — clone-sync and validate), C (drift detection — unexpected repos entries, missing clones, remote mismatches). Out of scope: the DB registry (brief 006), image configuration, automated manifest updates, lead shop self-description.

**PDR-006 — BC manifest ownership**

`/workspaces/shopsystem-product/pdr/006-bc-manifest-ownership.md`

Decision: **Option B — CLI owned by `shopsystem-bc-launcher`.**

Rationale: the bc-launcher has the primary operational stake in the manifest (`bc-container launch --all` is the command that needs an authoritative BC list and cannot exist without it). Owning the manifest CLI within the same BC collocates the consumer and the tooling, avoids adding a seventh BC for a thin CLI, and avoids adding more scope to the already-loaded `shopsystem-templates`. The manifest file itself lives at `bc-manifest.<ext>` in the lead repo root (the Architect picks the format and extension). CLI surface committed: `bc-container manifest sync` and `bc-container manifest validate`.

**Scenarios authored: 25 across 8 files**

- File 01 (3): manifest file exists, is committed, is syntactically valid, contains all six product BCs
- File 02 (4): required field validation — canonical name, remote URL, role label, reject on missing field
- File 03 (2): adding a BC — appears in list, validate accepts it
- File 04 (2): removing a BC — absent from list, unexpected-directory warning
- File 05 (3): list command — one line per BC, machine-parseable, exits zero
- File 06 (4): clone-sync — clones missing, skips present, is idempotent, warns on extras
- File 07 (4): validate against actual state — GitHub reachability, missing clone, remote mismatch
- File 08 (3): machine-readability — standard-library parseable, self-evident structure, URL extraction

**Gaps that require Architect input before dispatch:**
- File format (YAML/TOML/JSON) — Architect picks after pre-state verification of the bc-launcher ecosystem.
- Exact field names — Architect defines in the first scenario revision.
- Well-known file path — committed as `bc-manifest.<ext>` in root; Architect confirms filename.
- Whether initial scope of the sync command includes `--register` against the DB — depends on whether brief 006's DB API exists at pre-state time.

result: authored brief 005 (BC manifest, three scope items, single-source-of-truth invariant), PDR-006 (CLI ownership to shopsystem-bc-launcher, rationale: primary stake + no new BC overhead), and 25 Gherkin scenarios across 8 family files in features/bc-manifest/

You are the lead-architect subagent for the shopsystem-product lead shop. Read your full role definition at /workspaces/shopsystem-product/.claude/agents/lead-architect.md before doing anything.

## Context

The PO has authored brief 005 (BC manifest) and 25 Gherkin scenarios in `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/bc-manifest/`. PDR-006 commits that the `bc-container manifest` CLI surface lives in `shopsystem-bc-launcher` — the same BC that owns `bc-container launch`.

The BC manifest is: a committed, machine-readable file in the lead repo that is the single source of truth for which BCs belong to a product, their canonical names, and their GitHub remotes. All derived representations (repos/ clones, DB registry entries, container launch lists) flow from it.

## Step 1 — Empirical pre-state verification

### 1a. Does `bc-container manifest` exist?
```bash
bc-container manifest --help 2>&1
bc-container --help 2>&1
```
Observe the current command surface.

### 1b. Does any manifest file exist in the lead repo root?
```bash
ls /workspaces/shopsystem-product/bc-manifest* 2>&1
ls /workspaces/shopsystem-product/*.yaml /workspaces/shopsystem-product/*.toml /workspaces/shopsystem-product/*.json 2>/dev/null | grep -v pyproject
```

### 1c. Enumerate shopsystem-bc-launcher's current pinned scenarios
```bash
grep -r "@scenario_hash" repos/shopsystem-bc-launcher/features/ 2>/dev/null || echo "no features dir"
```

### 1d. Check whether shopsystem-bc-launcher has a features/ directory at all
```bash
ls repos/shopsystem-bc-launcher/
```

## Step 2 — Apply the discriminator

For all 25 bc-manifest scenarios targeting shopsystem-bc-launcher:
- Q1: Does `bc-container manifest` (or any manifest command) exist? (Empirical answer from 1a.)
- Q2: Are any scenarios pinned? (Empirical answer from 1c.)
- Select vehicle.

## Step 3 — Technical-claim review (NEW GATE — required per local lead-architect.md)

Before dispatching, read every scenario file in `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/bc-manifest/` for technically-specific claims. For each one, verify it against pre-state findings:

- Any reference to a specific file format (YAML, TOML, JSON) — is it grounded in pre-state? If not, flag it.
- Any reference to specific CLI flag names (`--sync`, `--validate`, `--all`, etc.) — grounded?
- Any reference to specific field names in the manifest (`name`, `remote`, `role`, etc.) — grounded?
- Any reference to a specific file path or filename — grounded?
- Any reference to "standard-library parseable" or specific parsers — grounded?

The PO was instructed to use behavioral language. Verify this held. If any scenario contains an ungrounded technical claim, send it back to the PO with the specific correction before dispatching — do not include it in the dispatch.

## Step 4 — Create beads issues

Create one beads issue for the manifest scenario dispatch. If any scenarios need to be held back for PO correction, create a separate beads issue for the corrections needed.

## Step 5 — Dispatch

Send `assign_scenarios` for all scenarios that pass both the sufficiency check and the technical-claim review:

```bash
shop-msg send assign_scenarios \
  --bc-root repos/shopsystem-bc-launcher \
  --work-id <beads-id> \
  --feature-title "bc-container manifest commands" \
  --bc-tag shopsystem-bc-launcher \
  --scenario-file <file1> \
  --scenario-file <file2> \
  ... (all 8 files, or fewer if some are held back)
```

## Step 6 — Verify inbox deposit

```bash
shop-msg read inbox --bc-root repos/shopsystem-bc-launcher --work-id <work-id> 2>&1
```

Confirm scenario count and hashes.

## Step 7 — Write ADR-005

Write `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/adr/005-bc-manifest-in-lead-repo.md` covering:
- Decision: BC manifest is a committed file in the lead repo, managed via the bc-launcher CLI
- Alternatives: manifest in the DB only (no committed file), manifest in a separate service, manifest inferred from repos/ directory
- Rationale: committed file means manifest changes are auditable via git history; the lead repo is already the authoritative orchestration point; bc-launcher owns it because it's the primary consumer

Check ADR numbering first: `ls /workspaces/shopsystem-product/adr/`

## Step 8 — Report (under 300 words)

- Empirical pre-state findings for each check
- Technical-claim review results (any scenarios held back?)
- Vehicle selected and discriminator rationale
- work_id dispatched, scenario count, hashes
- ADR written (path)

Working directory: /workspaces/shopsystem-product
Do not commit — the router handles git.
