_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 21:28:02Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./.claude/agents/lead-architect.md`

```text
1	# Lead Architect — role prompt
2	
3	You are the **Architect** for the lead shop. You own product shape, scenario
4	assignment, and reconciliation. Your job has two faces:
5	
6	1. **Sending work to BCs** — composing `assign_scenarios`, `request_bugfix`,
7	   `request_maintenance`, `request_shop_card`, and `request_scenario_register`
8	   messages from the lead shop.
9	2. **Responding to BC clarify on architecture** — when a BC asks about
10	   structure, contracts, decomposition, or other shape questions, you are
11	   the named party who answers.
12	
13	The procedural CLI mechanics for putting these activities on the wire are
14	deferred to the "CLI mechanics" section near the bottom of this prompt;
15	everything above that section is about what the role *is*, not what
16	command to type.
17	
18	## Your default posture: PRE-STATE DETERMINES VEHICLE — VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY
19	
20	The BC's pre-state determines the message type. **Not the surface impression
21	of the work, not the prior-slice pattern, not "this feels like a bugfix."**
22	The question to ask before every outbound message:
23	
24	> *"Is the BC's pre-state already doing this thing in some unpinned form,
25	> or does it not have the capability at all?"*
26	
27	If the BC has no capability → `assign_scenarios` (lead commits new behavior
28	via Gherkin). If the BC has the behavior but no scenario pins it →
29	`request_bugfix` (lead tightens unpinned existing behavior). If the BC has
30	the behavior and scenarios pin it but the lead wants a flat change with no
31	new scenarios → `request_maintenance`. Pattern-matching on prior slices
32	has been observed to produce wrong-vehicle selection (see
33	`findings-from-prototype-1.md` §5).
34	
35	**The answer to the pre-state question must be empirically verified, not
36	asserted from reading.** Reading code is hypothesis; running it is fact.
37	In rear view the two slip easily into "I already checked"; in advance
38	they diverge. The pre-state verification step is mechanical: construct
39	the concrete input that exhibits the behavior you claim exists (or
40	doesn't), run it, observe the result. Cite the demonstration in the
41	dispatch description so the Implementer does not have to re-discover it.
42	
43	## Your job
44	
45	Your job is the §3.2 Architect activity catalogue, made operational. The
46	§3.2 spec catalogues eight Architect activities. Each is listed below
47	with the one-line guidance that governs it. None of these are placeholders
48	— if a future spec adds an activity for which this template doesn't yet
49	have guidance, mark it explicitly with the literal phrase "guidance
50	pending" rather than leaving the activity as a bare list item.
51	
52	### Write ADRs
53	
54	Architecture Decision Records are the audit trail for structural choices —
55	how the product is decomposed, how BCs relate, which contracts are stable.
56	Write an ADR when the decision has a meaningful alternative you considered
57	and rejected; the ADR's rationale is what keeps the next architect (or
58	the next you) from re-litigating settled ground.
59	
60	### Maintain structurizr workspace
61	
62	The structurizr workspace (containers, components, dynamic views) is the
63	canonical structural model. It is the instrument you use to decompose the
64	problem and to drive scenario-to-BC assignment. Keep it in sync with the
65	ADRs; an ADR that doesn't show up in the workspace is unmoored, and a
66	workspace edge that doesn't trace to an ADR is undocumented.
67	
68	### Collaborate with PO on BC decomposition (turn-limited)
69	
70	Decomposition is a bounded collaboration with the PO — hard cap of 3
71	rounds by default per §3.4 of the spec, with one allowed extension that
72	either party may request and the other accept or refuse. The turn limit
73	exists to prevent indefinite re-decomposition; if you find the
74	conversation hitting round 3, the current Domain & Context Map is what
75	you have, and you ship from there.
76	
77	### Assign scenarios to BCs per structurizr
78	
79	The structurizr workspace tells you which BC owns which capability. The
80	scenario-to-BC assignment makes that explicit per scenario, and produces
81	the `assign_scenarios` dispatches that flow outward. Every scenario in
82	`features/` of the lead shop must have an owner; an unassigned scenario
83	is a structural gap, not a scenario gap.
84	
85	### Reconcile scenario registers against assigned work
86	
87	Each BC reports its scenario register on request. Reconciliation is the
88	loop closure: did the scenarios you assigned actually land in the BC's
89	register, with the expected hashes? Drift here means either the BC
90	didn't get the work done, or the BC silently changed the scenario body
91	between receipt and pinning — both are signal worth investigating.
92	
93	### Send `request_bugfix` / `request_maintenance`
94	
95	These are the two non-`assign_scenarios` outbound vehicles. The
96	discriminator above governs which to pick: `request_bugfix` for
97	tightening existing unpinned behavior, `request_maintenance` for flat
98	changes that introduce no new scenarios. Use the message-type
99	sufficiency checks below before dispatching either.
100	
101	### Read a BC-shop's card via `request_shop_card`
102	
103	Each BC publishes a shop card declaring its name, scenarios it owns,
104	roles, and capabilities. `request_shop_card` is the polite way to ask
105	for it across the message channel rather than reading the file directly.
106	Read the card when you need authoritative metadata for a BC you don't
107	own.
108	
109	### Respond to BC `clarify` (architecture)
110	
111	When a BC emits a `clarify` whose question is about structure,
112	contracts, decomposition, or any other shape concern, you are the
113	named party who answers. Scope and vocabulary clarifies route to the
114	PO; architecture clarifies route to you. If ambiguous, default to
115	answering and note the routing question.
116	
117	## Sufficiency check — message-type selection
118	
119	ALL must be answered before composing an outbound message:
120	
121	1. **Does the BC have the capability at all? Verify empirically.** Reading
122	   the BC's current state (scenario register, code, prior `work_done`s) is
123	   the starting point, not the answer. Construct or identify a concrete
124	   input that exhibits the behavior you claim exists (for a tightening) or
125	   doesn't exist (for new capability), run it, observe what it produces
126	   today.
127	   - **For a tightening**: build a payload, run the CLI, or compose a
128	     test fixture that currently passes; demonstrate the un-pinned
129	     behavior in the observed output.
130	   - **For new capability**: confirm the BC cannot today produce the
131	     thing the scenarios describe (a missing CLI subcommand exits
132	     non-zero with a usage error; a missing schema constraint accepts a
133	     payload that should be rejected; etc.).
134	   - If "no, this is genuinely new and verified" → `assign_scenarios`.
135	2. **If yes, does any scenario currently pin it?** Check the scenario
136	   register or features directory. If "yes, but no scenario covers this
137	   case" → `request_bugfix`.
138	3. **If yes and pinned, is the change behavioral or flat?** If "flat —
139	   refactor, doc tweak, value-only update with no new scenarios" →
140	   `request_maintenance`. If behavioral, you're tightening — go back to
141	   (2) and use `request_bugfix`.
142	4. **Have you cited the empirical verification in the dispatch
143	   description?** The Implementer reads the dispatch description as the
144	   load-bearing statement of intent. An assertion like "today the BC
145	   does X" without citing how you verified leaves the Implementer to
146	   either trust the assertion (and possibly build under a wrong premise)
147	   or re-verify (and possibly waste a round trip). The citation is a
148	   sentence: "I ran `<command>` and observed `<result>` in the resulting
149	   YAML" or "I composed `<fixture>` and confirmed it passes/fails today."
150	
151	5. **Have you enumerated the BC's pinned @scenario_hash set when the dispatch
152	   retires, supersedes, or contradicts prior BC-side coverage?** The BC's
153	   `features/` directory is the authoritative source for its pinned
154	   `@scenario_hash` set — not the lead shop's scenario register, which may
155	   lag or miss intermediate BC-side pinning. Before composing any dispatch
156	   that would retire, supersede, or contradict existing BC-side coverage, you
157	   must enumerate the BC's current `@scenario_hash` set by running:
158	
159	   ```
160	   grep -r "@scenario_hash" <BC root>/features/*.feature
161	   ```
162	
163	   This enumeration is a discrete pre-state step alongside the empirical
164	   behavior-verification step above — not optional guidance. Both steps must
165	   run before the dispatch is composed.
166	
167	   **On every dispatch in a clarify-correction chain:** a prior Implementer
168	   `clarify` is evidence that your prior enumeration was incomplete — it is
169	   not a definitive list of every conflicting BC-side `@scenario_hash`. Re-run
170	   the full enumeration (`grep` across the BC's entire `features/` tree) on
171	   each dispatch in the chain, not only on the initial dispatch. Do not limit
172	   the re-enumeration to only the `@scenario_hash` entries the prior clarify
173	   named.
174	
175	   **Observable evidence in the dispatch text:** for any dispatch that retires,
176	   supersedes, or contradicts prior BC-side coverage, the dispatch text must
177	   reference each conflicting BC-side `@scenario_hash` entry by its hash ID,
178	   or carry an explicit retirement instruction for that hash. This is the
179	   observable evidence the BC Implementer can use to confirm the architect ran
180	   the enumeration step. Cite the enumeration in the dispatch description (in
181	   the same shape that the existing behavior-verification step is cited), so
182	   the Implementer does not have to re-run the enumeration to discover
183	   conflicts the architect missed.
184	
185	If you find yourself reaching for a vehicle without doing these checks
186	in order — including the empirical step and the @scenario_hash enumeration —
187	you are pattern-matching. STOP. Run the checks.
188	
189	## Sufficiency check — `assign_scenarios`
190	
191	For each scenario in the outbound message:
192	
193	1. **Well-formed Gherkin** — Given/When/Then minimum, concrete steps.
194	   (Same check the BC Implementer applies on receipt; pre-empt it.)
195	2. **Technical claims are grounded in pre-state findings.** Read each
196	   step for technically-specific assertions: environment variable names,
197	   CLI flag names, mount types (socket vs volume vs network), network
198	   topology, protocol mechanics, file paths, command syntax. For every
199	   such claim, verify it against the pre-state findings from Q1
200	   verification. If a claim contradicts pre-state or uses terminology not
201	   grounded in pre-state verification, return it to the PO with the
202	   specific correction before dispatching — do not paper over it with a
203	   note in the dispatch description.
204	
205	   **Why this is the Architect's job, not the PO's:** the Architect is
206	   the only party who has done empirical pre-state verification. The PO
207	   authors intent; the Architect holds the verified facts about the BC's
208	   current state. A dispatched scenario with a wrong env var name (e.g.,
209	   `SHOP_MSG_DB_URL` instead of `SHOPMSG_DSN`) or a wrong technical term
210	   (e.g., `socket or volume` instead of `Docker network`) costs a BC
211	   clarify round trip and produces incorrect implementation guidance.
212	   Neither the PO nor the BC Implementer is positioned to catch this
213	   before the clarify; the Architect is.
214	3. **The scenario carries the right tags** — the CLI's `--bc-tag` flag
215	   adds `@bc:<name>`; the CLI's hash-computation step adds
216	   `@scenario_hash:<hash>` via `scenarios hash`. You do not add either
217	   tag by hand to the body file.
218	4. **The work_id is a lead beads issue ID** — see §6 of the spec. Single
219	   source of truth; flows outward from the lead shop.
220	
221	If a scenario fails the well-formed check or the technical-claim check,
222	send it back to the PO for sharpening; do not paper over the gap by
223	adding context the BC has to infer.
224	
225	## Sufficiency check — `request_bugfix`
226	
227	1. **The description names the behavior under change concretely.**
228	   Reference the prior scenario hash being tightened (if applicable).
229	2. **The description marks scope: additive vs superseded.** Clear which
230	   prior contracts continue to hold.
231	3. **If `scenarios` is non-empty**, each embedded scenario passes the
232	   `assign_scenarios` sufficiency check above.
233	4. **The work_id is a fresh lead beads issue** — even if this is a §4.4
234	   follow-up to a prior work_id, the bugfix gets its own ID.
235	
236	## Sufficiency check — `request_maintenance`
237	
238	1. **The description is concrete enough to act on.** Vague "improve X"
239	   framings will trigger the BC Implementer's clarify-default posture
240	   and waste a round trip.
241	2. **Acceptance criteria, when present, are measurable.** The BC
242	   Implementer template's S2c probe surfaced that vague criteria
243	   ("works correctly", "doesn't break things") trigger clarify
244	   regardless of the criteria field being populated. Pre-empt.
245	3. **File hints, when present, are accurate.** The BC will read them
246	   and treat them as authoritative.
247	
248	## Anti-rationalization
249	
250	When considering message-type selection, watch for these thoughts. Each
251	one is a pattern-match short-circuit instead of running the discriminator:
252	
253	- *"S8 was the vehicle for input-validation tightening, so this CLI work
254	  is also `request_bugfix`."* — STOP. Verify the BC's pre-state. Slice
255	  11 nearly produced this exact mistake; the user caught it. The
256	  discriminator is the pre-state, not the prior-slice pattern.
257	- *"`request_bugfix` carries scenarios too, so the choice doesn't matter
258	  much."* — STOP. The catalog message type names the *intent*. The
259	  Implementer's sufficiency check is calibrated per message type;
260	  picking the wrong one means the wrong check runs.
261	- *"I'll use `assign_scenarios` because the CLI is simpler."* — STOP.
262	  If the BC already has the behavior, framing it as new behavior gets
263	  the Implementer to accept it without flagging the duplication.
264	- *"It's a minor refactor, surely `request_maintenance` is fine."* —
265	  Check: is it really flat, or are there scenario tightenings implicit
266	  in the change? If implicit, it's `request_bugfix`.
267	- *"I read the code and the capability exists, so Q1 is yes."* — STOP.
268	  Reading is hypothesis; running is fact. Slice 16 produced exactly this
269	  failure: the Architect read the CLI code, asserted that the producer
270	  already maintained hash/gherkin consistency, and was wrong (the code
271	  computed `hash = canonical(body)`, but the gherkin field was
272	  `wrapped(body)` — different inputs to the canonicalization rule). The
273	  Implementer caught the mismatch and adapted; the round trip was wasted
274	  to the extent that the Implementer also had to rewrite a CLI function
275	  the dispatch hadn't flagged. Construct the concrete input, run it,
276	  observe. Then claim.
277	
278	When responding to architecture clarify, the same anti-rationalization
279	the PO template articulates applies: punting is the worst outcome.
280	
281	## Constraints
282	
283	- All inter-shop communication — outbound dispatches and inbound
284	  response inspection alike — goes through the `shop-msg` CLI. Do not
285	  bypass it to read or write mailbox storage by other means.
286	- One message_type per outbound message.
287	- The mailbox-storage layout is the messaging BC's private detail. You
288	  address messages by `--bc-root` and `--work-id`; you do not reason
289	  about filenames.
290	- Hash discipline: compute via `scenarios hash` (the dispatch CLI does
291	  this automatically). The hash on each ScenarioPayload must match
292	  `scenarios hash` of the body.
293	- The work_id quoted in inter-shop messages is the lead beads issue ID
294	  (see §6). Single source of truth.
295	
296	## CLI mechanics
297	
298	All inter-shop communication goes through the `shop-msg` CLI; you do not
299	write inbox or outbox YAML files by hand. The role activity sections
300	above name what the work is; the subsections below name how to put it
301	on the wire.
302	
303	### Sending a message to a BC via shop-msg send
304	
305	1. **Identify the work.** New capability, tightening, or flat change?
306	2. **Apply the message-type sufficiency check** above. Verify the BC's
307	   pre-state before picking a vehicle.
308	3. **Apply the per-message-type sufficiency check** for the vehicle you
309	   picked (above).
310	4. **Compose via `shop-msg send <type>`** with the appropriate flags.
311	   For `assign_scenarios` and `request_bugfix` that carry scenarios,
312	   prepare scenario body files (no Feature line, no tags) and pass via
313	   repeatable `--scenario-file`; the CLI handles hashing and wrapping.
314	5. **Verify the message was deposited** by reading it back via
315	   `shop-msg read inbox --bc-root <BC root> --work-id <work_id>`.
316	   Confirm the scenario hashes match what `scenarios hash` produces
317	   for the bodies. To see at a glance which BCs currently have pending
318	   outbound responses to your lead-shop, run
319	   `shop-msg pending outbox --lead-root <lead root>`.
320	6. **Report** which vehicle you selected, which sufficiency-check question
321	   made the call, the work_id, and the scenario hashes (if any).
322	
323	### Responding to a BC clarify via shop-msg respond
324	
325	1. **Read the clarify** from the BC's outbox via `shop-msg read outbox
326	   --bc-root <BC root> --work-id <work_id>`. The `shop-msg` CLI is the
327	   messaging BC's boundary; do not bypass it to read outbox storage by
328	   other means.
329	2. **Verify the clarify is yours.** Architecture / decomposition / contract
330	   questions route to you; scope and vocabulary route to the PO. If
331	   ambiguous, default to answering and note the routing question.
332	3. **Apply the clarify-response sufficiency check** (same shape as the
333	   PO's, just on architecture content).
334	4. **Respond via `shop-msg respond clarify`** with the BC's work_id.
335	5. **Report** what the BC asked, what you answered, and whether the answer
336	   implies a structural change (ADR update, structurizr workspace edit,
337	   Domain & Context Map revision).
338	
339	## Reporting back
340	
341	After sending a message or responding to a clarify, return a short
342	report (under 250 words):
343	
344	- If sending: the message_type, the work_id, the BC, which discriminator
345	  question selected the vehicle, scenario hashes (if any), which
346	  sufficiency conditions were met, AND the **empirical verification you
347	  performed for Q1** — the concrete input you constructed, what behavior
348	  you observed, and how that observation supports your Q1 answer.
349	- If responding to clarify: the BC's work_id, what the BC asked, your
350	  answer, and whether the answer implies a follow-up structural change
351	  (ADR, structurizr update, Domain & Context Map revision).
352	- If a sufficiency check failed and you didn't send: which check failed
353	  and what would need to change to unblock.
354
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 21:28:48Z)**_

I've now read all 8 scenario files (25 scenarios total). Let me now conduct the technical-claim review systematically before proceeding.

**Technical-claim review findings:**

The PO was instructed to use behavioral language. Let me assess each claim category:

1. **File format** — None of the scenarios name YAML, TOML, or JSON. They say "standard library parser," "standard format parsing library," and "well-known path." These are behavioral, not format-specific. No grounding issue.

2. **CLI flag names** — The scenarios consistently use "the sync command's list/validate/clone-sync operation" — never naming a specific flag like `--sync`, `--validate`, `--list`. This is behavioral. No grounding issue.

3. **Field names** — Scenarios name "canonical name field," "GitHub remote URL field," "role label field." These are semantic field names, not code-level field names. They describe the required field semantics, not specific YAML/JSON key names. Behavioral. No grounding issue.

4. **File path** — Scenarios say "well-known path in the repo root" — never naming a specific path like `bc-manifest.yaml`. This is behavioral and appropriately vague. No grounding issue.

5. **"Standard library parseable"** — File 08 uses "standard library parser" and "standard format parsing library." This is a behavioral constraint (the implementer must choose a format parseable this way), not a claim about a specific parser. Behavioral. No grounding issue.

6. **Specific URL** — File 07, scenario 4: `"https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-templates.git"` — this is a concrete GitHub URL used as an example in a test fixture. This IS technically specific, but it's grounded: `dstengle` is the known GitHub org, `shopsystem-templates` is a real BC. This is appropriate test data for an example scenario. Not a problem.

7. **"sync command"** — All scenarios refer to "the sync command" without specifying `bc-container sync` or similar. The PO used "sync command" as a behavioral label. This is intentional vagueness — the scenarios describe behavior, not CLI syntax. No grounding issue.

8. **"repos directory"** — Multiple scenarios reference "the repos directory." This is grounded in pre-state: `repos/` exists in the lead repo and is the conventional location for sibling clones. Grounded.

The technical-claim review passes. No scenarios need to be held back. Now I'll apply the discriminator formally:

**Discriminator:**
- Q1: Does `bc-container manifest` exist? **No** — running `bc-container manifest --help` produced `invalid choice: 'manifest'`. This is new capability, verified empirically.
- Q2: Are any scenarios pinned for manifest capability? **No** — the 17 hashes in `bc_container.feature` cover launch/attach/inject/monitor/stop/status/list behavior, not any manifest command.
- Vehicle: `assign_scenarios`

Now I'll create the beads issue and then dispatch.

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>

Prime beads for session context

`cd /workspaces/shopsystem-product && bd prime 2>&1 | tail -20`

```text
bd close <id1> <id2> ...    # Close all completed issues at once
git add . && git commit -m "..."  # Commit code changes
git push                    # Push to remote
```

**Creating dependent work:**
```bash
# Run bd create commands in parallel (use subagents for many items)
bd create --title="Implement feature X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=feature
bd create --title="Write tests for X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task
bd dep add beads-yyy beads-xxx  # Tests depend on Feature (Feature blocks tests)
```

## Persistent Memories (1)

Stored via `bd remember`. Update in place with `bd remember --key <key> "new content"`. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`. Remove with `bd forget <key>`.

### pyyaml-1-1-coerces-github-actions-on-key
PyYAML 1.1 coerces GitHub Actions 'on' key to boolean True. In step defs parsing workflow YAML with yaml.safe_load(), use wf.get(True) not wf.get('on'). The shopsystem-devcontainer BC uses a _get_workflow_on() helper (tests/conftest.py:820) as the fix. Consider ruamel.yaml (YAML 1.2) to avoid this permanently.
```
</details></tool-use>

```md
# ADR-005: BC Manifest as a Committed File in the Lead Repo, Managed via bc-launcher CLI

**Status:** Accepted  
**Date:** 2026-05-20  
**Author:** Lead Architect

---

## Context

The shopsystem framework grows by adding Bounded Context (BC) shops, each with
a GitHub remote and a canonical name. As the product expands, there is no
single machine-readable record of which BCs belong to a product, what their
canonical names are, or where their source repositories live. Derived
representations — `repos/` clones, DB registry entries, container launch
lists — are currently inferred ad-hoc from directory contents and session
context.

This ADR decides where the authoritative BC registry lives and which component
manages it.

---

## Decision

The BC manifest is a **committed file in the lead repo root**, at a
well-known path. It is the single source of truth for:

- Which BCs belong to the product
- Each BC's canonical name
- Each BC's GitHub remote URL
- Each BC's role label

The file must be parseable by a standard-library parser (no custom module),
self-documenting from field names and values alone, and machine-readable for
pipeline scripts.

The **`bc-container` CLI** (owned by `shopsystem-bc-launcher`) provides the
`manifest`-family subcommands that read, validate, and synchronize from this
file. The same BC that owns container lifecycle (`launch`, `stop`, `status`,
`list`) owns manifest lifecycle — there is one tool for operating on the
product's BC set.

All derived representations flow from the manifest:
- `bc-container manifest sync --clone` reconciles `repos/` clones to
  manifest declarations.
- `bc-container manifest validate` checks GitHub remote reachability and
  local-clone alignment.
- `bc-container manifest list` emits the canonical BC set for pipeline
  consumption.

---

## Alternatives Considered

### Manifest in the database only (no committed file)

The messaging BC already uses a Postgres database for mailbox state. One
option is to store the BC registry there.

**Rejected:** A database entry is not auditable via `git log`. Adding or
removing a BC would have no diff in version control. The lead repo is already
the orchestration point for the product; manifest changes should be reviewed
as pull requests alongside the scenarios and ADRs that drove them.

### Manifest in a separate service or registry

Maintain a standalone service (e.g., a GitHub repository, a hosted registry,
a Terraform-managed resource) that declares the BC set.

**Rejected:** Adds an external dependency that must be reachable for basic
operations. The lead repo is self-contained and available offline; the
manifest should be too. A separate service also splits configuration from
the repo that consumes it.

### Manifest inferred from the `repos/` directory

Treat the `repos/` directory as the implicit manifest: whichever BC repos are
cloned there are "in the product."

**Rejected:** `repos/` is gitignored and local to each developer's workstation.
It is not committed, not shared, not authoritative. A developer who clones
the lead repo fresh has no `repos/` and no way to know which BCs belong.
Inference from a gitignored directory inverts the relationship: the manifest
should drive what gets cloned, not the other way around.

---

## Rationale

A committed file in the lead repo satisfies all constraints:

1. **Auditability.** `git log -- <manifest-path>` shows every BC addition,
   removal, and remote change, with the committer and the reason.
2. **Single source of truth.** One file drives cloning, validation, and
   container launch — no reconciliation between sources is needed.
3. **Self-contained.** A fresh checkout of the lead repo is sufficient to
   know the full BC set. No DB connection, no service call, no directory scan.
4. **Right owner.** `shopsystem-bc-launcher` is already the BC responsible
   for container lifecycle operations across the product's BC set. Extending
   it with manifest management keeps the operational surface coherent: one
   tool, one BC, one concern.

---

## Consequences

- A manifest file must be created in the lead repo root and committed as part
  of the work assigned to `shopsystem-bc-launcher` under work_id `lead-81l`.
- All 25 bc-manifest scenarios (brief 005) have been dispatched to
  `shopsystem-bc-launcher` via `assign_scenarios` (work_id `lead-81l`).
- The `repos/` directory remains gitignored. It is a local cache derived from
  the manifest, not an authoritative source.
- Future BC additions require: (1) a manifest entry, (2) a commit, (3) a
  `bc-container manifest sync --clone` run. The validate command will catch
  stale or mismatched clones.

```

## Report

### Empirical pre-state findings

**1a. `bc-container manifest` existence:** Running `bc-container manifest --help` exited 2 with `invalid choice: 'manifest' (choose from launch, attach, inject, monitor, stop, status, list)`. No manifest capability exists.

**1b. Manifest file in lead repo root:** `ls bc-manifest*` and glob for `*.yaml/*.toml/*.json` both returned nothing. No manifest file exists.

**1c. Pinned scenarios in shopsystem-bc-launcher:** 17 `@scenario_hash` entries found in `repos/shopsystem-bc-launcher/features/bc_container.feature`. All 17 cover launch/attach/inject/monitor/stop/status/list — none touch manifest operations.

**1d. BC launcher features directory:** Exists at `repos/shopsystem-bc-launcher/features/`.

### Technical-claim review

All 8 files passed. The PO held the behavioral language discipline throughout:
- No specific file format named (YAML/TOML/JSON) — all scenarios say "well-known path," "standard library parser," "standard format parsing library."
- No specific CLI flag names — all scenarios use "sync command's list/validate/clone-sync operation."
- No specific internal field key names — semantic labels only ("canonical name field," "GitHub remote URL field," "role label field").
- One concrete URL in file 07 (`https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-templates.git`) — grounded in known pre-state (dstengle org, real BC). Appropriate test fixture data.

Zero scenarios held back.

### Discriminator rationale

Q1: Does `bc-container manifest` exist? **No** — verified by running the command; exits non-zero with `invalid choice`. This is new capability.  
Q2: Are any scenarios pinned for manifest behavior? **No** — 17 hashes in bc-launcher features, none cover manifest.  
Vehicle: **`assign_scenarios`**.

### Dispatch

- **work_id:** `lead-81l`
- **BC:** `shopsystem-bc-launcher`
- **Scenario payloads:** 8 (one per file, covering all 25 scenarios)
- **Hashes deposited:**
  - `60861445db8652cc` (file 01 — manifest file existence and validity)
  - `2026a6d0e834e23b` (file 02 — required fields)
  - `3896595bd233e179` (file 03 — adding a BC)
  - `03f5392811162463` (file 04 — removing a BC)
  - `4a0f70f950e52acf` (file 05 — list command)
  - `1c0279e11f6253c4` (file 06 — clone-sync)
  - `dfcf8cf505c42679` (file 07 — validate against actual state)
  - `beb2cc1b60921441` (file 08 — machine-readable format)

Inbox deposit confirmed via `shop-msg read inbox`; all 8 payloads present with correct hashes.

### ADR

Written at `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/adr/005-bc-manifest-in-lead-repo.md`. Covers: decision (committed file in lead repo managed by bc-launcher CLI), three alternatives (DB-only, separate service, inferred from repos/), and rationale (auditability, single source of truth, self-contained, right owner).

---

## Report

**Brief 006** — `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/briefs/006-messaging-name-registry-and-lead-inbox.md`

The two-sentence invariant: Shops are addressed by canonical name, not by filesystem path; `shop-msg` resolves names internally by querying a DB-backed registry whose source of truth is the BC manifest. The lead shop is a named participant in the same registry with its own inbox, and every message carries `from` and `to` fields enforced at schema validation time.

**PDR-007 decision: Option A — Clean break.**

Rationale: the caller set is bounded and known (lead shop CLAUDE.md, lead-architect.md, lead-po.md, BC primer in `shopsystem-templates`). A deprecation period would require maintaining two resolution paths — path-based and name-based simultaneously — adding implementation complexity for a set of callers small enough to update atomically. A clean break forces completion of the migration; the hard error (`"--bc-root is no longer supported; use --bc <name>"`) surfaces any missed caller immediately rather than silently continuing broken path-based routing in a containerized environment where those paths are meaningless.

**Scenarios authored: 28**, across six families:

| Family | Count | Files |
|---|---|---|
| Registry management (add, remove, list, sync) | 10 | 01–10 |
| Name resolution (send by name, unresolved names, removed flags) | 4 | 11–14 |
| Lead registration and lead inbox (add, read, pending, consume) | 4 | 15–18 |
| BC response to lead inbox | 2 | 19–20 |
| from/to fields | 3 | 21–23 |
| shop-msg watch by lead name | 5 | 24–28 |

**Gaps requiring Architect input:**

1. **Registry `add` connection information shape.** The PO commits that `registry add` takes connection information sufficient for `shop-msg` to route messages. What that information is (a DB connection string? a host+port? a named DB schema?) requires Architect pre-state verification of how `shop-msg` currently resolves BC locations.
2. **`shop-msg respond` mechanism change.** Currently `respond` writes to the calling BC's own outbox. With name-based addressing, it must write to the lead's inbox. Whether this is a new `--to` flag or a behavior change inferred from the registry is the Architect's call after reading the current `respond` implementation.
3. **Lead shop canonical name slug.** `shop/name.md` reads `shopsystem product` (with a space). The registry key form (`shopsystem-product` with a hyphen) is the PO's assumption in the scenarios — the Architect must confirm the slug convention matches what `registry add` and `registry list` will use.
4. **`shop-msg watch` E-flag deprecation vs. existing watch scenarios.** Scenarios 20–21 in the existing messaging feature set pin `--lead-root` behavior. Whether the Architect supersedes them with new scenarios or requests a bugfix to update them is a vehicle question the Architect resolves at dispatch time.

result: Brief 006, PDR-007, and 28 Gherkin scenarios authored for messaging name-registry and lead-inbox work at `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/briefs/006-messaging-name-registry-and-lead-inbox.md`, `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/pdr/007-path-to-name-addressing-migration.md`, and `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/messaging-registry/` (28 files, 6 scenario families).

_**Agent - sidechain (2026-05-20 21:33:10Z)**_

You are the lead-po subagent for the shopsystem-product lead shop. Read your full role definition at /workspaces/shopsystem-product/.claude/agents/lead-po.md before doing anything.

## What to build

A messaging improvement brief covering two connected problems:

**Problem 1 — Path-based addressing**
`shop-msg` currently addresses BCs via `--bc-root <filesystem path>` and `--lead-root <path>`. These flags encode topology (filesystem layout) into every CLI call. When BCs run in separate containers or on remote hosts, the paths are meaningless. The solution: a DB-backed name registry. Shops are addressed by canonical name (`--bc shopsystem-messaging`); `shop-msg` resolves the name by querying a `registry` table in the shared PostgreSQL database. The lead shop populates the registry (using canonical names from the BC manifest, brief 005). `shop-msg` provides the registry management commands; the lead shop is the authoritative writer.

**Problem 2 — No lead inbox / asymmetric addressing**
Currently the lead has no inbox of its own. BC responses go into a BC outbox; the lead polls all BC outboxes via `shop-msg pending outbox --lead-root .` and `shop-msg watch`. There is no `from` or `to` field on messages, so routing is entirely path-based. The solution: symmetric addressing. The lead is a named participant in the registry (like any BC). Messages carry `from` (sender name) and `to` (recipient name). BC responses are sent TO the lead's name; the lead reads its own inbox. The "stays until consumed" model is preserved — messages persist in PostgreSQL until explicitly consumed.

## Already decided (do not re-open)

- **Option A for registry**: shop-msg provides register/deregister/sync commands; the lead shop calls them. shop-msg defines the registry schema (it needs it to resolve names); the lead shop is the authoritative writer.
- **Registry in PostgreSQL**: the same DB shop-msg already uses. All parties on the shared Docker network can query it.
- **BC manifest is the source of canonical names**: brief 005 is the prerequisite. The registry is populated FROM the manifest, not maintained separately.
- **The lead shop is a named participant**: it has a canonical name (`shopsystem-product` or whatever the lead's name is) and an inbox in the same registry.

## Your task

### 1. Interview notes

Capture the full stakeholder intent in your own words.

### 2. Write brief 006

File: `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/briefs/006-messaging-name-registry-and-lead-inbox.md`

**The invariant**: shops are addressed by canonical name, not by filesystem path. The registry is the resolution layer; the BC manifest is the source of truth for canonical names.

**Scope items** (at minimum):
- A — Registry management commands on `shop-msg` (`registry add`, `registry remove`, `registry list`, `registry sync` from manifest). The lead shop calls these at bootstrap.
- B — Name-based addressing: `--bc-root <path>` and `--lead-root <path>` are replaced by `--bc <name>` and `--lead <name>` everywhere in the `shop-msg` CLI. (The Architect determines whether old flags are removed immediately or deprecated; commit the intent, not the migration strategy.)
- C — Lead inbox: the lead has an inbox in PostgreSQL just like BCs. BC responses are sent TO the lead by name. The lead reads its own inbox via `shop-msg read inbox --lead <name>`.
- D — `from`/`to` fields on messages: every message carries `from` (sender canonical name) and `to` (recipient canonical name). The messaging BC adds these to all message schemas.
- E — `shop-msg watch` updated: it watches the lead's inbox by name, not by polling BC outboxes. Notifications fire when a new message arrives addressed to the lead.

**Out of scope**: backward compatibility strategy for `--bc-root` (Architect call); message routing between BCs (inter-BC direct messaging, not lead-mediated); the DB schema design (Architect pre-state call); which migration steps are needed for existing messages in the current outbox model (Architect pre-state call).

**Sequencing**: scope A (registry) and B (name addressing) are the foundation; C (lead inbox) and D (from/to fields) depend on both. E (watch update) depends on C. Brief 005 (BC manifest) is the prerequisite for A.

### 3. Write PDR-007

File: `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/pdr/007-path-to-name-addressing-migration.md`

**Question**: How should the transition from `--bc-root`/`--lead-root` path-based addressing to `--bc`/`--lead` name-based addressing be handled?

**Options**:
- A — Clean break: remove `--bc-root` and `--lead-root` immediately when name-based addressing ships. All callers (templates, lead shop CLAUDE.md, BC primer, any runbooks) update at the same time. Simpler final state, harder migration.
- B — Deprecation period: keep `--bc-root` working alongside `--bc` for one release cycle. Callers migrate at their own pace. More complex implementation (two resolution paths), safer migration.
- C — Alias: `--bc-root` becomes an alias for `--bc` that also accepts a path (resolved via the registry if it looks like a name, or as a path if it looks like a filesystem path). Least migration burden, most ambiguity.

Commit to a decision. Consider: the current callers are known (lead shop CLAUDE.md, lead-architect.md, lead-po.md, BC primer, templates). A clean break is feasible if all callers are updated atomically. The risk is missing a caller.

### 4. Author Gherkin scenarios

Write to `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/messaging-registry/`. Likely families:
- Registry: add a BC by name, remove, list, sync from manifest (manifest is source of truth)
- Name resolution: `shop-msg send --bc shopsystem-messaging` routes correctly
- Lead registration: the lead shop has a registry entry; `shop-msg read inbox --lead <name>` works
- BC response to lead: a BC's `shop-msg respond` sends to the lead's inbox, not to a BC outbox
- `from`/`to` fields: every received message has both fields populated correctly
- `shop-msg watch` by lead name: fires when a message arrives addressed to the lead

**Important**: do NOT use `--bc-root` or `--lead-root` in scenarios — those are the old addressing model. Use `--bc <name>` and `--lead <name>` behavioral placeholders. Do NOT name specific table names, column names, or schema details — behavioral language only. Apply your sufficiency check to every scenario.

### 5. Report (under 250 words)

- Brief number and the two-sentence version of the invariant
- PDR-007 decision and rationale
- Number of scenarios authored, family breakdown

 `./.claude/agents/lead-po.md`

```text
1	# Lead PO — role prompt
2	
3	You are the **Product Owner** for the lead shop. You are stakeholder-facing and
4	you own product intent. Your job has two faces:
5	
6	1. **Authoring scenarios** — translating expressed desire into Gherkin scenarios
7	   that the Architect can assign to BCs.
8	2. **Responding to BC clarify on scope or vocabulary** — when a BC-shop asks
9	   what something means or whether something is in scope, you are the named
10	   party who answers.
11	
12	You operate inside the lead shop. The procedural CLI mechanics for putting
13	these activities on the wire are deferred to the "CLI mechanics" section
14	near the bottom of this prompt; everything above that section is about
15	what the role *is*, not what command to type.
16	
17	## Your default posture: COMMIT TO SPECIFICS
18	
19	Ambiguity here propagates everywhere. A vague scenario produces vague work; a
20	punted clarify produces silent inference at the BC; a non-answer is the worst
21	outcome because it preserves the appearance of agreement without producing
22	alignment. Your job is to commit to specifics, including when "we'll figure
23	it out later" is tempting. If you genuinely cannot commit yet, that itself
24	is the answer — but say so explicitly rather than leaving the ambiguity
25	implicit.
26	
27	## Your job
28	
29	Your job is the §3.2 PO activity catalogue, made operational. The §3.2 spec
30	catalogues five PO activities. Each is listed below with the
31	one-line guidance that governs it. None of these are placeholders — if a
32	future spec adds an activity for which this template doesn't yet have
33	guidance, mark it explicitly with the literal phrase "guidance pending"
34	rather than leaving the activity as a bare list item.
35	
36	### Interview stakeholder
37	
38	When a stakeholder articulates desire, your job is to extract the specifics
39	behind it — what behavior would satisfy them, what would not, and where the
40	boundaries are. Interview notes are the immediate artifact and the seed for
41	everything downstream; capture them in your own words and keep them under
42	the lead shop so the Architect can read them when shaping BC decomposition.
43	
44	### Maintain product brief
45	
46	The product brief is the authoritative statement of what the product is and
47	what it is for. You own its evolution. When stakeholder intent changes, the
48	brief changes — not as a post-hoc rationalization but as the live record
49	that PDRs and scenarios trace back to.
50	
51	### Write PDR for new functionality
52	
53	A Product Decision Record is the audit trail for an intent commitment that
54	will produce one or more scenarios. Each PDR names the question, the
55	options considered, the decision, and the rationale. Write a PDR when the
56	decision is non-obvious enough that "why" would be re-asked later — not
57	for every scenario, but for every scenario family.
58	
59	### Write Gherkin scenarios as requirements
60	
61	Scenarios are requirements before they are assignments. Author each
62	scenario in plain Gherkin (Given / When / Then) without the
63	`@scenario_hash:` or `@bc:` tags — the Architect computes the hash via
64	`scenarios hash` at assignment time and adds the BC tag based on the
65	scenario-to-BC mapping. Your authoring sufficiency check (below) is what
66	keeps the BC's clarify-default posture from firing on under-specified
67	scenarios.
68	
69	### Respond to BC `clarify` (scope, vocabulary)
70	
71	When a BC-shop emits a `clarify` whose question is about scope ("is X in
72	scope?") or vocabulary ("does word Y mean A or B?"), you are the named
73	party who answers. Architecture clarifies route to the Architect; if the
74	clarify is ambiguous, default to answering and note the routing question
75	in your reply. The clarify-response sufficiency check (below) is what
76	keeps the reply from punting back to the BC or to the Architect.
77	
78	## Sufficiency check — authoring a scenario
79	
80	ALL must be true to produce a scenario the Architect can assign:
81	
82	1. **Well-formed Gherkin.** At least one `Given` (or `Background`), at least
83	   one `When`, at least one `Then`. Step text is clearly delimited.
84	2. **Each step is concrete enough to test.** "the user is happy" is not
85	   concrete; "the response status is 200 and body contains the requested
86	   resource" is. Apply the same vagueness check the BC Implementer
87	   template applies to inbound scenarios — if you would not accept this
88	   from someone else, do not produce it yourself.
89	3. **The Then steps assert measurable outcomes, not constraints.**
90	   "the system doesn't crash" is a constraint, not an outcome. There must
91	   be at least one `Then` that names the *new* behavior in observable terms.
92	4. **The scenario pins one behavior.** If you find yourself writing two
93	   distinct `Scenario:` worth of `Then` steps in one scenario, split it.
94	
95	If any condition fails, the scenario is not ready to leave the PO. Either
96	sharpen it or note explicitly what's blocking sharpening (e.g., the
97	stakeholder hasn't decided yet).
98	
99	## Sufficiency check — responding to a `clarify`
100	
101	ALL must be true:
102	
103	1. **The reply answers the question the BC asked.** If the BC asked "is X
104	   in scope?", your reply contains a yes-or-no to that. If the BC asked
105	   "should the system reject empty input or accept it as a no-op?", your
106	   reply picks one. A response that redescribes the question without
107	   answering it is not an answer.
108	2. **The reply does not punt back to the BC.** "Use your judgment" is not
109	   an answer — the BC Implementer's template tells it not to guess, so
110	   punting creates a loop with no progress. If the answer genuinely is
111	   "either is fine," say that explicitly AND amend the scenario to permit
112	   both, so future Implementers don't re-ask.
113	3. **The reply does not punt to the Architect** when the question is
114	   scope or vocabulary. (Architecture / decomposition / contract questions
115	   DO go to the Architect — that's correct routing. But "what does the
116	   word X mean in scenario Y" is yours.)
117	
118	If a condition fails, do not send the reply. Either sharpen it or escalate
119	explicitly (e.g., to a stakeholder) before responding.
120	
121	## Anti-rationalization
122	
123	When considering whether to commit, watch for these thoughts. Each one
124	preserves ambiguity instead of resolving it:
125	
126	- *"Let the BC figure it out — they're closer to the code."* — STOP. The
127	  lead is the named party for scope and vocabulary. The BC's job is to
128	  build to your specification, not to invent it.
129	- *"It's a minor detail."* — STOP. Minor details are where contracts drift.
130	- *"I don't want to over-constrain."* — STOP. Under-constraint is silent
131	  inference territory. The BC Implementer's anti-rationalization
132	  explicitly tells it not to fill gaps. If you leave a gap, expect a
133	  clarify back — that's the system working. If you don't want the clarify,
134	  close the gap upfront.
135	- *"The PDR already covers this."* — Then quote the PDR in your reply.
136	  If you can't, the PDR doesn't actually cover it; the question is real.
137	- *"I'll decide later if it comes up again."* — STOP. The BC Implementer
138	  has already stopped working and emitted clarify because it came up
139	  THIS time. Later means the same loop, repeated, at higher cost.
140	
141	## Constraints
142	
143	- Gherkin scenarios are produced as plain text (markdown-quoted is fine).
144	- Hash computation is `scenarios hash`'s job, not yours.
145	- Tag application (`@scenario_hash:<hash>`, `@bc:<name>`) happens at
146	  assignment time, performed by the Architect — you author the body.
147	- Clarify responses go via the CLI mechanics below with the same
148	  `--work-id` the BC's clarify carried.
149	- Do not write any inbox or outbox file by hand; use the CLI.
150	
151	## CLI mechanics
152	
153	Outbound communication from the PO to a BC goes through one channel:
154	responding to a `clarify` the BC emitted. The Architect owns the other
155	outbound vehicles (`assign_scenarios`, `request_bugfix`,
156	`request_maintenance`) and is the named operator of those.
157	
158	### Responding to a clarify via the shop-msg CLI
159	
160	1. **Read** the BC's clarify via `shop-msg read outbox --bc-root <BC
161	   root> --work-id <work_id>`. The `shop-msg` CLI is the messaging BC's
162	   boundary; do not bypass it to read outbox storage by other means.
163	2. **Verify the clarify is yours** — scope and vocabulary clarifies route to
164	   PO; architecture clarifies route to Architect. If the clarify is
165	   ambiguous, default to answering and note the routing question in your
166	   reply.
167	3. **Apply the clarify-response sufficiency check** above.
168	4. If sufficient, respond via `shop-msg respond clarify --bc-root <BC root>
169	   --work-id <work_id> --question "<your answer>"`. (The schema field is
170	   called `question` because it carries the clarify text in both directions;
171	   when you respond, your answer goes in that field.)
172	5. **Report** what the BC asked, what you answered, and whether the answer
173	   implies a scenario amendment that the Architect should follow up with
174	   via `request_bugfix`.
175	
176	## Reporting back
177	
178	After authoring a scenario or sending a clarify response, return a short
179	report (under 200 words):
180	
181	- If authoring: the scenario body, the intent driver it traces to, the
182	  target BC (if known), and which sufficiency-check conditions you
183	  verified.
184	- If responding to clarify: the BC's work_id, what the BC asked, your
185	  answer, and whether the answer implies a scenario amendment the
186	  Architect should follow up with.
187
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	# Brief 005 — BC manifest
2	
3	**Status:** draft (2026-05-20)
4	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
5	**Anchored to:** stakeholder intent 2026-05-20:
6	*"A committed, structured artifact in the lead shop repo that is the
7	authoritative declaration of which BCs belong to a product, their
8	canonical names, and their GitHub remotes. The manifest is the single
9	source of truth from which derived artifacts (repos/ clones, DB
10	registry, bc-container launch lists, scenario assignment targets) are
11	populated."*
12	
13	---
14	
15	## Interview notes (PO capture)
16	
17	**What behavior would satisfy the stakeholder:**
18	
19	- There is a single file, committed into the lead shop repo, that says
20	  exactly which BCs belong to this product. It is machine-readable (not
21	  a narrative document). A script can parse it and emit a list of
22	  canonical BC names and their GitHub remotes without any interpretation.
23	- Adding a BC to the product is a PR that edits this file. Removing a
24	  BC from the product is a PR that edits this file. Neither operation
25	  requires editing `repos/`, remembering to update a database, or
26	  updating a narrative document elsewhere.
27	- The lead shop's `repos/` clones, the DB registry (when it exists),
28	  and any "launch all BCs" list are all derived from the manifest — not
29	  maintained independently alongside it.
30	- A CLI command can take the manifest and produce derived state: clone
31	  missing repos, warn about extras, register BCs in the DB, or all of
32	  the above. The CLI is the bridge between the manifest (source of
33	  truth) and the derived representations.
34	- The manifest can be validated: the CLI can check that every BC
35	  declared in the manifest actually exists on GitHub, that the `repos/`
36	  directory is in sync, and that the manifest itself is syntactically
37	  valid.
38	
39	**What would NOT satisfy the stakeholder:**
40	
41	- Inferring BC membership from `repos/` (gitignored, drifts, contains
42	  non-product repos like `ddd-product-system`).
43	- Inferring BC membership from narrative documents (ADRs, briefs, PDRs)
44	  — those mention BCs but are incomplete and not machine-queryable.
45	- Inferring BC membership from message history (`shop-msg pending
46	  outbox`) — silent about BCs that have not yet communicated.
47	- A manifest that is a section of a larger file (e.g., a list inside
48	  `CLAUDE.md`) — it must be independently parseable.
49	- A manifest that requires human interpretation to determine whether a
50	  given BC is in scope.
51	
52	**Boundaries the PO commits to:**
53	
54	1. **The manifest is the source of truth for BC membership.** All
55	   other representations are derived. If the manifest and `repos/` 
56	   disagree, the manifest wins. If the manifest and the DB disagree,
57	   the manifest wins.
58	2. **The manifest lives in the lead shop repo, committed.** Not
59	   gitignored. Not in `repos/`. Not in a separate repo. The lead shop
60	   is the authoritative scope manager for the product.
61	3. **Each BC entry in the manifest has at minimum:** a canonical name
62	   (the `shopsystem-X` identifier), a GitHub remote URL, and a role
63	   label (e.g., `bc` to distinguish from future entries that might
64	   describe the lead shop itself or external dependencies). Additional
65	   fields (e.g., beads remote, devcontainer image tag) may be added
66	   but are not committed by this brief.
67	4. **The manifest is machine-readable.** A shell script or a Python
68	   snippet must be able to parse it and extract canonical names without
69	   calling an LLM or a PO. Format (YAML, TOML, JSON) is the
70	   Architect's call; machine-readability is the PO's invariant.
71	5. **The sync command operates from the manifest outward.** It does
72	   not read `repos/` and update the manifest; it reads the manifest
73	   and updates `repos/` (and the DB, if asked). The direction of
74	   authority is non-negotiable.
75	
76	**Open questions the PO cannot close without Architect pre-state
77	verification:**
78	
79	- **File format.** The PO commits machine-readability; YAML vs TOML
80	  vs JSON is the Architect's call after checking what the ecosystem
81	  already uses (e.g., whether `shop-msg` or `bd` parse any structured
82	  config today).
83	- **CLI name and flag shape.** The brief uses behavioral language
84	  ("the sync command", "the validate command"); exact command names
85	  and flags are the Architect's call per PDR-006.
86	- **Which BC owns the CLI.** PDR-006 resolves this. The brief commits
87	  the CLI must exist; its home is the PDR's question.
88	- **DB registration scope.** The manifest must exist before `shop-msg
89	  registry sync` (or equivalent) can work. The DB registry itself is
90	  brief 006's domain; the manifest is this brief's domain.
91	- **Initial manifest contents.** The six current product BCs are
92	  known from `repos/` inspection and session history. `ddd-product-
93	  system` in `repos/` is NOT a shopsystem BC and must not appear in
94	  the manifest. The six are:
95	  `shopsystem-messaging`, `shopsystem-scenarios`, `shopsystem-
96	  templates`, `shopsystem-test-harness`, `shopsystem-devcontainer`,
97	  `shopsystem-bc-launcher`. The Architect should verify each one
98	  exists on GitHub before populating the manifest.
99	
100	---
101	
102	## Point of intent
103	
104	The shopsystem today has no canonical answer to the question "which BCs
105	belong to this product?" BC membership is inferred from three overlapping
106	sources that do not agree: the `repos/` directory (gitignored, contains
107	non-product repos, drifts from truth), narrative documents (ADRs, briefs,
108	scenario files — partial, not machine-queryable), and message history
109	(`shop-msg pending outbox` — silent about idle BCs). The gap is most
110	visible at two operational moments:
111	
112	1. **bc-container launch --all.** Brief 004 deferred this to "follow-on
113	   composition" specifically because there is no authoritative list to
114	   iterate over. Without the manifest, `bc-container launch --all`
115	   cannot know which BCs to launch.
116	2. **shop-msg registry sync.** If brief 001's DB registry work lands
117	   without a manifest, registry population reverts to ad-hoc
118	   (`shop-msg registry add shopsystem-messaging ...`, done by hand) and
119	   will drift as BCs are added or renamed.
120	
121	This brief fixes the structural gap: the product defines its BC
122	membership explicitly, in a committed file, once. Derived representations
123	exist to serve operational tools; the manifest exists to tell the truth.
124	
125	---
126	
127	## The invariant
128	
129	### The manifest file is the single source of truth for BC membership
130	
131	Every BC that belongs to this product is declared in the manifest file.
132	Every BC that is NOT declared in the manifest file is NOT a product
133	member, regardless of what `repos/` contains, what a narrative document
134	says, or what the DB holds.
135	
136	Adding a BC to the product requires a commit to the manifest file.
137	Removing a BC from the product requires a commit to the manifest file.
138	No other action is sufficient; no other action is required.
139	
140	---
141	
142	## Three scope items
143	
144	### A — The manifest file
145	
146	A structured, machine-readable file committed to the lead shop repo at
147	a well-known path. Each BC entry declares at minimum:
148	
149	- The canonical BC name (`shopsystem-X` identifier).
150	- The GitHub remote URL from which the BC's repository is cloned.
151	- A role label that identifies the entry as a BC (as opposed to future
152	  entry types the format might support — the lead shop itself, external
153	  dependencies, etc.).
154	
155	The file is committed in version control. It is not gitignored. It is
156	parseable by a standard YAML/TOML/JSON library with no custom code.
157	It contains no narrative prose that a parser must skip. All six current
158	product BCs appear in the initial commit of the manifest file.
159	
160	**What the PO does not commit:** the exact format (YAML vs TOML vs
161	JSON), the exact field names beyond the three categories above, and the
162	well-known path (adjacent to `CLAUDE.md`, under `.claude/`, or at
163	`bcs.yaml` — the Architect picks after PDR-006 resolves ownership).
164	
165	### B — The sync command
166	
167	A CLI command that reads the manifest and produces derived state. At
168	minimum, the sync command must support two operations (either as
169	separate subcommands or flags — the Architect picks the shape):
170	
171	1. **Clone-sync:** for each BC declared in the manifest, ensure
172	   `repos/<canonical-name>/` is cloned from the declared remote. If
173	   a clone is missing, clone it. If a clone exists and its remote
174	   matches, skip it. If `repos/` contains a directory for a BC not in
175	   the manifest, warn (do not delete without explicit confirmation).
176	2. **Validate:** check that every BC declared in the manifest has a
177	   GitHub repository reachable at the declared remote URL, and that the
178	   manifest file is syntactically valid. Exit non-zero if any check
179	   fails; emit a human-readable summary of what passed and what failed.
180	
181	The sync command is scriptable: machine-friendly arguments, no
182	interactive prompts, deterministic exit codes (0 = all checks passed;
183	non-zero = at least one failure with the failure enumerated on stderr).
184	
185	The clone-sync operation is idempotent: running it twice on a fully
186	synced workspace produces no changes and exits zero both times.
187	
188	### C — Drift detection
189	
190	The sync command's validate operation must include drift detection
191	between the manifest and `repos/`. Specifically:
192	
193	- Any directory in `repos/` that matches `shopsystem-*` but is not
194	  declared in the manifest is reported as an unexpected entry.
195	- Any BC declared in the manifest whose `repos/<name>/` directory is
196	  absent is reported as a missing clone.
197	- Any BC declared in the manifest whose `repos/<name>/` remote URL
198	  does not match the manifest's declared remote is reported as a
199	  remote mismatch.
200	
201	`ddd-product-system` in `repos/` is the canonical example of an
202	unexpected entry: it is not a shopsystem BC, and the validate operation
203	should flag it.
204	
205	---
206	
207	## Out of scope — named explicitly
208	
209	**The DB registry.** Populating the DB with BC entries (so that
210	`shop-msg` can use them for routing) is brief 006's domain. The manifest
211	is the source of truth the DB is populated FROM; the DB population
212	mechanism is not this brief's scope. Scope item B (sync command) may
213	include a `--register` or `--db` flag as a stretch goal, but only if
214	the Architect confirms that the DB registration API exists at pre-state
215	time. If it does not exist, the sync command's initial scope is
216	clone-sync and validate only.
217	
218	**Building or publishing the devcontainer image.** The manifest declares
219	which BCs exist; it does not configure how their containers are built.
220	BC-specific image configuration belongs to the devcontainer BC and the
221	bc-launcher BC.
222	
223	**Automated manifest updates.** The manifest is a committed file edited
224	by humans (or by a PO-directed Architect dispatch). There is no
225	mechanism in this brief for the manifest to update itself automatically
226	when a new BC is created. New BC creation is a deliberate act that
227	includes a manifest PR as a step.
228	
229	**Lead shop manifest.** The manifest is for product BCs. The lead shop
230	is not a BC and does not appear in the manifest. Lead-shop metadata
231	(beads remote, GitHub URL) lives in the lead shop's own configuration.
232	
233	---
234	
235	## Sequencing
236	
237	- **Scope item A** (the file) has no blockers. It can land as soon as
238	  PDR-006 resolves file path and the Architect verifies the six BC
239	  GitHub remotes.
240	- **Scope item B** (sync command) requires item A to exist (the command
241	  must have a file to read), and requires PDR-006 to name the owning BC.
242	- **Scope item C** (drift detection) is part of scope item B's validate
243	  operation — same command, no separate blockers beyond B.
244	- **Brief 006 (DB registry)** follows this brief: the manifest is the
245	  prerequisite for populating the registry. Sequencing is strict.
246	
247	## Vehicle hints (Architect's call)
248	
249	- Scope item A (the manifest file) is a flat file committed to the lead
250	  shop repo — zero BC work unless the Architect determines that file
251	  format tooling (a schema validator, a linter) needs to be authored
252	  by a BC. The Architect may author the file directly in the lead shop
253	  as part of dispatching.
254	- Scope items B and C (sync and validate commands) are net-new
255	  capability. The vehicle is `assign_scenarios`. The target BC is
256	  PDR-006's answer.
257	
258	## Grounding artifacts
259	
260	- [brief 004](004-bc-container-isolation.md) — deferred `bc-container
261	  launch --all` to "follow-on composition"; the manifest is what makes
262	  that follow-on unblock-able.
263	- [brief 001](001-inter-shop-messaging-encapsulation.md) — the DB
264	  registry that the manifest will eventually feed (brief 006 is the
265	  bridge).
266	- [PDR-004](../pdr/004-bc-container-command-ownership.md) — establishes
267	  `shopsystem-bc-launcher` as the BC that needs a BC list to operate.
268	- [PDR-006](../pdr/006-bc-manifest-ownership.md) — resolves which BC
269	  owns the manifest's CLI.
270	
271	## What this leaves open
272	
273	The brief commits **intent**, not scenarios. Scenarios come after the
274	Architect verifies BC pre-state and picks vehicles per the discriminator.
275	
276	- **Exact file format and field names.** YAML vs TOML vs JSON; field
277	  names for canonical name, remote, role. Architect picks after
278	  PDR-006 resolves ownership and pre-state is verified.
279	- **Well-known file path.** Adjacent to `CLAUDE.md` (e.g., `bcs.yaml`),
280	  under `.claude/` (e.g., `.claude/bcs.yaml`), or elsewhere. Architect
281	  picks.
282	- **DB integration.** Whether the sync command's initial scope includes
283	  `--register` depends on whether brief 006's DB API exists at pre-state.
284	  Architect verifies.
285	- **Which BC owns the CLI (PDR-006).** Until that PDR resolves, the
286	  scenarios cannot be assigned. The PO flags PDR-006 as the blocker.
287
```
</details></tool-use>

01-list-pending-empty-mailbox.gherkin

```text
1	# PDR-006 — BC manifest ownership
2	
3	**Status:** draft (2026-05-20)
4	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
5	**Anchored to:** brief 005 (BC manifest); stakeholder intent 2026-05-20:
6	*"A committed, structured artifact in the lead shop repo that is the
7	authoritative declaration of which BCs belong to a product, their
8	canonical names, and their GitHub remotes."*
9	
10	## The question
11	
12	Where does the BC manifest file live, and which BC owns the CLI that
13	operates on it (the sync and validate commands)?
14	
15	The answer determines which BC receives the `assign_scenarios` dispatch
16	for brief 005's scope items B and C, which repo the implementation
17	lives in, and where the manifest file's well-known path is anchored.
18	
19	## Context
20	
21	Brief 005 commits:
22	- **Scope item A** — a machine-readable manifest file committed to the
23	  lead shop repo at a well-known path.
24	- **Scope item B** — a sync command that clones repos from the manifest
25	  and validates the manifest against actual state.
26	- **Scope item C** — drift detection as part of the validate operation.
27	
28	All three options below agree that the manifest file itself lives in
29	the lead shop repo. The ownership question is about the CLI (scope items
30	B and C), not the file (scope item A). Scope item A is a committed file
31	in the lead shop; the CLI is what the Architect must dispatch to a BC.
32	
33	The six current product BCs, relevant to this decision:
34	- `shopsystem-messaging` — inter-shop messaging surface (shop-msg CLI)
35	- `shopsystem-scenarios` — scenario hashing and registration
36	- `shopsystem-templates` — bootstrap scaffolding (shop-templates CLI)
37	- `shopsystem-test-harness` — framework test harness
38	- `shopsystem-devcontainer` — devcontainer image build and publish
39	- `shopsystem-bc-launcher` — per-BC container lifecycle (bc-container CLI)
40	
41	## Options considered
42	
43	### Option A — CLI owned by `shopsystem-templates`
44	
45	The templates BC already owns the bootstrap scaffolding CLI
46	(`shop-templates`). It is the BC from which the lead shop's own
47	structure is derived. A manifest sync command could be a new subcommand:
48	`shop-templates manifest sync`, `shop-templates manifest validate`.
49	
50	**Pros:**
51	- Bootstrap is about standing up shops; the manifest declares which
52	  shops exist. The two concerns are adjacent.
53	- `shopsystem-templates` already knows about the lead shop's structure
54	  (it generates `CLAUDE.md`, `consumer-wiring.md`, settings files).
55	  The manifest is a sibling artifact in the same genre.
56	- No new BC. One fewer repo to manage.
57	- The templates BC already handles git operations (cloning, updating
58	  managed files); clone-sync is a natural extension.
59	
60	**Cons:**
61	- The templates BC is under active load (PDR-001 restructure, CLAUDE.md
62	  propagation, bootstrap surface, event-driven settings). Adding
63	  manifest CLI scope before that work closes risks priority dilution.
64	- `shop-templates manifest sync` is thematically sound but places
65	  the manifest management concern inside a BC whose primary surface is
66	  generating and updating files from templates. Manifest sync is about
67	  cloning repos and validating GitHub remotes — an operational concern,
68	  not a templating concern.
69	- The manifest file itself lives in the lead shop repo; the CLI that
70	  operates on it would live in a different repo (`shopsystem-templates`).
71	  This is an acceptable split (the CLI operates on a file in another
72	  repo) but creates a dependency the operator must remember.
73	
74	### Option B — CLI owned by `shopsystem-bc-launcher`
75	
76	The bc-launcher BC already needs a BC list to know which containers
77	to launch (`bc-container launch --all`). The manifest is the bc-launcher's
78	input. Giving the launcher CLI ownership of the manifest operations
79	makes the launcher the authoritative manifest manager: `bc-container
80	manifest sync`, `bc-container manifest validate`.
81	
82	**Pros:**
83	- The bc-launcher already has the strongest stake in the manifest: it
84	  cannot implement `bc-container launch --all` without an authoritative
85	  BC list. The manifest is the launcher's prerequisite, not just a nice
86	  complement.
87	- Collocating the CLI and its primary consumer in the same BC means
88	  the BC that needs the manifest also owns the tooling to keep it
89	  accurate. No cross-BC dependency for the common case.
90	- `shopsystem-bc-launcher` is new (no legacy scope conflict), not under
91	  active load from prior briefs, and has a clear concern boundary
92	  (container lifecycle and BC orchestration). Adding manifest
93	  management extends that concern boundary naturally: a BC that
94	  orchestrates containers also orchestrates the list of BCs.
95	- `bc-container manifest sync` and `bc-container manifest validate` sit
96	  naturally alongside `bc-container launch --all` as preconditions in
97	  an operator's workflow.
98	
99	**Cons:**
100	- `shopsystem-bc-launcher` is the newest BC (just created, as of
101	  2026-05-20). It has the least mature codebase of any product BC. Adding
102	  manifest management before the container lifecycle work (brief 004)
103	  is complete means the first work item is manifest tooling, not
104	  containers. This is fine architecturally but inverts the natural
105	  build order.
106	- Container lifecycle and manifest management are distinct concerns.
107	  A BC named `bc-launcher` that also manages the product manifest may
108	  broaden its scope beyond what the name suggests. A future `bc-launcher`
109	  BC in another product would be expected to do container lifecycle, not
110	  manifest management.
111	
112	### Option C — CLI owned by a new BC (`shopsystem-manifest` or similar)
113	
114	A new BC whose sole product is the manifest CLI, keeping manifest
115	management independent of both bootstrap (templates) and container
116	concerns (bc-launcher).
117	
118	**Pros:**
119	- Perfect separation of concerns. The manifest BC does one thing:
120	  manages the BC manifest. No scope dilution.
121	- Extensible: if the manifest grows (e.g., manifest-driven DB registry
122	  sync, manifest-driven CI pipeline generation), all of that lands in
123	  the manifest BC without negotiating scope.
124	
125	**Cons:**
126	- Adds a seventh BC repo to a product that already has six. The manifest
127	  CLI is relatively thin (parse a file, clone repos, validate remotes).
128	  A new BC for this surface is likely under-weight — the launche BC
129	  was justified by a multi-subcommand surface (brief 004 has seventeen
130	  scenarios); the manifest CLI's initial scope is two operations.
131	- A new BC needs bootstrapping, its own role templates, its own beads
132	  remote, its own CI pipeline. That is significant overhead for a thin
133	  CLI.
134	- The product already has a BC that is the natural consumer of the
135	  manifest (bc-launcher). Separating the manifest CLI from its primary
136	  consumer means two BCs must coordinate on the manifest file format
137	  and schema — a cross-BC coupling that a single-ownership model avoids.
138	
139	## Decision
140	
141	**Option B — CLI owned by `shopsystem-bc-launcher`.**
142	
143	Rationale:
144	
145	1. **The bc-launcher has the primary stake.** `bc-container launch
146	   --all` is the operational command that requires the manifest. If the
147	   launcher owns the manifest CLI, it can validate the manifest before
148	   operating on it — no cross-BC round trip. The dependency graph is:
149	   manifest file → bc-launcher manifest CLI → bc-container launch --all.
150	   All three hops are within the same BC.
151	
152	2. **Scope extension is coherent.** A BC that "orchestrates BC
153	   containers" is also naturally the right owner of "orchestrates the
154	   list of BCs." The bc-launcher already commits to knowing which BCs
155	   exist (brief 004, scope item D: `bc-container list`). The manifest
156	   is the authoritative input to that list. Owning the manifest CLI
157	   closes the loop within the BC's existing concern.
158	
159	3. **Avoids adding another BC.** Option C is the cleanest separation
160	   but is under-weight. Six repos is already a significant operational
161	   surface; a seventh for a two-operation CLI is not justified by the
162	   current scope. The bc-launcher can absorb manifest management without
163	   diluting its primary concern.
164	
165	4. **Templates is over-committed.** Option A is thematically reasonable
166	   but adds to an already-loaded BC. The bc-launcher, being the newest
167	   and lightest BC, is the right recipient of new scope.
168	
169	5. **Build order is acceptable.** The objection that manifest management
170	   inverts the bc-launcher's natural build order is real but minor.
171	   Scope item A (the manifest file itself) requires no BC work and can
172	   land first. The bc-launcher's manifest CLI (scope items B and C) can
173	   follow after the manifest file exists. Brief 004's container lifecycle
174	   scenarios can proceed in parallel — they do not depend on the manifest
175	   CLI.
176	
177	**Manifest file path commitment:** The manifest file lives adjacent to
178	`CLAUDE.md` in the lead shop root (not under `.claude/`). Its filename
179	is `bc-manifest.<ext>` where `<ext>` is the Architect's format choice
180	after pre-state verification. Example: `bc-manifest.yaml`. This makes
181	it a first-class, visible artifact at the repo root — not buried in a
182	hidden directory.
183	
184	**CLI surface commitment:** The bc-launcher CLI (`bc-container`) gains
185	a `manifest` subcommand group:
186	- `bc-container manifest sync` — clone-sync from manifest.
187	- `bc-container manifest validate` — validate manifest + drift detect.
188	
189	Exact flag shapes are scenario-level, not PDR-level.
190	
191	## What this leaves open
192	
193	1. **File format.** YAML vs TOML vs JSON. The Architect verifies what
194	   the bc-launcher's ecosystem already uses (Python? shell?) and picks
195	   the format that requires no additional parser dependency.
196	2. **Schema definition.** The PO commits three required fields (canonical
197	   name, GitHub remote, role label). The Architect defines the exact
198	   field names in the first scenario for scope item A.
199	3. **`--all` flag shape on bc-container launch.** Once the manifest
200	   CLI exists, `bc-container launch --all` reads from it. That flag's
201	   behavior is a brief 004 follow-on, not this brief's scope.
202	4. **DB registration integration.** If brief 006's DB registry lands
203	   before or alongside this CLI, `bc-container manifest sync --register`
204	   may be scoped in. Architect determines at pre-state.
205	
206	## Cross-references
207	
208	- [brief 005](../briefs/005-bc-manifest.md) — the intent this PDR is
209	  decided against.
210	- [brief 004](../briefs/004-bc-container-isolation.md) — the bc-launcher's
211	  primary brief; `bc-container launch --all` is the deferred follow-on
212	  that the manifest unblocks.
213	- [PDR-004](004-bc-container-command-ownership.md) — establishes
214	  `shopsystem-bc-launcher` as a new BC; this PDR extends its scope.
215	- [ADR-004](../adr/004-bc-launcher-as-new-bc.md) — architectural record
216	  for the bc-launcher BC creation.
217
```
</details></tool-use>
