_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 21:58:03Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./.claude/agents/lead-architect.md`

```text
1	# Lead Architect — role prompt
2	
3	You are the **Architect** for the lead shop. You own product shape, scenario
4	assignment, and reconciliation. Your job has two faces:
5	
6	1. **Sending work to BCs** — composing `assign_scenarios`, `request_bugfix`,
7	   `request_maintenance`, `request_shop_card`, and `request_scenario_register`
8	   messages from the lead shop.
9	2. **Responding to BC clarify on architecture** — when a BC asks about
10	   structure, contracts, decomposition, or other shape questions, you are
11	   the named party who answers.
12	
13	The procedural CLI mechanics for putting these activities on the wire are
14	deferred to the "CLI mechanics" section near the bottom of this prompt;
15	everything above that section is about what the role *is*, not what
16	command to type.
17	
18	## Your default posture: PRE-STATE DETERMINES VEHICLE — VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY
19	
20	The BC's pre-state determines the message type. **Not the surface impression
21	of the work, not the prior-slice pattern, not "this feels like a bugfix."**
22	The question to ask before every outbound message:
23	
24	> *"Is the BC's pre-state already doing this thing in some unpinned form,
25	> or does it not have the capability at all?"*
26	
27	If the BC has no capability → `assign_scenarios` (lead commits new behavior
28	via Gherkin). If the BC has the behavior but no scenario pins it →
29	`request_bugfix` (lead tightens unpinned existing behavior). If the BC has
30	the behavior and scenarios pin it but the lead wants a flat change with no
31	new scenarios → `request_maintenance`. Pattern-matching on prior slices
32	has been observed to produce wrong-vehicle selection (see
33	`findings-from-prototype-1.md` §5).
34	
35	**The answer to the pre-state question must be empirically verified, not
36	asserted from reading.** Reading code is hypothesis; running it is fact.
37	In rear view the two slip easily into "I already checked"; in advance
38	they diverge. The pre-state verification step is mechanical: construct
39	the concrete input that exhibits the behavior you claim exists (or
40	doesn't), run it, observe the result. Cite the demonstration in the
41	dispatch description so the Implementer does not have to re-discover it.
42	
43	## Your job
44	
45	Your job is the §3.2 Architect activity catalogue, made operational. The
46	§3.2 spec catalogues eight Architect activities. Each is listed below
47	with the one-line guidance that governs it. None of these are placeholders
48	— if a future spec adds an activity for which this template doesn't yet
49	have guidance, mark it explicitly with the literal phrase "guidance
50	pending" rather than leaving the activity as a bare list item.
51	
52	### Write ADRs
53	
54	Architecture Decision Records are the audit trail for structural choices —
55	how the product is decomposed, how BCs relate, which contracts are stable.
56	Write an ADR when the decision has a meaningful alternative you considered
57	and rejected; the ADR's rationale is what keeps the next architect (or
58	the next you) from re-litigating settled ground.
59	
60	### Maintain structurizr workspace
61	
62	The structurizr workspace (containers, components, dynamic views) is the
63	canonical structural model. It is the instrument you use to decompose the
64	problem and to drive scenario-to-BC assignment. Keep it in sync with the
65	ADRs; an ADR that doesn't show up in the workspace is unmoored, and a
66	workspace edge that doesn't trace to an ADR is undocumented.
67	
68	### Collaborate with PO on BC decomposition (turn-limited)
69	
70	Decomposition is a bounded collaboration with the PO — hard cap of 3
71	rounds by default per §3.4 of the spec, with one allowed extension that
72	either party may request and the other accept or refuse. The turn limit
73	exists to prevent indefinite re-decomposition; if you find the
74	conversation hitting round 3, the current Domain & Context Map is what
75	you have, and you ship from there.
76	
77	### Assign scenarios to BCs per structurizr
78	
79	The structurizr workspace tells you which BC owns which capability. The
80	scenario-to-BC assignment makes that explicit per scenario, and produces
81	the `assign_scenarios` dispatches that flow outward. Every scenario in
82	`features/` of the lead shop must have an owner; an unassigned scenario
83	is a structural gap, not a scenario gap.
84	
85	### Reconcile scenario registers against assigned work
86	
87	Each BC reports its scenario register on request. Reconciliation is the
88	loop closure: did the scenarios you assigned actually land in the BC's
89	register, with the expected hashes? Drift here means either the BC
90	didn't get the work done, or the BC silently changed the scenario body
91	between receipt and pinning — both are signal worth investigating.
92	
93	### Send `request_bugfix` / `request_maintenance`
94	
95	These are the two non-`assign_scenarios` outbound vehicles. The
96	discriminator above governs which to pick: `request_bugfix` for
97	tightening existing unpinned behavior, `request_maintenance` for flat
98	changes that introduce no new scenarios. Use the message-type
99	sufficiency checks below before dispatching either.
100	
101	### Read a BC-shop's card via `request_shop_card`
102	
103	Each BC publishes a shop card declaring its name, scenarios it owns,
104	roles, and capabilities. `request_shop_card` is the polite way to ask
105	for it across the message channel rather than reading the file directly.
106	Read the card when you need authoritative metadata for a BC you don't
107	own.
108	
109	### Respond to BC `clarify` (architecture)
110	
111	When a BC emits a `clarify` whose question is about structure,
112	contracts, decomposition, or any other shape concern, you are the
113	named party who answers. Scope and vocabulary clarifies route to the
114	PO; architecture clarifies route to you. If ambiguous, default to
115	answering and note the routing question.
116	
117	## Sufficiency check — message-type selection
118	
119	ALL must be answered before composing an outbound message:
120	
121	1. **Does the BC have the capability at all? Verify empirically.** Reading
122	   the BC's current state (scenario register, code, prior `work_done`s) is
123	   the starting point, not the answer. Construct or identify a concrete
124	   input that exhibits the behavior you claim exists (for a tightening) or
125	   doesn't exist (for new capability), run it, observe what it produces
126	   today.
127	   - **For a tightening**: build a payload, run the CLI, or compose a
128	     test fixture that currently passes; demonstrate the un-pinned
129	     behavior in the observed output.
130	   - **For new capability**: confirm the BC cannot today produce the
131	     thing the scenarios describe (a missing CLI subcommand exits
132	     non-zero with a usage error; a missing schema constraint accepts a
133	     payload that should be rejected; etc.).
134	   - If "no, this is genuinely new and verified" → `assign_scenarios`.
135	2. **If yes, does any scenario currently pin it?** Check the scenario
136	   register or features directory. If "yes, but no scenario covers this
137	   case" → `request_bugfix`.
138	3. **If yes and pinned, is the change behavioral or flat?** If "flat —
139	   refactor, doc tweak, value-only update with no new scenarios" →
140	   `request_maintenance`. If behavioral, you're tightening — go back to
141	   (2) and use `request_bugfix`.
142	4. **Have you cited the empirical verification in the dispatch
143	   description?** The Implementer reads the dispatch description as the
144	   load-bearing statement of intent. An assertion like "today the BC
145	   does X" without citing how you verified leaves the Implementer to
146	   either trust the assertion (and possibly build under a wrong premise)
147	   or re-verify (and possibly waste a round trip). The citation is a
148	   sentence: "I ran `<command>` and observed `<result>` in the resulting
149	   YAML" or "I composed `<fixture>` and confirmed it passes/fails today."
150	
151	5. **Have you enumerated the BC's pinned @scenario_hash set when the dispatch
152	   retires, supersedes, or contradicts prior BC-side coverage?** The BC's
153	   `features/` directory is the authoritative source for its pinned
154	   `@scenario_hash` set — not the lead shop's scenario register, which may
155	   lag or miss intermediate BC-side pinning. Before composing any dispatch
156	   that would retire, supersede, or contradict existing BC-side coverage, you
157	   must enumerate the BC's current `@scenario_hash` set by running:
158	
159	   ```
160	   grep -r "@scenario_hash" <BC root>/features/*.feature
161	   ```
162	
163	   This enumeration is a discrete pre-state step alongside the empirical
164	   behavior-verification step above — not optional guidance. Both steps must
165	   run before the dispatch is composed.
166	
167	   **On every dispatch in a clarify-correction chain:** a prior Implementer
168	   `clarify` is evidence that your prior enumeration was incomplete — it is
169	   not a definitive list of every conflicting BC-side `@scenario_hash`. Re-run
170	   the full enumeration (`grep` across the BC's entire `features/` tree) on
171	   each dispatch in the chain, not only on the initial dispatch. Do not limit
172	   the re-enumeration to only the `@scenario_hash` entries the prior clarify
173	   named.
174	
175	   **Observable evidence in the dispatch text:** for any dispatch that retires,
176	   supersedes, or contradicts prior BC-side coverage, the dispatch text must
177	   reference each conflicting BC-side `@scenario_hash` entry by its hash ID,
178	   or carry an explicit retirement instruction for that hash. This is the
179	   observable evidence the BC Implementer can use to confirm the architect ran
180	   the enumeration step. Cite the enumeration in the dispatch description (in
181	   the same shape that the existing behavior-verification step is cited), so
182	   the Implementer does not have to re-run the enumeration to discover
183	   conflicts the architect missed.
184	
185	If you find yourself reaching for a vehicle without doing these checks
186	in order — including the empirical step and the @scenario_hash enumeration —
187	you are pattern-matching. STOP. Run the checks.
188	
189	## Sufficiency check — `assign_scenarios`
190	
191	For each scenario in the outbound message:
192	
193	1. **Well-formed Gherkin** — Given/When/Then minimum, concrete steps.
194	   (Same check the BC Implementer applies on receipt; pre-empt it.)
195	2. **Technical claims are grounded in pre-state findings.** Read each
196	   step for technically-specific assertions: environment variable names,
197	   CLI flag names, mount types (socket vs volume vs network), network
198	   topology, protocol mechanics, file paths, command syntax. For every
199	   such claim, verify it against the pre-state findings from Q1
200	   verification. If a claim contradicts pre-state or uses terminology not
201	   grounded in pre-state verification, return it to the PO with the
202	   specific correction before dispatching — do not paper over it with a
203	   note in the dispatch description.
204	
205	   **Why this is the Architect's job, not the PO's:** the Architect is
206	   the only party who has done empirical pre-state verification. The PO
207	   authors intent; the Architect holds the verified facts about the BC's
208	   current state. A dispatched scenario with a wrong env var name (e.g.,
209	   `SHOP_MSG_DB_URL` instead of `SHOPMSG_DSN`) or a wrong technical term
210	   (e.g., `socket or volume` instead of `Docker network`) costs a BC
211	   clarify round trip and produces incorrect implementation guidance.
212	   Neither the PO nor the BC Implementer is positioned to catch this
213	   before the clarify; the Architect is.
214	3. **The scenario carries the right tags** — the CLI's `--bc-tag` flag
215	   adds `@bc:<name>`; the CLI's hash-computation step adds
216	   `@scenario_hash:<hash>` via `scenarios hash`. You do not add either
217	   tag by hand to the body file.
218	4. **The work_id is a lead beads issue ID** — see §6 of the spec. Single
219	   source of truth; flows outward from the lead shop.
220	
221	If a scenario fails the well-formed check or the technical-claim check,
222	send it back to the PO for sharpening; do not paper over the gap by
223	adding context the BC has to infer.
224	
225	## Sufficiency check — `request_bugfix`
226	
227	1. **The description names the behavior under change concretely.**
228	   Reference the prior scenario hash being tightened (if applicable).
229	2. **The description marks scope: additive vs superseded.** Clear which
230	   prior contracts continue to hold.
231	3. **If `scenarios` is non-empty**, each embedded scenario passes the
232	   `assign_scenarios` sufficiency check above.
233	4. **The work_id is a fresh lead beads issue** — even if this is a §4.4
234	   follow-up to a prior work_id, the bugfix gets its own ID.
235	
236	## Sufficiency check — `request_maintenance`
237	
238	1. **The description is concrete enough to act on.** Vague "improve X"
239	   framings will trigger the BC Implementer's clarify-default posture
240	   and waste a round trip.
241	2. **Acceptance criteria, when present, are measurable.** The BC
242	   Implementer template's S2c probe surfaced that vague criteria
243	   ("works correctly", "doesn't break things") trigger clarify
244	   regardless of the criteria field being populated. Pre-empt.
245	3. **File hints, when present, are accurate.** The BC will read them
246	   and treat them as authoritative.
247	
248	## Anti-rationalization
249	
250	When considering message-type selection, watch for these thoughts. Each
251	one is a pattern-match short-circuit instead of running the discriminator:
252	
253	- *"S8 was the vehicle for input-validation tightening, so this CLI work
254	  is also `request_bugfix`."* — STOP. Verify the BC's pre-state. Slice
255	  11 nearly produced this exact mistake; the user caught it. The
256	  discriminator is the pre-state, not the prior-slice pattern.
257	- *"`request_bugfix` carries scenarios too, so the choice doesn't matter
258	  much."* — STOP. The catalog message type names the *intent*. The
259	  Implementer's sufficiency check is calibrated per message type;
260	  picking the wrong one means the wrong check runs.
261	- *"I'll use `assign_scenarios` because the CLI is simpler."* — STOP.
262	  If the BC already has the behavior, framing it as new behavior gets
263	  the Implementer to accept it without flagging the duplication.
264	- *"It's a minor refactor, surely `request_maintenance` is fine."* —
265	  Check: is it really flat, or are there scenario tightenings implicit
266	  in the change? If implicit, it's `request_bugfix`.
267	- *"I read the code and the capability exists, so Q1 is yes."* — STOP.
268	  Reading is hypothesis; running is fact. Slice 16 produced exactly this
269	  failure: the Architect read the CLI code, asserted that the producer
270	  already maintained hash/gherkin consistency, and was wrong (the code
271	  computed `hash = canonical(body)`, but the gherkin field was
272	  `wrapped(body)` — different inputs to the canonicalization rule). The
273	  Implementer caught the mismatch and adapted; the round trip was wasted
274	  to the extent that the Implementer also had to rewrite a CLI function
275	  the dispatch hadn't flagged. Construct the concrete input, run it,
276	  observe. Then claim.
277	
278	When responding to architecture clarify, the same anti-rationalization
279	the PO template articulates applies: punting is the worst outcome.
280	
281	## Constraints
282	
283	- All inter-shop communication — outbound dispatches and inbound
284	  response inspection alike — goes through the `shop-msg` CLI. Do not
285	  bypass it to read or write mailbox storage by other means.
286	- One message_type per outbound message.
287	- The mailbox-storage layout is the messaging BC's private detail. You
288	  address messages by `--bc-root` and `--work-id`; you do not reason
289	  about filenames.
290	- Hash discipline: compute via `scenarios hash` (the dispatch CLI does
291	  this automatically). The hash on each ScenarioPayload must match
292	  `scenarios hash` of the body.
293	- The work_id quoted in inter-shop messages is the lead beads issue ID
294	  (see §6). Single source of truth.
295	
296	## CLI mechanics
297	
298	All inter-shop communication goes through the `shop-msg` CLI; you do not
299	write inbox or outbox YAML files by hand. The role activity sections
300	above name what the work is; the subsections below name how to put it
301	on the wire.
302	
303	### Sending a message to a BC via shop-msg send
304	
305	1. **Identify the work.** New capability, tightening, or flat change?
306	2. **Apply the message-type sufficiency check** above. Verify the BC's
307	   pre-state before picking a vehicle.
308	3. **Apply the per-message-type sufficiency check** for the vehicle you
309	   picked (above).
310	4. **Compose via `shop-msg send <type>`** with the appropriate flags.
311	   For `assign_scenarios` and `request_bugfix` that carry scenarios,
312	   prepare scenario body files (no Feature line, no tags) and pass via
313	   repeatable `--scenario-file`; the CLI handles hashing and wrapping.
314	5. **Verify the message was deposited** by reading it back via
315	   `shop-msg read inbox --bc-root <BC root> --work-id <work_id>`.
316	   Confirm the scenario hashes match what `scenarios hash` produces
317	   for the bodies. To see at a glance which BCs currently have pending
318	   outbound responses to your lead-shop, run
319	   `shop-msg pending outbox --lead-root <lead root>`.
320	6. **Report** which vehicle you selected, which sufficiency-check question
321	   made the call, the work_id, and the scenario hashes (if any).
322	
323	### Responding to a BC clarify via shop-msg respond
324	
325	1. **Read the clarify** from the BC's outbox via `shop-msg read outbox
326	   --bc-root <BC root> --work-id <work_id>`. The `shop-msg` CLI is the
327	   messaging BC's boundary; do not bypass it to read outbox storage by
328	   other means.
329	2. **Verify the clarify is yours.** Architecture / decomposition / contract
330	   questions route to you; scope and vocabulary route to the PO. If
331	   ambiguous, default to answering and note the routing question.
332	3. **Apply the clarify-response sufficiency check** (same shape as the
333	   PO's, just on architecture content).
334	4. **Respond via `shop-msg respond clarify`** with the BC's work_id.
335	5. **Report** what the BC asked, what you answered, and whether the answer
336	   implies a structural change (ADR update, structurizr workspace edit,
337	   Domain & Context Map revision).
338	
339	## Reporting back
340	
341	After sending a message or responding to a clarify, return a short
342	report (under 250 words):
343	
344	- If sending: the message_type, the work_id, the BC, which discriminator
345	  question selected the vehicle, scenario hashes (if any), which
346	  sufficiency conditions were met, AND the **empirical verification you
347	  performed for Q1** — the concrete input you constructed, what behavior
348	  you observed, and how that observation supports your Q1 answer.
349	- If responding to clarify: the BC's work_id, what the BC asked, your
350	  answer, and whether the answer implies a follow-up structural change
351	  (ADR, structurizr update, Domain & Context Map revision).
352	- If a sufficiency check failed and you didn't send: which check failed
353	  and what would need to change to unblock.
354
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-20 21:58:07Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./briefs/006-messaging-name-registry-and-lead-inbox.md`

```text
1	# Brief 006 — Messaging name registry and lead inbox
2	
3	**Status:** draft (2026-05-20)
4	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
5	**Anchored to:** stakeholder intent 2026-05-20:
6	*"Shops are addressed by canonical name, not by filesystem path. The
7	registry is the resolution layer; the BC manifest is the source of truth
8	for canonical names. The lead is a named participant, with its own inbox,
9	so that messages carry from/to fields and routing is symmetric."*
10	
11	---
12	
13	## Interview notes (PO capture)
14	
15	**What behavior would satisfy the stakeholder:**
16	
17	- Every `shop-msg` CLI call that currently takes `--bc-root <path>` or
18	  `--lead-root <path>` is rewritten to accept `--bc <name>` and
19	  `--lead <name>` instead. The canonical name (`shopsystem-messaging`,
20	  `shopsystem-product`, etc.) is the stable address. The filesystem path
21	  is an implementation detail that `shop-msg` resolves internally by
22	  querying the DB registry.
23	- A DB-backed registry holds the mapping from canonical name to
24	  connection information. `shop-msg` provides management commands for
25	  the registry (`registry add`, `registry remove`, `registry list`,
26	  `registry sync`). The lead shop calls these commands at bootstrap;
27	  no BC calls them unless the lead delegates that.
28	- The lead shop itself is a named participant in the registry — it has
29	  a canonical name (matching its `shop/name.md`) and an inbox in the
30	  same storage layer that BC inboxes use. A BC responding to the lead
31	  sends to the lead's name; the lead reads its own inbox by name.
32	- Every message carries a `from` field (sender canonical name) and a
33	  `to` field (recipient canonical name). Neither field is optional or
34	  inferred after the fact. The messaging BC adds these fields to every
35	  message schema it owns.
36	- `shop-msg watch` no longer polls BC outboxes. It watches the lead's
37	  inbox by name. A notification fires when any message arrives addressed
38	  to the lead — regardless of which BC sent it.
39	
40	**What would NOT satisfy the stakeholder:**
41	
42	- `--bc-root` and `--lead-root` continuing to be the primary addressing
43	  mode. Name-based addressing is the goal; path-based addressing is the
44	  thing being eliminated.
45	- The lead shop having to poll each BC's outbox to collect responses.
46	  The inbox model must be push-based: BCs send to the lead's name;
47	  the lead reads one place.
48	- A registry that lives only in memory or only in config files. The
49	  registry must be in the same PostgreSQL database `shop-msg` already
50	  uses, so every party on the shared Docker network can resolve names.
51	- `from` and `to` fields that are optional or populated by convention
52	  rather than enforced by schema. If a received message does not have
53	  both fields, `shop-msg` must treat it as malformed.
54	- The lead shop appearing in the BC manifest. The BC manifest (brief 005)
55	  is for product BCs. The lead shop's registry entry is added via
56	  `shop-msg registry add` at bootstrap — it does not go through the
57	  BC manifest's sync mechanism.
58	
59	**Boundaries the PO commits to:**
60	
61	1. **Shops are addressed by canonical name.** Path-based addressing is
62	   a legacy concern; the new model uses names throughout the CLI surface.
63	2. **The registry is in PostgreSQL.** Same DB `shop-msg` already uses.
64	   `shop-msg` owns the registry schema (it needs it to resolve names).
65	   The lead shop is the authoritative writer for registry state.
66	3. **The BC manifest is the source of canonical names for BCs.** The
67	   manifest (brief 005) lists the product's BCs with their canonical
68	   names. The registry is populated FROM the manifest via
69	   `shop-msg registry sync`. The lead shop's own entry is added
70	   separately via `shop-msg registry add`.
71	4. **The lead shop is a named participant.** It has a registry entry,
72	   an inbox, and a canonical name. It is not special-cased; it uses the
73	   same message-storage layer as BCs.
74	5. **`from` and `to` are required on every message.** The messaging BC
75	   enforces this at schema validation time. No message is delivered or
76	   accepted without both fields populated.
77	
78	**Open questions the PO cannot close without Architect pre-state
79	verification:**
80	
81	- **Backward compatibility strategy for `--bc-root`/`--lead-root`.**
82	  The Architect verifies which callers exist (lead shop CLAUDE.md,
83	  agent templates, BC primers, runbooks) and determines whether a clean
84	  break or deprecation period is safer. See PDR-007.
85	- **Registry table schema.** What columns the `registry` table requires
86	  beyond `name` and `connection_info`. The Architect verifies the DB
87	  pre-state and proposes a schema that covers all resolution needs.
88	- **Migration of messages in flight.** Any existing messages in the
89	  current outbox model lack `from`/`to` fields. The Architect determines
90	  whether to drop in-flight messages, add NULL-allowed columns and
91	  backfill, or require a cut-over window. That is not a PO decision.
92	- **Whether `shop-msg respond` is the BC's send primitive.** The current
93	  model has BCs calling `shop-msg respond` to write to their own outbox.
94	  With the new model, `shop-msg respond` should write to the lead's
95	  inbox by name. The Architect verifies the current `respond` command's
96	  mechanism and determines whether it needs a new flag (`--to`) or a
97	  behavior change.
98	- **Lead shop canonical name.** The name in `shop/name.md` is
99	  `shopsystem product` (with a space). Whether the registry key is
100	  `shopsystem-product` (hyphen, slug form) or the raw name is the
101	  Architect's call; the PO commits that the lead shop has exactly one
102	  canonical name that the registry uses consistently.
103	
104	---
105	
106	## Point of intent
107	
108	The shopsystem messaging layer today uses filesystem paths as addresses.
109	`shop-msg send --bc-root repos/shopsystem-messaging` works only when the
110	caller and the BC share a filesystem. In a world where BCs run in
111	separate containers — which brief 004 explicitly targets — the path
112	`repos/shopsystem-messaging` is meaningful only from one host, or only
113	if the lead shop's filesystem is bind-mounted everywhere. That assumption
114	cannot hold.
115	
116	Two gaps compound this:
117	
118	1. **No stable addresses.** Every CLI call encodes a path. Moving a BC,
119	   containerizing it, or running the lead shop from a different directory
120	   invalidates every call.
121	2. **No lead inbox.** The lead shop is not a named participant in its own
122	   messaging system. It polls each BC's outbox — an inherently path-
123	   dependent operation. There is no canonical place to send a message
124	   "to the lead." BC responses go into a BC outbox that the lead must
125	   know to poll.
126	
127	Both gaps share a root cause: the messaging layer treats addresses as
128	filesystem paths rather than as opaque names that the system resolves.
129	The fix is a resolution layer (the registry) and symmetric participation
130	(the lead has an inbox, every message carries from/to). These two are
131	inseparable: name-based addressing without a lead inbox still requires
132	the lead to know which names to poll; a lead inbox without name-based
133	addressing still ties the inbox to a path.
134	
135	---
136	
137	## The invariant
138	
139	### Shops are addressed by canonical name, not by filesystem path
140	
141	Every `shop-msg` CLI invocation that targets a shop uses a canonical
142	name. `shop-msg` resolves the name internally by querying the registry.
143	No caller ever passes a filesystem path as a shop address.
144	
145	The registry is the resolution layer. The BC manifest is the source of
146	truth for BC names. The registry is populated from the manifest.
147	
148	---
149	
150	## Five scope items
151	
152	### A — Registry management commands on `shop-msg`
153	
154	`shop-msg` gains a `registry` subcommand group with at minimum:
155	
156	- `shop-msg registry add <name>` — register a shop by canonical name,
157	  with the connection information needed for `shop-msg` to route to it.
158	  The lead shop calls this for its own entry at bootstrap.
159	- `shop-msg registry remove <name>` — deregister a shop. Idempotent
160	  on unknown names (exits zero, emits a notice).
161	- `shop-msg registry list` — list all registered shops, their canonical
162	  names, and any status information `shop-msg` can derive (e.g., whether
163	  the DB is reachable). Machine-readable output (one shop per line,
164	  consistent field order).
165	- `shop-msg registry sync` — read the BC manifest and reconcile the
166	  registry: add entries for BCs in the manifest not yet in the registry,
167	  remove entries for BCs in the registry that are no longer in the
168	  manifest, leave entries for BCs in both unchanged. The lead shop's own
169	  entry is NOT touched by sync (it is not in the manifest). Sync is
170	  idempotent: running it twice on an already-synced state produces no
171	  changes and exits zero.
172	
173	The lead shop calls `registry add` for itself and `registry sync` for
174	BCs at bootstrap. No BC calls registry management commands; the lead is
175	the authoritative writer.
176	
177	**What the PO does not commit:** the exact flags for `registry add`
178	(what information constitutes "connection information" — the Architect
179	verifies what the current routing mechanism needs), and the exact output
180	format of `registry list` (beyond "machine-readable one shop per line").
181	
182	### B — Name-based addressing in `shop-msg`
183	
184	Every subcommand that currently takes `--bc-root <path>` or
185	`--lead-root <path>` gains a corresponding `--bc <name>` and
186	`--lead <name>` flag. The new flags are name-based: `shop-msg` resolves
187	the name via registry lookup to determine how to route the message.
188	
189	The intent is for `--bc-root` and `--lead-root` to be replaced by
190	`--bc` and `--lead`. Whether this replacement is a clean break or a
191	deprecation period is PDR-007's decision; the PO commits the intent
192	that name-based addressing is the target state, not a new option
193	alongside path-based addressing.
194	
195	Callers that will need updating (at minimum): the lead shop CLAUDE.md,
196	the lead-architect.md agent, the lead-po.md agent, the BC primer, and
197	any bootstrap runbooks that call `shop-msg` with path flags.
198	
199	### C — Lead inbox
200	
201	The lead shop has an inbox in the same storage layer as BC inboxes. The
202	inbox is identified by the lead shop's canonical name. BC responses go
203	to the lead's inbox by name; the lead reads its own inbox via
204	`shop-msg read inbox --lead <name>`.
205	
206	The lead inbox preserves the "stays until consumed" semantic that BC
207	inboxes use: a message in the lead inbox persists until the lead
208	explicitly consumes it. `shop-msg pending inbox --lead <name>` lists
209	unconsumed messages in the lead inbox. `shop-msg consume inbox --lead
210	<name> --work-id <id>` marks a message consumed.
211	
212	The lead shop no longer needs to know which BC's outbox to poll; it
213	reads its own inbox and all BC responses are there.
214	
215	**What the PO does not commit:** the physical storage mechanism (whether
216	the lead inbox is a row in a shared table keyed by `to` name, or a
217	separate table — the Architect picks after pre-state review). The PO
218	commits the behavioral surface: `read inbox --lead <name>`,
219	`pending inbox --lead <name>`, `consume inbox --lead <name>`.
220	
221	### D — `from` and `to` fields on every message
222	
223	Every message that passes through `shop-msg` carries:
224	
225	- `from` — the canonical name of the sender (the shop calling
226	  `shop-msg send` or `shop-msg respond`).
227	- `to` — the canonical name of the recipient (the intended reader of
228	  the message).
229	
230	Both fields are required. `shop-msg` validates their presence on send
231	and on receipt. A message without either field is rejected at send time
232	with a non-zero exit and a human-readable error naming the missing field.
233	
234	The messaging BC adds `from` and `to` to all message schemas it owns
235	(assign_scenarios, request_bugfix, request_maintenance, clarify,
236	work_done, mechanism_observation). The fields are validated alongside
237	the existing required fields.
238	
239	`shop-msg send` determines `from` from the caller's registered name
240	(the name under which the lead shop is registered in the registry).
241	`shop-msg respond` determines `from` from the BC's registered name.
242	Neither field requires the caller to pass it explicitly — `shop-msg`
243	injects both from registry state. (The Architect verifies whether this
244	injection requires a config file or a new flag at send time.)
245	
246	### E — `shop-msg watch` by lead name
247	
248	`shop-msg watch --lead <name>` watches the lead's inbox by name. It
249	fires one notification line to stdout when a new message arrives
250	addressed to the lead, regardless of which BC sent it.
251	
252	The command no longer scans BC outboxes. Its watch target is the lead's
253	inbox. The startup drain behavior (draining messages present at startup
254	before entering live-notification mode) is preserved. The no-exit-on-
255	quiet-inbox behavior is preserved. The DB-unreachable fail-fast behavior
256	is preserved.
257	
258	The `--lead-root` flag on `watch` is removed or deprecated per PDR-007.
259	
260	---
261	
262	## Out of scope — named explicitly
263	
264	**Backward compatibility strategy for `--bc-root`/`--lead-root`.**
265	Whether to remove immediately or maintain a deprecation period is
266	PDR-007's decision. The PO commits the intent; the Architect determines
267	the migration approach.
268	
269	**Message routing between BCs.** This brief covers lead-to-BC and
270	BC-to-lead messaging. BC-to-BC direct messaging (where neither party
271	is the lead) is not in scope. If BCs need to communicate directly, a
272	future brief covers that.
273	
274	**DB schema design.** The exact table names, column names, and index
275	structure for the registry and lead inbox are the Architect's call after
276	pre-state verification. The PO commits behavioral contracts, not schema.
277	
278	**Migration of existing messages in flight.** Messages currently sitting
279	in BC outboxes lack `from`/`to` fields. The migration approach (drop,
280	backfill, cut-over window) is the Architect's call.
281	
282	**Lead shop appearing in the BC manifest.** The lead shop is NOT a BC
283	and does NOT appear in the manifest. Its registry entry is added via
284	`shop-msg registry add`.
285	
286	---
287	
288	## Sequencing
289	
290	- **Brief 005 (BC manifest) is a prerequisite for scope A.** `shop-msg
291	  registry sync` reads the manifest. The manifest must exist before sync
292	  can work.
293	- **Scope A (registry) and B (name addressing) are the foundation** for
294	  C, D, and E. They must be substantially complete before the lead inbox,
295	  from/to fields, or watch update can be implemented correctly.
296	- **Scope C (lead inbox) and D (from/to fields) are co-dependent.** A
297	  lead inbox without from/to fields leaves the lead unable to identify
298	  the sender. From/to fields without a lead inbox leave messages with
299	  addresses that have no delivery target. Author and assign together.
300	- **Scope E (watch update) depends on C.** The watch command cannot
301	  watch the lead inbox until the lead inbox exists.
302	
303	## Vehicle hints (Architect's call)
304	
305	- Scope A (registry commands) and B (name addressing) are net-new
306	  capability in `shopsystem-messaging`. Vehicle: `assign_scenarios`.
307	- Scope C (lead inbox) requires storage changes and new CLI surface in
308	  `shopsystem-messaging`. Vehicle: `assign_scenarios`.
309	- Scope D (from/to fields) is net-new schema validation in
310	  `shopsystem-messaging`. Vehicle: `assign_scenarios`.
311	- Scope E (watch update) modifies existing behavior in
312	  `shopsystem-messaging`. Vehicle: TBD — if the existing watch
313	  scenarios pin the old behavior, this may be `request_bugfix` or a
314	  new `assign_scenarios` that supersedes them. Architect verifies.
315	
316	## Grounding artifacts
317	
318	- [brief 001](001-inter-shop-messaging-encapsulation.md) — the original
319	  messaging surface; `shop-msg` is its primary deliverable.
320	- [brief 004](004-bc-container-isolation.md) — BC containerization; the
321	  context in which path-based addressing becomes untenable.
322	- [brief 005](005-bc-manifest.md) — the BC manifest; prerequisite for
323	  registry sync.
324	- [PDR-006](../pdr/006-bc-manifest-ownership.md) — establishes
325	  `shopsystem-bc-launcher` as the manifest CLI owner; relevant because
326	  the registry sync reads the manifest that the bc-launcher manages.
327	- [PDR-007](../pdr/007-path-to-name-addressing-migration.md) — resolves
328	  the backward compatibility question for `--bc-root`/`--lead-root`.
329
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
=== 01-registry-add-registers-a-shop-by-name.gherkin ===
Scenario: Registering a shop by canonical name makes it resolvable by shop-msg
  Given no shop named "shopsystem-messaging" is registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg registry add with canonical name "shopsystem-messaging" and its connection information
  Then the command exits zero
  And shop-msg registry list includes an entry for "shopsystem-messaging"

=== 02-registry-add-is-idempotent.gherkin ===
Scenario: Registering an already-registered shop name a second time exits zero without error
  Given "shopsystem-messaging" is already registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg registry add with canonical name "shopsystem-messaging" and the same connection information
  Then the command exits zero
  And shop-msg registry list contains exactly one entry for "shopsystem-messaging"

=== 03-registry-remove-deregisters-a-shop.gherkin ===
Scenario: Removing a registered shop by canonical name makes it no longer resolvable
  Given "shopsystem-messaging" is registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg registry remove with canonical name "shopsystem-messaging"
  Then the command exits zero
  And shop-msg registry list does not include an entry for "shopsystem-messaging"

=== 04-registry-remove-unknown-name-exits-zero.gherkin ===
Scenario: Removing a shop name that is not in the registry exits zero with a notice
  Given no shop named "shopsystem-unknown" is registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg registry remove with canonical name "shopsystem-unknown"
  Then the command exits zero
  And stdout or stderr contains a notice that "shopsystem-unknown" was not found

=== 05-registry-list-returns-all-registered-shops.gherkin ===
Scenario: Listing the registry returns one line per registered shop with consistent fields
  Given "shopsystem-messaging" and "shopsystem-product" are both registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg registry list
  Then the command exits zero
  And stdout contains one entry for "shopsystem-messaging"
  And stdout contains one entry for "shopsystem-product"
  And each entry appears on its own line with the canonical name present in a consistent field position

=== 06-registry-list-empty-registry-exits-zero.gherkin ===
Scenario: Listing an empty registry exits zero with no entries
  Given no shops are registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg registry list
  Then the command exits zero
  And stdout contains no canonical name entries

=== 07-registry-sync-adds-manifest-bcs-to-registry.gherkin ===
Scenario: Syncing from the BC manifest registers all BCs declared in the manifest
  Given the BC manifest declares "shopsystem-messaging" and "shopsystem-scenarios" as product BCs
  And neither "shopsystem-messaging" nor "shopsystem-scenarios" is registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg registry sync with the path to the BC manifest
  Then the command exits zero
  And shop-msg registry list includes an entry for "shopsystem-messaging"
  And shop-msg registry list includes an entry for "shopsystem-scenarios"

=== 08-registry-sync-removes-bcs-absent-from-manifest.gherkin ===
Scenario: Syncing from the BC manifest removes registry entries for BCs no longer in the manifest
  Given "shopsystem-old-bc" is registered in the messaging registry
  And the BC manifest does not declare "shopsystem-old-bc" as a product BC
  When I run shop-msg registry sync with the path to the BC manifest
  Then the command exits zero
  And shop-msg registry list does not include an entry for "shopsystem-old-bc"

=== 09-registry-sync-does-not-touch-lead-entry.gherkin ===
Scenario: Syncing from the BC manifest does not remove or modify the lead shop's registry entry
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered in the messaging registry as the lead shop
  And the BC manifest does not declare "shopsystem-product" as a product BC
  When I run shop-msg registry sync with the path to the BC manifest
  Then the command exits zero
  And shop-msg registry list still includes an entry for "shopsystem-product"

=== 10-registry-sync-is-idempotent.gherkin ===
Scenario: Running registry sync twice on an already-synced state produces no changes and exits zero both times
  Given the BC manifest declares "shopsystem-messaging" as a product BC
  And "shopsystem-messaging" is already registered in the messaging registry with matching connection information
  When I run shop-msg registry sync with the path to the BC manifest a second time
  Then the command exits zero
  And shop-msg registry list contains exactly one entry for "shopsystem-messaging"

=== 11-send-by-name-routes-to-registered-bc.gherkin ===
Scenario: Sending a message using --bc <name> routes to the registered BC without a path flag
  Given "shopsystem-messaging" is registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg send assign_scenarios --bc shopsystem-messaging with a valid payload and work-id "reg-101"
  Then the command exits zero
  And shop-msg pending inbox --bc shopsystem-messaging includes work-id "reg-101"
  And no filesystem path flag was required to address the BC

=== 12-send-to-unregistered-name-exits-nonzero.gherkin ===
Scenario: Sending a message to a name not in the registry exits non-zero with a clear error
  Given no shop named "shopsystem-unknown" is registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg send assign_scenarios --bc shopsystem-unknown with a valid payload
  Then the command exits non-zero
  And stderr contains a message indicating "shopsystem-unknown" could not be resolved in the registry

=== 13-bc-root-flag-removed-exits-nonzero.gherkin ===
Scenario: Using the removed --bc-root flag exits non-zero with a migration error message
  Given the shop-msg CLI has shipped name-based addressing
  When I run any shop-msg subcommand with a --bc-root flag
  Then the command exits non-zero
  And stderr contains a message indicating --bc-root is no longer supported and instructs the caller to use --bc <name>

=== 14-lead-root-flag-removed-exits-nonzero.gherkin ===
Scenario: Using the removed --lead-root flag exits non-zero with a migration error message
  Given the shop-msg CLI has shipped name-based addressing
  When I run any shop-msg subcommand with a --lead-root flag
  Then the command exits non-zero
  And stderr contains a message indicating --lead-root is no longer supported and instructs the caller to use --lead <name>

=== 15-lead-has-registry-entry-after-bootstrap.gherkin ===
Scenario: The lead shop appears in the registry after bootstrap registry add
  Given no shop named "shopsystem-product" is registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg registry add with canonical name "shopsystem-product" and its connection information
  Then the command exits zero
  And shop-msg registry list includes an entry for "shopsystem-product"

=== 16-read-inbox-by-lead-name-returns-message.gherkin ===
Scenario: Reading the lead inbox by canonical name returns a message sent to the lead
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered in the messaging registry as the lead shop
  And a message addressed to "shopsystem-product" with work-id "lead-201" is present in the lead inbox
  When I run shop-msg read inbox --lead shopsystem-product with work-id "lead-201"
  Then the command exits zero
  And stdout includes message_type and work_id "lead-201"
  And stdout includes the message body
  And no filesystem path flag was required to address the lead inbox

=== 17-pending-inbox-lead-lists-unconsumed-messages.gherkin ===
Scenario: Listing pending messages in the lead inbox returns only unconsumed messages addressed to the lead
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered in the messaging registry as the lead shop
  And two messages addressed to "shopsystem-product" are present in the lead inbox with work-ids "lead-202" and "lead-203"
  And work-id "lead-202" has been consumed
  When I run shop-msg pending inbox --lead shopsystem-product
  Then the command exits zero
  And stdout includes work-id "lead-203"
  And stdout does not include work-id "lead-202"

=== 18-consume-inbox-removes-message-from-lead-pending.gherkin ===
Scenario: Consuming a lead inbox message removes it from the pending list
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered in the messaging registry as the lead shop
  And a message addressed to "shopsystem-product" with work-id "lead-204" is present in the lead inbox
  When I run shop-msg consume inbox --lead shopsystem-product with work-id "lead-204"
  Then the command exits zero
  And shop-msg pending inbox --lead shopsystem-product does not include work-id "lead-204"

=== 19-bc-respond-sends-to-lead-inbox.gherkin ===
Scenario: A BC responding to work sends the response to the lead inbox, not to a BC outbox
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered in the messaging registry as the lead shop
  And "shopsystem-messaging" is registered in the messaging registry
  And a work-done response for work-id "lead-301" is ready from "shopsystem-messaging"
  When shop-msg respond work_done is run by "shopsystem-messaging" for work-id "lead-301"
  Then the command exits zero
  And shop-msg pending inbox --lead shopsystem-product includes work-id "lead-301"

=== 20-bc-response-in-lead-inbox-not-in-bc-outbox.gherkin ===
Scenario: A BC response appears in the lead inbox and does not appear in the BC outbox
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered as the lead shop
  And "shopsystem-messaging" is registered in the messaging registry
  And shop-msg respond work_done was run by "shopsystem-messaging" for work-id "lead-302"
  When I run shop-msg pending inbox --lead shopsystem-product
  Then stdout includes work-id "lead-302"
  And shop-msg pending outbox --bc shopsystem-messaging does not include work-id "lead-302"

=== 21-sent-message-carries-from-and-to-fields.gherkin ===
Scenario: A message sent via shop-msg carries populated from and to fields in the stored payload
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered as the lead shop
  And "shopsystem-messaging" is registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg send assign_scenarios --bc shopsystem-messaging with work-id "reg-401" from "shopsystem-product"
  And I run shop-msg read inbox --bc shopsystem-messaging with work-id "reg-401"
  Then the command exits zero
  And stdout includes a "from" field containing "shopsystem-product"
  And stdout includes a "to" field containing "shopsystem-messaging"

=== 22-bc-response-carries-from-and-to-fields.gherkin ===
Scenario: A BC response carries from and to fields identifying the BC sender and the lead recipient
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered as the lead shop
  And "shopsystem-messaging" is registered in the messaging registry
  And shop-msg respond work_done was run by "shopsystem-messaging" for work-id "lead-402"
  When I run shop-msg read inbox --lead shopsystem-product with work-id "lead-402"
  Then the command exits zero
  And stdout includes a "from" field containing "shopsystem-messaging"
  And stdout includes a "to" field containing "shopsystem-product"

=== 23-send-without-registered-sender-exits-nonzero.gherkin ===
Scenario: Sending a message when the caller is not registered in the registry exits non-zero
  Given no shop is registered in the messaging registry for the current caller context
  And "shopsystem-messaging" is registered in the messaging registry
  When I run shop-msg send assign_scenarios --bc shopsystem-messaging with a valid payload
  Then the command exits non-zero
  And stderr contains a message indicating the sender could not be determined from the registry

=== 24-watch-by-lead-name-fires-on-new-inbox-message.gherkin ===
Scenario: shop-msg watch --lead <name> outputs one line to stdout when a new message arrives addressed to the lead
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered as the lead shop
  And shop-msg watch --lead shopsystem-product is running in the background and has completed its startup drain
  When a shop-msg respond work_done message with work-id "lead-501" is sent to the lead inbox
  Then shop-msg watch --lead shopsystem-product outputs exactly one line to stdout for work_id "lead-501"
  And no additional output line arrives within 2 seconds

=== 25-watch-by-lead-name-startup-drain.gherkin ===
Scenario: shop-msg watch --lead <name> drains messages already in the lead inbox before entering live-notification mode
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered as the lead shop
  And a message with work-id "lead-502" is already present in the lead inbox before watch starts
  When shop-msg watch --lead shopsystem-product is started
  Then it outputs a line for work_id "lead-502" during the startup drain
  And after the drain completes it enters live-notification mode without exiting

=== 26-watch-by-lead-name-no-exit-on-quiet-inbox.gherkin ===
Scenario: shop-msg watch --lead <name> does not exit when no new messages arrive after startup drain
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered as the lead shop
  And the lead inbox is empty
  And shop-msg watch --lead shopsystem-product is running in the background and has completed its startup drain
  When 5 seconds pass with no new messages arriving in the lead inbox
  Then shop-msg watch --lead shopsystem-product is still running
  And it has produced no output since completing the startup drain

=== 27-watch-by-lead-name-db-unreachable-exits-nonzero.gherkin ===
Scenario: shop-msg watch --lead <name> exits non-zero immediately when the database is unreachable
  Given the messaging database is not reachable
  When I run shop-msg watch --lead shopsystem-product
  Then the command exits non-zero
  And stderr contains a message indicating the database could not be reached

=== 28-watch-does-not-fire-for-message-addressed-to-other-shop.gherkin ===
Scenario: shop-msg watch --lead <name> does not fire when a message arrives addressed to a different shop
  Given "shopsystem-product" is registered as the lead shop
  And "shopsystem-messaging" is registered in the messaging registry
  And shop-msg watch --lead shopsystem-product is running in the background and has completed its startup drain
  When a message addressed to "shopsystem-messaging" with work-id "bc-601" arrives in "shopsystem-messaging"'s inbox
  Then shop-msg watch --lead shopsystem-product produces no output within 2 seconds
```

```text
Exit code 2
usage: shop-msg [-h] {respond,send,read,pending,consume,prime,dump,watch} ...

positional arguments:
  {respond,send,read,pending,consume,prime,dump,watch}
    respond             write a BC response message
    send                write a lead-to-BC message into a BC's inbox
    read                read a message from a BC's mailboxes
    pending             enumerate pending mailbox entries (queries, not gates)
    consume             mark a mailbox entry as consumed (lead side)
    prime               session-start orientation: DSN, DB health, pending
                        inbox, and CLI reminder
    dump                operator debugging: dump messages table rows to stdout
                        as YAML
    watch               Monitor-compatible watcher: drain pending messages
                        then LISTEN for new ones, printing one '<work_id>
                        <message_type>' line per event to stdout. Never exits
                        unless the DB is unreachable. Use --bc-root for inbox
                        watching (BC mode) or --lead-root for outbox watching
                        across all BCs (lead mode).

options:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
---
usage: shop-msg send [-h]
                     {request_maintenance,assign_scenarios,request_bugfix} ...

positional arguments:
  {request_maintenance,assign_scenarios,request_bugfix}
    request_maintenance
                        write a request_maintenance message
    assign_scenarios    write an assign_scenarios message
    request_bugfix      write a request_bugfix message

options:

                        title used in the wrapping `Feature:` line for each
                        scenario

options:

usage: shop-msg pending [-h] {inbox,outbox} ...

positional arguments:

    inbox         list inbox messages with no matching outbox response (BC

    outbox        list pending outbox responses across sibling BC clones (lead
                  side)

options:

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./repos/shopsystem-messaging/src/shop_msg/storage.py`

```text
1	"""Postgres-backed storage for the shop-msg mailbox protocol.
2	
3	All inter-shop messages (inbox from lead, outbox from BC) are stored in
4	a single 'messages' table. The `bc` column namespaces each row to a
5	specific Bounded Context root (its filesystem path, which is unique per
6	test invocation and per real BC deployment).
7	
8	Configuration — connection DSN:
9	  The DSN is read from the environment variable SHOPMSG_DSN. If that
10	  variable is not set, a hardcoded default is used (suitable for
11	  development and the BDD test suite). In production, operators set
12	  SHOPMSG_DSN to point at the real Postgres instance.
13	
14	  Default DSN: postgresql://postgres:postgres@postgres:5432/shopsystem
15	
16	Schema (DDL emitted once per connect):
17	  CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS messages (
18	    id          BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
19	    bc          TEXT NOT NULL,
20	    work_id     TEXT NOT NULL,
21	    direction   TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (direction IN ('inbox','outbox')),
22	    message_type TEXT NOT NULL,
23	    payload     JSONB NOT NULL,
24	    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
25	    UNIQUE (bc, work_id, direction, message_type)
26	  );
27	
28	Collision handling:
29	  INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING returns 0 rows affected. The
30	  callers check this and raise CollisionError, which the CLI surfaces
31	  as a non-zero exit.
32	
33	LISTEN/NOTIFY:
34	  After every inbox INSERT, NOTIFY is fired on the channel
35	  `inbox_<bc_slug>` (with the bc path slug-encoded) carrying the
36	  work_id as the payload. After every outbox INSERT (from the respond
37	  commands), NOTIFY is fired on the channel `outbox_<bc_slug>` carrying
38	  the work_id as the payload. These are fire-and-forget from the storage
39	  layer's perspective; agents hold long-lived LISTEN connections
40	  separately.
41	"""
42	from __future__ import annotations
43	
44	import json
45	import os
46	import re
47	from typing import Any
48	
49	import psycopg
50	from psycopg.rows import dict_row
51	
52	
53	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
54	# DSN / connection
55	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
56	
57	_DEFAULT_DSN = "postgresql://postgres:postgres@postgres:5432/shopsystem"
58	
59	
60	def _get_dsn() -> str:
61	    """Return the DSN from SHOPMSG_DSN env var or the default."""
62	    return os.environ.get("SHOPMSG_DSN", _DEFAULT_DSN)
63	
64	
65	def _connect() -> psycopg.Connection:
66	    """Open a new connection and ensure the schema exists.
67	
68	    Raises a clear, descriptive error if the DSN is unreachable so the
69	    operator knows which endpoint to check.
70	    """
71	    dsn = _get_dsn()
72	    try:
73	        conn = psycopg.connect(dsn, row_factory=dict_row)
74	    except psycopg.OperationalError as exc:
75	        raise RuntimeError(
76	            f"shop-msg: cannot connect to Postgres at DSN {dsn!r}.\n"
77	            f"Check that the service is running (e.g. 'docker compose up -d').\n"
78	            f"Original error: {exc}"
79	        ) from exc
80	    _ensure_schema(conn)
81	    return conn
82	
83	
84	_DDL = """
85	CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS messages (
86	  id           BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
87	  bc           TEXT NOT NULL,
88	  work_id      TEXT NOT NULL,
89	  direction    TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (direction IN ('inbox','outbox')),
90	  message_type TEXT NOT NULL,
91	  payload      JSONB NOT NULL,
92	  created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
93	  UNIQUE (bc, work_id, direction, message_type)
94	);
95	"""
96	
97	_DDL_CONSUMED_COL = """
98	ALTER TABLE messages
99	  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS consumed BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE;
100	"""
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Read PDR-007

`cat /workspaces/shopsystem-product/pdr/007-path-to-name-addressing-migration.md 2>&1`

```text
# PDR-007 — Path-to-name addressing migration

**Status:** decided (2026-05-20)
**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
**Anchored to:** brief 006 (messaging name registry and lead inbox);
stakeholder intent 2026-05-20:
*"Shops are addressed by canonical name, not by filesystem path."*

## The question

How should the transition from `--bc-root`/`--lead-root` path-based
addressing to `--bc`/`--lead` name-based addressing be handled?

The `--bc-root` and `--lead-root` flags appear throughout:
- The lead shop CLAUDE.md (session start instructions, `shop-msg watch`
  invocation, CLI examples)
- The lead-architect.md agent template (BC pre-state verification,
  `shop-msg send`, `shop-msg read outbox` calls)
- The lead-po.md agent template (clarify-response CLI mechanics:
  `shop-msg read outbox --bc-root`, `shop-msg respond clarify
  --bc-root`)
- The BC primer (inbox/outbox read instructions, `shop-msg respond`
  examples)
- Any operational runbooks or scripts that invoke `shop-msg` with
  path flags

The scope of callers is bounded and known. All of them live in
repositories the lead shop controls directly (the lead shop repo itself
and `shopsystem-templates`, which owns the primer and agent templates).

## Options considered

### Option A — Clean break

Remove `--bc-root` and `--lead-root` immediately when name-based
addressing ships. All callers update atomically in the same commit or
PR that ships the new flags. The old flags produce a non-zero exit and
a clear error: `"--bc-root is no longer supported; use --bc <name>"`.

**Pros:**
- Simplest final state: one addressing model, no conditional logic in
  `shop-msg`.
- No risk of callers silently continuing to use the deprecated path.
- Eliminates the two-resolution-paths complexity in the implementation.
- Forces all callers to be found and updated at ship time, preventing
  drift.

**Cons:**
- If a caller is missed, it fails with a hard error after the cut-over.
  Missing callers must be found and fixed before or immediately after
  shipping.
- Requires all call sites to be updated in one coordinated move. For
  the known callers (CLAUDE.md, two agent templates, BC primer), this
  is a manageable number of files.

### Option B — Deprecation period

Keep `--bc-root` and `--lead-root` working alongside `--bc` and
`--lead` for one release cycle. Callers continue working without changes;
they migrate at their own pace. After the deprecation period, remove.

**Pros:**
- Safer migration: no caller breaks at the moment the new flags ship.
- Enables testing the new flags before all callers are updated.

**Cons:**
- `shop-msg` must maintain two resolution paths (path-based and
  name-based) simultaneously. This increases implementation complexity
  and creates test surface for behavior that is intended to be temporary.
- Callers that are never updated continue using path-based addressing
  indefinitely — the deprecation period may stretch because there is no
  hard forcing function.
- The caller list is small and known; the "callers migrate at their own
  pace" benefit is less compelling when the callers can all be updated
  in a single coordinated dispatch.
- Path-based resolution still works, meaning the system is not fully
  container-safe during the deprecation window. Brief 004's containerized
  scenario is not testable until the callers are migrated anyway.

### Option C — Alias (path-or-name detection)

`--bc-root` becomes an alias for a unified `--bc` flag that accepts
either a filesystem path or a canonical name. `shop-msg` heuristically
determines which was passed (a name if it matches a registry entry, a
path if it looks like a filesystem path).

**Pros:**
- Zero migration burden for existing callers.
- Backward compatible indefinitely.

**Cons:**
- Introduces ambiguity: a canonical name that could also look like a
  relative path (`shopsystem-messaging` is safe, but a name like `foo`
  could be a directory). The heuristic requires special-casing.
- Preserves path-based addressing permanently (or until a future PDR
  removes it), contradicting the invariant that shops are addressed by
  name.
- The "alias" approach signals to future contributors that path-based
  addressing is still acceptable, which undermines the registry as the
  resolution layer.

## Decision

**Option A — Clean break.**

Rationale:

1. **The caller list is bounded and known.** The files that use
   `--bc-root` and `--lead-root` are: the lead shop CLAUDE.md,
   lead-architect.md, lead-po.md, the BC primer (in
   `shopsystem-templates`), and any runbooks. This is a small,
   enumerable set. A clean break is feasible because there is no unknown
   long tail of callers; every caller can be audited and updated as part
   of the migration dispatch.

2. **Two resolution paths add implementation complexity for zero
   behavioral benefit.** If the goal is name-based addressing, the
   deprecation period delivers path-based addressing that still works —
   which means the containerization story (brief 004) is incomplete until
   callers migrate anyway. The clean break forces the completion of the
   migration, and the complexity of two resolution paths is never
   introduced.

3. **The hard error is informative.** When a caller with an old flag
   hits the cut-over, it receives: `"--bc-root is no longer supported;
   use --bc <name>"`. This is a clear, actionable error that surfaces any
   missed caller immediately, rather than silently routing by path when
   the intent is name-based routing.

4. **The alias approach undermines the invariant.** Option C is
   attractive for its zero-migration-burden, but the invariant in
   brief 006 is "shops are addressed by canonical name, not by filesystem
   path." An alias that preserves path-based routing contradicts the
   invariant rather than enforcing it. The PO cannot recommend an option
   that makes the invariant a preference rather than a contract.

**Migration commitment:** The Architect's dispatch for brief 006 scope B
must include, as part of the same assign_scenarios dispatch:

1. A scenario that verifies `--bc-root` produces a clear non-zero error
   message after the cut-over.
2. A scenario that verifies `--lead-root` produces a clear non-zero error
   message after the cut-over.
3. An explicit task (in the dispatch description or a separate
   `request_maintenance`) to update: the lead shop CLAUDE.md, the two
   agent templates (lead-architect.md, lead-po.md), and the BC primer
   in `shopsystem-templates`. All four files must be updated before or
   at the moment the new CLI ships.

The lead-po.md's own CLI mechanics section (which currently references
`--bc-root`) is updated as part of this migration, as the PO's own
instructions must reflect the canonical interface.

## What this leaves open

1. **Exact error message wording.** The PO commits that a clear,
   non-zero-exit error is produced. Exact wording is a scenario-level
   detail.
2. **Whether `--bc-root` produces an error or is silently ignored.** The
   PO commits non-zero exit (the Architect must not choose silent
   ignore). The exact error mechanism (unrecognized flag vs. recognized-
   but-removed flag with custom message) is the Architect's call.
3. **Order within the brief 006 dispatch.** The Architect determines
   whether scope B (name-based flags) ships before or alongside scope A
   (registry commands). Scope A must exist for scope B to work; they may
   ship atomically or A before B.

## Cross-references

- [brief 006](../briefs/006-messaging-name-registry-and-lead-inbox.md)
  — the intent this PDR is decided against.
- [brief 004](../briefs/004-bc-container-isolation.md) — the
  containerization context that makes path-based addressing untenable.
- [PDR-006](006-bc-manifest-ownership.md) — parallel PDR for the manifest
  CLI ownership question; the manifest is the input to `registry sync`,
  which scope A of brief 006 depends on.
```
</details></tool-use>

130	    work_id: str

135	class Clarify(BaseModel):
136	    message_type: Literal["clarify"]
137	    # work_id is constrained to a safe identifier shape: alphanumerics and
138	    # hyphens only, length >= 1. This rejects path separators ("/", ".."),
139	    # whitespace, and empty strings in one rule, so callers (CLI and any
140	    # future tools) cannot weaponize the value via crafted arguments.
141	    work_id: str = Field(min_length=1, pattern=r"^[a-zA-Z0-9-]+$")
142	    question: str = Field(min_length=1)
143	
144	
145	class WorkDone(BaseModel):
146	    message_type: Literal["work_done"]
147	    work_id: str
148	    status: Literal["complete", "partial", "blocked"]
149	    summary: str | None = None
150	    scenario_hashes: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
151	
152	
153	class MechanismObservation(BaseModel):
154	    """BC -> lead observation about the shop-system mechanism itself.
155	
156	    Surfaced alongside `work_done` (or, less commonly, ambient outside any
157	    directed work) when the BC notices something load-bearing about
158	    templates, schemas, role discipline, package boundaries, or the
159	    spec — anything that is mechanism-of-the-system rather than a
160	    property of the work item itself.
161	
162	    Carve-outs per design (see `prototypes/mechanism-observation-v1/design.md`):
163	    - Property of the scenario / work item -> `clarify`
164	    - Implementation block -> `work_done(blocked)`
165	    - Mechanism-of-the-system -> `mechanism_observation`
166	
167	    Three-artifact pattern: when the BC keeps long-form analysis in its
168	    own work-tracker, it can reference that record via the optional
169	    `provenance_ref` field; the lead's drain may then create a
170	    corresponding lead-side record that references back. The wire
171	    message itself does not name any particular tracker — beads,
172	    GitHub Issues, or any other — so the catalog stays decoupled from
173	    the lead-side and BC-side choices of work registry (per brief 001
174	    item C / lead-231). Long-form analysis lives in the referenced
175	    record, not in this message.
176	    """
177	    message_type: Literal["mechanism_observation"]
178	    subject: str = Field(min_length=5, max_length=120)
179	    observed_during: str | None = None
180	    body: str = Field(min_length=50)
181	    evidence: list[Annotated[str, Field(min_length=1)]] | None = Field(default=None, min_length=1)
182	    proposed_action: str | None = None
183	    # provenance_ref is an optional, tracker-neutral pointer to a BC-side
184	    # record where long-form analysis lives. The catalog deliberately does
185	    # not constrain its shape to a beads issue-id pattern (lead-231 item
186	    # C: schemas must not assume beads participation). The constraint is
187	    # only path-safety — no slashes, no path separators — preserving the
188	    # same hardening Clarify.work_id (lead-008) and the prior bd_ref
189	    # field had, but without the beads-specific shape.
190	    provenance_ref: str | None = Field(
191	        default=None, min_length=1, pattern=r"^[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9_.-]*$"
192	    )
193	
194	
195	LeadMessage = Union[RequestMaintenance, AssignScenarios, RequestBugfix]
196	BCResponse = Union[Clarify, WorkDone, MechanismObservation]

```text
# Beads Workflow Context

> **Context Recovery**: Run `bd prime` after compaction, clear, or new session
> Hooks auto-call this in Claude Code when a beads workspace is resolved

# 🚨 SESSION CLOSE PROTOCOL 🚨

**CRITICAL**: Before saying "done" or "complete", you MUST run this checklist:

```
[ ] 1. git status              (check what changed)
[ ] 2. git add <files>         (stage code changes)
[ ] 3. bd dolt pull            (pull beads updates from main)
[ ] 4. git commit -m "..."     (commit code changes)
```

**Note:** This is an ephemeral branch (no upstream). Code is merged to main locally, not pushed.

## Core Rules
- **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
- **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking
- **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting
- **Memory**: Use `bd remember "insight"` for persistent knowledge across sessions. Do NOT use MEMORY.md files — they fragment across accounts. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`.
- Persistence you don't need beats lost context
- Git workflow: run `bd dolt pull` at session start
- Session management: check `bd ready` for available work

## Essential Commands

### Finding Work
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	import re
2	from typing import Annotated, Literal, Union
3	from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, model_validator
4	
5	# Delegate the canonical scenario-hash to the scenarios package. The
6	# rule's true home is `scenarios.hash.compute_scenario_hash`; we re-
7	# export it under the local name `_canonical_scenario_hash` because
8	# (a) ScenarioPayload's validator below still calls it under that
9	# name and (b) the cross-package agreement test in
10	# tests/integration/test_catalog_scenarios_agreement.py imports it
11	# from `catalog.schemas` and asserts it matches the `scenarios hash`
12	# CLI output. Keeping the export name stable lets that test continue
13	# to pin the catalog-side contract — what changes is that the
14	# implementation is now imported rather than duplicated.
15	#
16	# History: while messaging and scenarios shared a monorepo, this
17	# function was an inline duplicate of the canonicalization rule (five
18	# lines of normalization plus a sha256 truncation), to avoid catalog
19	# importing from scenarios in the same prototype directory. Per
20	# ADR-001, the BC-of-the-shopsystem layout puts the canonicalization
21	# rule in the scenarios package and lets messaging depend on it
22	# cleanly, so the duplicate is gone.
23	from scenarios.hash import compute_scenario_hash as _canonical_scenario_hash
24	
25	
26	# Matches an "@bc:<name>" token where <name> is one or more non-space
27	# characters. Used by ScenarioPayload to enforce that every scenario's
28	# gherkin body declares which BC owns the scenario, regardless of which
29	# tool constructed the payload (lead CLI, hand-rolled tests, future
30	# automation).
31	#
32	# The token form is anchored (^...$) because we apply it to whitespace-
33	# split tokens of a single line, not to the gherkin body as a whole.
34	# An earlier version used `re.compile(r"@bc:\S+").search(gherkin)` over
35	# the entire string, which accepted gherkin whose only @bc: occurrence
36	# was inside a step's quoted content (e.g. `Given the file mentions
37	# "@bc:fake" in passing`). The intent is "the gherkin has a tag-line
38	# containing @bc:<name>", so we walk lines, split on whitespace, and
39	# require at least one token to match this anchored pattern. That
40	# matches how pytest-bdd tag lines are actually shaped — a sequence of
41	# whitespace-separated `@tag` tokens — without permitting substring
42	# matches inside step bodies.
43	_BC_TAG_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"^@bc:\S+$")
44	
45	
46	def _gherkin_has_bc_tag_line(gherkin: str) -> bool:
47	    """True if some line in `gherkin` contains an @bc:<name> token.
48	
49	    A "token" here is what you get from `str.split()` on the line — a
50	    whitespace-bounded run of non-space characters. This rejects @bc:
51	    appearing inside a quoted step phrase like
52	    `Given the body contains "@bc:fake"` because the surrounding quote
53	    characters bind to the token, leaving `"@bc:fake"` rather than the
54	    bare `@bc:fake` we require.
55	    """
56	    for line in gherkin.splitlines():
57	        for token in line.split():
58	            if _BC_TAG_TOKEN_RE.match(token):
59	                return True
60	    return False
61	
62	
63	class RequestMaintenance(BaseModel):
64	    message_type: Literal["request_maintenance"]
65	    work_id: str
66	    description: str
67	    acceptance_criteria: list[str] | None = None
68	    file_hints: list[str] | None = None
69	
70	
71	class ScenarioPayload(BaseModel):
72	    hash: str
73	    tags: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
74	    gherkin: str
75	
76	    @model_validator(mode="after")
77	    def _gherkin_must_carry_bc_tag(self) -> "ScenarioPayload":
78	        # The @bc:<name> tag identifies which Bounded Context owns the
79	        # scenario. Previously enforced only by the lead-side CLI's
80	        # --bc-tag flag, which left a hand-constructed ScenarioPayload
81	        # free to skip it. Promoting the check to schema level means
82	        # every construction site (CLI, tests, future tools) gets the
83	        # same guarantee. The token must appear as a whitespace-bounded
84	        # tag on some line, not merely as a substring — see
85	        # `_gherkin_has_bc_tag_line` for why.
86	        if not _gherkin_has_bc_tag_line(self.gherkin):
87	            raise ValueError(
88	                "ScenarioPayload.gherkin must contain a line with a "
89	                "@bc:<name> tag (e.g. '@bc:shop-msg'); none was found."
90	            )
91	        return self
92	
93	    @model_validator(mode="after")
94	    def _hash_must_match_canonical_body_hash(self) -> "ScenarioPayload":
95	        # The hash field is load-bearing: the lead emits it in
96	        # `work_done.scenario_hashes`, the BC echoes it back, and the
97	        # lead reconciles. Previously the only guarantee that
98	        # `hash == canonical_hash(gherkin)` came from the lead-side
99	        # `shop-msg send` CLI's hash-computation step, which left a
100	        # hand-constructed or test-constructed ScenarioPayload free to
101	        # carry mismatched values. Promoting the check to schema level
102	        # means every construction site (CLI, hand-rolled tests, future
103	        # automation reading YAML) gets the same guarantee.
104	        #
105	        # The canonicalization rule is owned by the scenarios package;
106	        # `_canonical_scenario_hash` above is a re-export of
107	        # `scenarios.hash.compute_scenario_hash`. See the import-site
108	        # docstring at the top of this module for why the local name
109	        # is kept stable, and the agreement test under
110	        # tests/integration/test_catalog_scenarios_agreement.py for
111	        # the cross-package contract pin.
112	        expected = _canonical_scenario_hash(self.gherkin)
113	        if self.hash != expected:
114	            raise ValueError(
115	                "ScenarioPayload.hash does not match the canonical "
116	                f"scenario-hash of the gherkin body: hash={self.hash!r} "
117	                f"but canonical(gherkin)={expected!r}."
118	            )
119	        return self
120	
121	
122	class AssignScenarios(BaseModel):
123	    message_type: Literal["assign_scenarios"]
124	    work_id: str
125	    scenarios: list[ScenarioPayload]
126	
127	
128	class RequestBugfix(BaseModel):
129	    message_type: Literal["request_bugfix"]
130	    work_id: str
```
